['[WomansExponent | Vol4 | 1875 | November | Issue: 11 | Title: WOMAN'S WORK. | Author: BLANCHE BEECHWOOD]

	WOMAN'S WORK.

	BY BLANCHE BEECHWOOD.

	"Seek to be good, but aim not to be great;
	A woman's noblest station is retreat;
	Her fairest virtues fly from public sight,
	Domestic worth, that shuns too strong a light."
	The author of the above was a very great
man; a man of brilliant genius and exten-
sive knowledge; learned in science, and one
who held a distinguished name and rank;
but alas! he was exceedingly uninformed
in regard to women. One would judge
from his views here expressed, that woman
was incapable of having an opinion of her
own upon any subject.
	We who live in the Nineteenth century,
many of us, differ with this great poet,
writer and novelist, Lord, Member of Par-
liament, etc.; we hold other views on
the subject. I am very much afraid that
this author's style of woman would only be
a living specimen in very exceptional cases.
	There are thousands of women who never
have the opportunity of proving whether
they possess domestic worth; they have no
fireside to sit by, no table to preside over,
and no husband to provide them with the
shelter of a home.
	In marking out woman's course of con-
duct, he has failed to take a broad view of 
the matter; he was evidently thinking of
one woman only, and that one undoubtedly
his wife, or his servant; certainly she was
not his companion in literary work; etc., or
he would have formed a better conception
of womanly character.
	What did the great Creator bestow upon
woman such wonderful talents, gifts and
qualities for if not for use? Would He cre-
ate something for nothing? No; for He
has shown us clearly that all things are
created for a purpose, to fill a certain sphere
to perform a certain work, to complete a
plan which was marked out, and understood
by the great devisor.
	If woman cannot radiate beyond the do-
mestic circle, then thousands are created for
no purpose whatever.
 	But why philosophize like this? it is all a
sheer nonsense; women have not only a
work to do at home but abroad for all man-
kind. This work is more complicated than
that of man, for whereas men are only ex-
pected to fill one office or calling usually,
women are always expected, under all cir-
cumstances, to fill at least two, and perform
the real labor of a dozen. Man singles out
a profession, or a trade, he spends a speci-
fied amount of time in making himself ac-
quainted with his business; if he marries he
is just as competent to discharge the duties
of his profession; marriage does not interfere
with him in the least.
	How is it with woman? Vastly different,
I assure you. When she marries she takes
upon herself the cares of housekeeping and
all its accumulation of work; next comes
maternity and its increasing anxiety and re-
sponsibility. And yet she has capacity to
enjoy and endure even more, how many
men would manifest similar courage? Many
women, in addition to housekeeping, the
care of children and husband, have found
time to pursue, to some extent, other em-
ployments, and to improve and cultivate
their own minds.
	It would be a deplorable thing if women,
who married and were rearing families,
settled down to think this was the end of
their usefulness, that hereafter there were
only meals to get, little children to wash
and dress, sewing, knitting and all the

[Column 2]
minor details of home-life to be attended to.
In the mean time husband is keeping up
with the times, and everything new he
recognizes, and familiarizes himself with
the progress of the age. How much bene-
fit does the wife derive from his knowledge?
Very little. Occasionally he may give her
some information if it suits him, but in a
general way, if the wife asks her husband
for information he replies in monosyllables.
	What remains to be done then? inform
one's self. I know numberless instances
where a man has married a girl fresh from
school and society, or from some sphere
where she had been accustomed to see and
hear and understand what existed in the
world around her, and he has immediately
shut her out from all association except his
own, and perhaps some personal friends,
and if she had the newspaper to read and
now and then a magazine to look over,
seemed to think it all that could be expected
of him. If he took her sometimes to a
theatre or lecture it was a favor (or she
ought to consider it so.) If this contented
the man, or satisfied him, it would be a lit-
tle more commendable; but unfortunately
the husband is the one who is dissatisfied
and don't know what's the matter; his wife
ceases to be companionable, she is old fash-
ioned in her notions, she cannot talk inter-
estingly as she once could, her dress doesn't
please him, she is not nice enough, her hair
is not arranged according to his taste, her
manners are less polished, in fact, she is be-
hind the times; he can perceive that, but
thinks not he is at fault. He sees other
ladies who have not married so soon per-
haps, or who have not married so soon per-
haps, or who are not so completely swal-
lowed up in their husbands, that appear to
better advantage than his wife, and yet he
can not, for the life of him, understand what
is the reason.
	Women have quicker perception in these
nice points of distinction than men; they
readily discern slight, neglect, or want of
affection, and usually begin to grieve, de-
spond or murmur; neither of these will
ever raise them in their own estimation,
and one must have self-respect before they
can inspire it in another.
	The time has gone by when women may
sit idly down and wait for a lover or hus-
band. There is too much to be done, there
is no room for idlers; there is an essential
work that women can do; I do not believe
there are too many women in the world,
there is work for them all, if they will come
to the rescue; it is because so few are will-
ing to work, or understand what is expected
of them, that a few must do it all; but let
all be awakened to the wrongs that must
be righted, to the down-trodden and suffer-
ing ones, and the world will see that wo-
man's work is not simply to multiply and
replenish, but to renovate and restore; to
cultivate and develop the highest instincts
and aspirations, and to subdue and crush
out the evil tendencies of mortality.
	Let woman speak for herself, she has the
right of freedom of speech; let her voice
be raised against immorality and impurity;
let her thoughts and aspirations flow out in
the clear fountain of light; if she is herself
pure, her words will carry conviction with
them. God has not made woman in vain;
she has a place to fill, a work to do, and if
she seeks diligently unto the Lord He will
appoint her work. But women are too slow
in moving forward, afraid of criticisms, of
being called unwomanly, of being thought
masculine. What of it? If men are so
much superior to women, the nearer we
come up to the manly standard the higher
we elevate our sex.

[Column 3]
	Mothers, make yourselves companionable
to your boys; it is the best way to give
them exalted ideas and sentiments of wo-
men. Recently I heard a boy, in speaking
of women, declare he could not bear to see
a woman push herself forward, and place
herself in a position fit only for men. His
idea of a woman was to stay at home and
keep the house, etc. If she wanted to
study and improve her mind at home that
was all very well, but not to make a public
display of it.
	It may be well enough to have learning:
one never makes use of, but in my opinion
it is exactly similar to having clothes you
never wear, or a grand parlor you never
use. To keep one's mind bright and active
one must use their faculties; and a little
learning put to some practical use is far bet-
ter, in my opinion, than endless stores of
information left to moulder away in the
prison-house of the human mind. Gold
hoarded up may benefit posterity, but let
one be ever so learned and if they never
write, or talk, or teach, who is benefitted
by it? Who do they make happier or bet-
ter?
	Woman's work in this day and age is not
only an individual work, but a universal
work; a work for all her suffering sister-
hood; and it will want all the courage and
heroism it is possible to arouse in the rising
generation to stem the current of vice and
evil, and so purify the earth that the Mil-
lennium can be ushered in.
	Salt Lake City, Nov. 9, 1875. 
[The following is written sideways on the right margin of column 1]
E.B. Wells


', '[WomansExponent | Vol7 | 1878 | Unknown | Issue: 9 | Title: WOMEN AS EDUCATORS. | Author: Unknown]

WOMEN AS EDUCATORS.

	A great deal is said of the duties of mothers,
and in many instances much blame is attached
to them for the immorality, lack of honesty
and uprightness, and want of high moral tone
in society at the present time.  It is said that
mothers neglect their children for dress, to
follow fashion and outrival their associates,
that another class desire to take the place of
men, and are unfitting themselves for domestic
life and home education; and altogether that
"the times are out of joint," and as in the days
of Adam down to the present time it is wo-
man's fault.
	"What we want is better mothers," is heard
on every hand; "those who are contented in
their sphere, and not assuming more rights and
privileges than belong to them."  Are you so
sure they have got all their rights?  "Certainly;
haven't they got men to protect them, to work
for them, to nourish and cherish them?"  This
answer implies a great deal and covers a wide
area; but what are the facts?  Every one ac-
quainted with the existing state of society in
the world knows how far this is carried out in
the lives of men and women.  That there are
promises of this nature, solemn covenants too,
all are aware, but how frequently are they pro-
faned and broken until confidence is lost, and
the very structure upon which society is based
has become unreliable.  The relation of men
and women to each other is an ever underly-
ing principle of the great human problem,
which mankind are all engaged in solving.
	It has become quite a common practice with
people now a days to cry out against evils, from
the pulpit, the platform, and the press, to de-
nounce, to criticize and censure all without
offering any remedy that can be grasped by
the multitude - any potent healing balm for
the many grievances and errors that are con-
stantly being developed.
 When the imperative need of some
certain element is felt, the usual method is to
search for and apply it.  The world has become
aware that there is a deficiency in society now
that wants remedying, and they desire better
mothers.  The desire is good, and would no
doubt be of great benefit, but what are the con-
ditions of the sex at this time, what the facili-
ties conducive to a higher moral tone, and the
prospects for a change for the better. Do men
consider how much they themselves owe to
their mothers?  Do they know that she who
bore them went "down into the dark valley of
of the shadow of death" to endow man with life,
and in all tenderness she nursed, tended and
cherished her offspring through all the perils
incident to childhood, which imply all sorts of
self-denial, toil and patient endurance.  The
proudest man, in all his might, and strength,
and wisdom has the same peculiar history.
	Is it not indeed necessary that woman, upon
whom the bearing, rearing, and intellectually
developing the child rests, should be endowed
with all the advantages of education and con-
dition it is possible for her to enjoy?  Can wo-
man be too well qualified for the position of
mother? The highest and holiest relations of
life should certainly be worthy our strongest
efforts, and claim our best thoughts.
 It is customary to call woman a teacher, an
educator; - what the children imbibe in early
life strongly marks, or mars, their future;
mothers are expected to lay the very founda-
tion of virtues, on which the man, or woman,
bases the whole edifice of human character;
then ought not woman to be prepared, to be
qualified, to be surrounded with all the most
favorable conditions for such an education as
shall strengthen and endow her, physically,
mentally, morally and spiritually, for the high-
est phase of woman's life - Motherhood?
	But while woman strives to aid mankind,
may she not be privileged not only to dispense
good around her own private hearth-stone, but
to others extend a helping hand: reach out to
those who have not the same great courage,
the same understanding, whose conditions,
perchance, have not been so favorable to a
higher life, or who may have been misguided?
If even a little good can be done to others who
are hungering for it, or who stand in fearful
need, why should woman be debarred from
crossing her own threshold in her mission of
good works.  If there has been a great mistake
made, and society is to-day degenerated, why
should not woman, in connection with her
brother man, assist in regenerating?  Is it not
woman whose heart is agonized by the evils
with which the earth is filled?  Are not her
maternal instincts strong in regarding the suf-
fering of all humanity?  Does not her bosom
glow with a maternal love that is universal to
some extent, as well as individual!  Can man
redeem the world without woman's assistance?
He knows he cannot; he knows that in con-
nection with the strength and power of his
manhood, woman's gentler and kindlier ele-
ments are necessary to a high state of civiliza-
tion.  In countries where women are only sub-
ordinate and servile, what is the effect upon
society?  A constant tendency to barbarism.
Then, the more generally intelligent woman
is, the more influence she will be able to exert
through all possible avenues, towards improv-
ing social life; then so much deeper, and high-
er, and broader, and stronger will her system
of educating be.  If society is ever to be ele-
vated to a higher moral or spiritual condition,
then the power of woman must be sensibly
felt in its renovation.  That the world wants
better mothers there is no doubt, women who
regard sacredly the condition of motherhood,
women who make more thorough preparation
for duties than they do to dance, or sing, or at-
tract admiration, women who will choose their
husbands, for their sterling integrity, honesty
and uprightness.  The large majority of women
are not so trained and educated.  Our heart
goes out warmly to those women who have
much poverty and prejudice to overcome; they
need sympathy and teaching, and who can be
their educators, who  so well adapted as woman
herself, who, knowing all the longings and
needs of woman, all the suffering and pain in-
cident to maternity, knows how to enter into
the subject with a special inspiration.  Men
have no reason to fear the help of woman in
the great battle-field of life, for if she becomes
more self-helpful, more capable of standing
alone, she will be all the better qualified to
assist man in the many heavy burdens that
devolve upon him.
	Let us not forget to thank all noble men who
generously protect and defend women; and
also all brave, true-hearted women, who incur
ridicule, and opprobrium and frowns, who,
though wounded at every step they take, still
press forward in the path of conviction, and
seek to educate woman up to a higher standard
of intelligence.  Woman's part in the great
programme of creation is not only an essential
but a lofty one, and she should so consider it.
"To bear the souls of men" and women.  What
greater honor need she aspire to, except to
educate them in the path of duty.  And the
very sorrow through which woman passes in
fulfilling this great law of her being, should
make her a thousand times dearer to man, for
there is a sacredness about motherhood that
stands alone in its sublimity; and there is a
glory attending it, of which the weakest and
most unsophisticated of mothers possess a
timid sort of consciousness. 
							E. B. Wells


', '[WomansExponent | Vol20 | 1891 | October | Issue: 7b | Title: WOMAN. | Author: Unknown]

WOMAN.
---
	Woman is, or should be the equal of man. 
She would be if those rights and privileges,

[column 2]
political, social and otherwise, to which she is
justly and fairly entitled were not fraudulently
withheld from her.  Woman in herself is a
study, and as man at all times has misunder-
stood, and failed to understand or to recog-
nize her true merit, to comprehend of what
she is capable and competent of doing,it is time
woman herself undertook this stupendous and
Herculean task, and to man for the time be-
ing let her remain as a riddle unsolved. Let
her thoroughly examine, discuss and analyze
woman from every point of view.  She per-
ceives many evils at the present time for which
apparently there is no remedy.  Truth is per- 
verted, the Constitution of the United States,
which was founded and established under the
inspiration of Almighty God, spoken of and
treated as a dead letter.  Dead!  Who shall
redeem it?  Raise it from the dust under
which it is trodden? This is a question which
presents itself to the mind of thinking woman.
Is she a fit instrument for such a mighty work?
In what way could she, lone, unaided woman
effect the needful and welcome change. It
would take time, perhaps not in this genera-
tion could the grand consumnation be reached.
Has she patience, perseverance, fortitude and
all the necessary qualifications sufficient to
carry her through?  Let her read herself as
before stated, and become thoroughly ac-
quainted with woman; her ability, and re-
sources, before contemplating a work of such
vast magnitude, and for which she may be
utterly incapacitated.
	Woman in the beginning, when placed in
the garden of Eden, by her Creator to be a
helpmeet for man was in no way either physi-
cally or mentally the inferior of man. But
says man she transgressed the laws laid down
for them by their Maker, in partaking of the
forbidden fruit, she also tempted Adam to
partake of the same, thus being the first to
commit sin and to bring trouble into the world.
Very true, but the probability is if Adam had
tasted it first he would have kept the knowl-
edge to himself and have not offered to share
it with his companion.  Woman has always
since that time had the reputation of being of
an inquisitive nature.  And what is inquisi-
tiveness? but a thirst for knowledge, a desire
to find out something that will be of mutual
advantage to others, and the person immedi-
ately concerned. She wished to share that
which was good with her fellow creature
which is a credit to her, and certainly showed
her to be actuated solely by motives of a purely
unselfish and generous character.
	Woman is generally spoken of and univers-
ally acknowleged to be the weaker sex. 
That she is not mentally and intellectually is an un-
disputable fact, as the abundant literary pro-
ductions produced by women of the past and
present day go to show, some of them being
the most able historical works ever written,
requiring as much careful discrimination and
profound thought as could ever be conceived or
imagined in the brain and mind of her would
be superior, man.    As far as morals are con-
cerned, woman is decidedly in the advance.
One has only to read and observe that in all
criminal offences and of vice in all its various
forms and phases man practically takes the
lead.  It may be argued that woman is just
as corrupt only she does not happen to get
found out.  She denies that, but taking for
granted that it was so, it would go to show that
she possessed superior skill in planning and

[column 3]
devising, and a large amount of executive
ability, which if operated in the right direction
would produce great results.
	That women in civilized life is physically 
weaker than man is beyond a doubt, but that
she was ever created or designed to be so by
her Maker she fails to see; If there were a
difference it should be in her favor as she
has not only to nourish herself but others. 
That woman can endure prolonged labor pri-
vation of food and sleep, in most instances 
better than man has been particularly noticed
and referred to by physicians and others, and
there are living to-day quite as many aged 
and vigorous women who have been as active
and hardworking through life as men.
	The healthy woman is she who has some- 
thing to do, and who finds a reasonable amount
of enjoyment in so doing.   The weakly woman 
is either she who has very little else to do ex-
cept in helping others to make her pretty
things to wear, laces tight, compressing her 
body out of shape; eats everything which is
calculated to disagree with her, thus rendering
her dyspeptic, dances to excess, and outrages 
nature’s laws generally; and she who having 
to work twice as hard as a man, for a small
pittance barely sufficient to keep body and
soul together; or having a large family to take
care of and obtaining no sympathy from her
husband, is allowed to work unceasingly and
beyond her strength, thus destroying her
health, making room for disease, and at length
becomes a confirmed invalid.  This is woman's
fault to a great extent, and should be rectified
by her. 
It is a pretty idea, that of the sturdy oak
and clinging ivy, but is useless in the affairs of
practical and every day life.  Woman needs
something more than poetry, she finds she has
to face the stern realities of life just the same 
as man.  If she commits a crime she is 
punished as severely as man.  But if she per-
form a day’s work just as well, intelligently 
and conscientiously as man, she does not re-
ceive the same remuneration.  The lord of
creation has wrested her natural-born rights
from her under the pretence that she is not as 
competent in fulfilling a duty as man.  She is 
not considered capable of understanding or 
reasoning on such matters as politics and so
forth, and therefore deserves no voice in the 
same.  Man calls her his darling, his love and
his dear, which is all very pretty, but hardly
satisfying in these days when truth is rocked
to sleep, justice chloroformed and politicians 
will persist in viewing woman through green
spectacles.
Well, says man, what is it you require do
you want to sit in Congress, and all that sort
of thing?  No, woman craves no such thing
(although if she ever gets there it will be be-
cause she is fitted for that position.) She
simply asks for what ought to be voluntarily
extended to her, the right to vote, let her stay
at home, mind her own business, and attend 
to her household affairs.  Certainly, Sir, that 
is what she intends to do, but as one conscien-
tous gentleman remarked to a party of ladies,
“If a man cannot spare his wife for fifteen
minutes once in every six months to cast her
vote, he is not deserving of a wife at all."
Exactly, woman entirely coincides in that
opinion.  She is not likely to stop on the road 
to procure a drink of whiskey, neither could
her vote be bought for the same.  That
quarter of an hour would not be wasted


There would be no need of man losing his 
supper on that account, or the mending of
the children's stockings being postponed.  The
laws effect her just as much as they do him,
in fact they effect her more seriously.  Take 
for instance the condition of a widow at the 
decease of her husband,she can touch nothing,
she is not even the guardian of her own chil-
dren, without first requesting the favor of be-
ing allowed to become so.  But the tax card
drops in just the same.  Does the country
need better and more able politicians? De-
cidedly, men who will recognize and assign to 
woman that which belongs her.
*	*	*	*	*
	Let women improve the present time, the
moments are golden do not let them pass by
unheeded. Let woman educate herself, and 
cultivate her mental and intellectual faculties
to the fullest extent.  A healthy mind will
help to build up a healthy body, the two
working in harmony together will make of
woman what she ought to be.  Let her first
study herself, and having learned the alphabet
progress step by step, until she becomes versed
in all matters pertaining to the present and
future welfare of a great nation.  Then having
thoroughly learned the science of government
let her teach it to her sons and daughters, they 
in turn will impart to theirs.  By this means
the peerless and glorious constitution of
America will be preserved that a correct form
 of government be maintained.
	Let woman bestir herself and prove equal
to the emergency.  It is only when stern
necessity and matters requiring the greatest
expediency stare her in the face that she ever
renders herself conspicuous.  Man need not be 
afraid she will ever disgrace any privileges
which may be accorded to her, or usurp the
rights which properly belong to man.
	There is a grand reformation at hand, a great
future opening up before us.  The organiza-
tion of the Woman Suffrage Association was
an important step in the right direction.  The
men of Mormondom,(speaking of the majority)
are the only men on the face of the earth who
appreciate and estimate woman at her true
value.  The little stone which was cut out of the 
mountain without hands, has rolled forth, and 
will eventually fill the whole earth.  There is
something in the far distance which can only
be discerned by the eye of faith.  It is the
reformation not of a nation, but of the whole
world, the time spoken of in Holy Writ when
men shall beat their swords into ploughshares,
their spears into pruning hooks, when nation
shall not rise up against nation, neither shall
they learn war any more.  This is the grand
consummation of all things.  The opening up
of the Millenium in which one of the chief and
prime movers will be woman.
				Frances.
	Fountain Green, Sanpete Co.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
']

['[WomansExponent | Vol18 | 1889 | September | Issue: 7 | Title: LEAVES FROM MY JOURNAL. | Author: M. J. TANNER]

	﻿LEAVES FROM MY JOURNAL.
		BY M. J. TANNER.
			[Continued.]
	Monday June 15, 1885. Out again: How
I love to travel: It seems to infuse fresh vigor
in my life. Everything is new and novel, and
there is an excitement in it that is very fasci-
nating. The country is pleasant and the
scenery constantly changing. The houses we
pass are all built of frame and painted white.
The latest style is olive-green trimmed with
red. Here is one pea-green and a slate-colored
one. Many houses are poor looking and have
no paint. On, on we go, past farms and sta-
tions. Here the land is rocky. Large piles of
stones are scattered about where they have been
gathered from off the land, and grass and
weeds form a border around them. It is in-
teresting to see the people who get on the train
at different towns. Cousin Emily and I were
weighed while waiting at the depot. I weighed
117 lbs. Gained about 8 lbs., since leaving
home.
	Rochester, 10 a. m. More like the eastern
cities judging by the depot. Thickly settled,
high houses, covered depot, buildings all of 
brick. I am so busy taking notes that I do
not notice that every one has left the car un-
til a man tells me that I must change cars here.
I look around and see that I am the only per-
son left, gather up my things in haste and get
on another car as directed. I seat myself and
in looking over my belongings find that I have
left my parasol. It is too late to get it now for
the car is moving. I once more take out my
note book and pencil. 
	Near Lyons, pleasantly situated on a hill, is 
a large brick building which is pointed out to
me as an asylum for idiots. And here is Lyons. 
11 a. m., of which little can be seen as we run
[Column 2]
in between two rows of freight cars. The car
is full and I have as a seat mate a gentleman
who has traveled all night coming from
Chicago, he is sleeping and I have no one to
tell me about the country.
	Syracuse 12.15. Twenty minutes for dinner.
I have my lunch and eat it on the train as do
some others. I see by the sign that it is 130
miles to Schenectady. Syracuse is a large city
but there is little to be seen from the depot,
and one cannot tell much about people and
places on so slight an acquaintance. I can look
into the dining room and see the people eating.
Collared waiters are passing straw-berries.
Some ladies are walking on the platform
dressed in lovely costumes. As we start out
we pass through what appears to be Main
Street. On each side are very high brick
houses, and a continuous line of stores, mil-
linery shops, business houses and churches. 
Near the suburbs stands "Wards Temperance
Hotel.” It looks small and poorly patronized.
Out in the country, we pass large hop farms.
	Oneida 1.20 p. m. There is a beautiful lawn
around the depot and the word Oneida laid in
white cobble stones.
	Rome, 1.55. Here people crowd on the 
train, more than there are seats for. The
weather is hot. My neighbor gives his seat to
a lady.
	Utica, 2.20. Here several people leave the 
train. 3.15. We are traveling through Herkimer
Co., celebrated for its large dairies. Through
the valley of the Mohawk River. It is a 
beautiful country. Change cars at Schenectady.
getting a ticket to Caldwell where they tell me
I will have to stay all night and go down the
lake on a steamboat in the morning to Balton.
I meet a  pleasant young lady who is going to
Caldwell and tells me something about the
route and what hotel to stay at in Caldwell.
The face of the country changes. Ballstown
seems burried in hills and woods.
	Saritoga. Change cars, waiting about half
an hour. Here at the depot is the "United
States Hotel,” the largest house I think I ever
saw. The two sides which we see from the
platform reach a block each way. I walk down
the road to the corner and find that it extends
along the third side of the block. I am afraid
to take time to explore any farther. Many
people get off here for it is a famous resort for
the world of fashion and must, no doubt allure
many adventurers. I think I should not like
to stay here. We travel on and change cars
at Fort Edward where I part with my com-
panion. The country grows more hilly and
thicker wooded as we go on.
	Glens Falls—Caldwell.  Here is the end of
the road, for we are at the head of the beautiful
and far famed Lake George, and twilight is 
gathering around us. A steamboat stands at
the landing which connects with depot but it
will not go until morning. It is 8 o'clock and
I turn to go toward the cab stand after getting
the above information. I see a big, burly,
pleasant looking man in an old felt hat, look-
ing something after the fashion of the boys hats
at home, with the rim lapped down, holding a
whip in his hand. I take him for a cabby and
ask which cab will take me to the American
Hotel. He says he does not know, and I pass
 on. I wish I had asked him if he knows any-
one by the name of Putney. Still it would be
useless as they will not expect me until I reach
Balton where they will be sure to meet me to-
morrow. But Oh, how I do hate to stop at a
hotel among strangers. I feel so shrinking and
diffident away off here alone. If the boat had
only gone down tonight how nice it would
have been. However there is no time now for
regrets, I must hasten to secure a cab before
they are gone. It would not be pleasant walk-
ing through town in search of a hotel. I reach
the line of cabs and ask for the American 
Hotel. A cabby takes my satchel and seats


[Column 1]
﻿me in the last cab on the line. Several delays
occur and finally we are just starting out when
a voice from the outside asks if Mrs. Myron
Tanner is in here. I say that is my name and
ask if he has come for me. He replies that
he has and I get out of the cab thinking at
the time that the American Hotel has lost one
customer. He tells me to wait on the plat-
form while he brings the horse around. I recog-
nize in him the man I spoke to on the platform.
Presently he appears with a buckboard of
ancient and uncertain appearance drawn by a
horse which to ride after might lay one liable to
prosecution for cruelty to animals My heart
fails me for a moment and I feel that possibly
I am in a “delima.” However, I yield to the
force of circumstances and allow him to help
me into the vehicle and while we are being
whirled away he very politely informes me
that he is Caleb. I am glad to hear it and
kindly enquire what our relationship may be.
He says he is Caleb Putney son of my husband’s
Aunt Maria, and tells me that he has a brother
and two sisters. In the meantime darkness
is gathering around us and we have a ride of
ten miles before us. I enquire if the road is
good and receive an affirmative answer. There
is no moon and the sky is cloudy, as we enter
the woods and drive between rows of tall trees
it is sometimes so dark that we cannot see the
horse. We necessarily travel rather slowly,
and our long and somewhat lively conversation
is frequently interrupted by an apostraphy to
the horse. “Get up there you old lazy if you
don’t go faster I shall have to whip you dread-
ful hard.”
	I am asked many questions about Utah and
in return for information given am made ac-
quainted with the history of the family at home.
There had been a family council held about
coming to meet me. “Father” said I would
come up on the boat in the morning and they
would meet me at the landing. But he thought
I would not like to stay at a hotel among
strangers, which I assure him I was decidedly
averse to.
	The seat of our conveyance has scarcely any
back and he is very anxious lest I should go
to sleep and fall off, and what shall he do with
me if I fall and break my neck. Box me up
and send me back to Utah, I say, but he
would rather send me back alive, and so en-
joines me to be very careful not to fall, even
offering, if I think there is any danger, to hold
me on. Whenever there is a lull in the con-
versation he enquires if I am sleepy and en-
joines me not to fall off. I am certainly not
sleepy. The situation is so novel, the surround-
ings so strange and the air so bracing after the
hot day’s ride, and the conversation of my
companion so (what shall I say) exhilerating
that altogether I seem possessed of a spirit of
fun, and almost fancy myself a girl again.
	I learn that he is the only one of his fathers
children that has married though David and
Rebecka are both older than he. That he has
five children living and has burried one. That
his girls are dreadful good singers. That his
mother is a dreadful good woman. That his
horse is dreadful poor. Father would not let
his horse come because it was afraid of the
train. He has no horse, but a yoke of cattle
and the boy claims them. He also has some
sheep and the boy claims them. I see some
water and ask if that is Lake George. No
that is only “the pond.” He will take me to
Lake George in the morning. I hear a coarse
grum voice and ask “what is that?” Why!
that is a bull-frog. Did I never hear a bull-
frog before? I supposed I never had if that
was the kind of noise they made. Our frogs in
Utah made a different noise. They were a
small green frog. “Am I afraid?” “Oh no,
not of a bull-frog.” But I am beginning to
think the ride “dreadful” long, for I am
getting “dreadful” tired. He thinks it must
[Column 2]
be getting dreadful late and wonders if the girls
will be up. We get home at last, 11.15 p. m.
find the girls up and supper waiting. We sit
down to supper and he asks the blessing. The
first blessing I have heard since leaving home,
though my friends all profess religion. It gives
me a very comfortable homelike feeling. His
father gets up to welcome me and after supper
I am shown to a clean comfortable room and I
go to rest feeling that I am among friendly,
good people. Caleb’s home is a mile farther.
	Balton June 16,1885.—Get up late this
morning feeling weary after my long ride. I
am introduced to Caleb’s mother, who is a tall
slim elderly woman, very quiet and pleasant in
her ways. She gives me a kind welcome and
is glad to see one of her sister’s family. The
men are all gone to their work and after
breakfast Rebecka, or as she is called in the
family, Becky Ann goes down to the pond
with me. It is called the Trout Lake, and is
a pretty body of water two miles long and half
a mile wide surrounded with trees. Their home
is only a short distance from the bank but
owing to the thick growth of bushes we cannot
see it from the house. I think I should like to
see the frog that makes such a great noise but
he is rather shy and we cannot find one.  We
take a walk in the woods and pick winter-
green. The fragrant leaves of which are tender
and sweet, the berries are nearly gone but now
and then a red berry rewards our search.
	After dinner Caleb comes over and gets his
father’s horse and buggy and takes me to Lake
George. It is a pleasant ride through the
woods of about two and a half miles. The
country here is all hills, and woods, with here
and there a rock thrown in. The rock is black
granite and ranges from the size of a trunk to
the size of a house, or as the boy said “about
as big as a lump of chalk,” which may be quite
as definite. We call at the old homestead
that Father Tanner used to own, and where
my husband was born. The lady of the
house, Mrs. Vandenberg is very pleasant and
shows us through all the rooms and tells us the
alterations that have been made. The kitchen
and woodshed, (everybody here has a wood-
shed well filled with stove wood) has been
taken from the west side and built on the
south end, making the whole house face the
street which runs east of the house, between it
and the Lake. The portico is taken away and
a porch built the whole length, which makes a
lovely place to sit in the afternoon, with the
beautiful lake spread out before us and the
mountains, thickly covered with trees on the
opposite side. The house has been newly
weatherboarded and painted white. Since
Father Tanner left there with his family fifty
years ago, considerable of the land has been
sold in small lots and quite a village now
stands on what used to be known as the Tanner
Farm. A large hotel stands on the bank of
the lake a few rods east of the house, known
as the “Balton House,” and a steamboat land-
ing is made there. Mrs. V. takes me down to
the lake and shows me through the hotel.
From the observatory we have a fine view of
the surrounding country. She invites me to
spend a few days with her and visit various
points of interest. We go from there and call
on Morgan Tanner, a grandson of my hus-
band’s uncle, Pardon Tanner. He is of medium
size, dark complexion with keen black eyes,
about thirty years of age and a widower. I
tell him I am looking up family history and he
promises to help me all he can. From there
we go to Green Island. It once belonged to
the Tanner estate. It is now laid out in parks
and groves and a fine hotel, and some cottages
are built there as it is a fashionable summer
resort. It comprises sixty-five acres of land;
has a steamboat landing and is connected with
the mainland by a bridge. It is called the
Sagamore, and is certainly a lovely summer
[Column 3]
retreat. Little boats are kept there for rowing
on the lake, which is certainly a very pleasant
pastime. A steamboat comes puffing toward
the landing and our horse gets restive, so we
get in the buggy and start for home. She
trots nicely while she hears the engine and we
think it would be well to have a steamboat be-
hind us all the way. We stop at the post-
office to get some stamps and mail some letters.
Caleb introduces me to several of his friends
and they all have some joke to make about
Utah. It rains a little before we get home.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


', '[WomansExponent | Vol12 | 1883 | August | Issue: 5 | Title: MY OWN DEAR MOTHER: | Author: Unknown]

MY OWN DEAR MOTHER:
	I tried so hard to write last night, but was so
tired that I fell asleep when I was writing.
Belle and I left St. Louis on the railway train
after eight o'clock on the evening of the 23rd,
and arrived at Montrose, Iowa, at five a.m. We
had such a day yesterday, such tramping
around that at night when we did stop we were
perfectly worn out. I was delighted with Nau-
voo. It is really a beautiful place, but such
stillness, such death-like silence, I never
dreamed of; they call it "The deserted City,"
and it is well named. Its situation is more
picturesque than anything I ever saw, and it
has such a weird, sad attraction about it for me,
that my eyes constantly filled with tears in spite
of all my efforts to keep them back. It was a
cool gray morning that we arrived at Montrose,
and the river was very quiet. We crossed in a
boat, and waited on the other side until our fel-
low traveler could go and find Mr. Smith and
his spring wagon to take us up to the hotel; he
soon came back with the old gentlemen and his
wagon. We rode up quite slow, so very slow,
that often I had to watch a stone or some object
on the ground to tell whether we were really
moving or not. However we did not mind, for
we had a splendid chance to look around at the
green fields, vineyards and beautiful hedges,
and the old tumbled down houses. Mr. Smith
pointed out to us several places where people
had lived whom we knew very well; it was very 
affecting. I thought of your sufferings, how 
perhaps you had gone through these same
streets, and of all the sorrow and trouble you
had known right there when you were so young.
It seemed like a dream that I, your youngest
daughter, should be so blessed as to have the
privilege of seeing all these same places. We
arrived at the German Hotel, called the Temple
House, about six o'clock Tuesday morning, July
24th; every one around us was German, but of
course they could all speak a little English.
	The house faced the north, and we had a nice,
large, quiet room up stairs with a porch in front,
and from it we could see where the old Temple
used to stand; we were exactly one block east of
it across the street. We had breakfast and be-
fore we went up to our room again, went up to
see what was left of the Temple, but where it
once stood is a nice, new, frame house, and the
store on the south west corner of the block is
built entirely of the Temple rock; it is as 
smooth and white as ever, and they say it does

[Column 2]
not wear away one particle. This store is used
for a drug store and post office, and is very
large, 100 ft. by 30. Ever so many foundations
and houses around there seemed to be made of 
this same rock, and several here have carriage
steps made of it, how we envied them; from
there we went one block east, and saw a very
little square house, built of the same rock, we
went in among the trees, it was a perfect grove,
and found that this was the city park, and the
little house was used as a sort of store house.
A man who was working in the park told us
that Mrs. Clark, who lived on the hill, could tell
us who had lived in all the old houses, but he
knew nothing, so we left him and started for
Mrs. Clark's; we went in several old houses to
inquire the way there.  * * The houses
are almost all occupied by Dutch people; we at
last found Mrs. Clark and her two widowed
daughters; they were very pleasant, and so kind
to us. We were a long time deciding whether
to go in or not, but we thought if we were not
courageous enough for that we would not find
out anything so (we braced up) so to speak, and
went in, and as I have already said, they were
very kind, and insisted on our stopping to dinner;
she went out with us and showed us where the
rest of the Temple rock was that had been
bought by Mr. Ritter, a very rich old man; one
of the daughters went over with us, and we saw
all there was to see, hunted round for a piece
with carving on, but only found one of those
immense heads, and it was perfect; the stone
was scattered over the whole block, and he has
sold a great deal; it hardly seemed possible there
could have been so much in the building. One
large piece with carving on, he sold to a man
from Chicago for $300, and he would not sell
that head for less; he gave us some small speci-
mens. We had dinner, and then, accompanied
by the two girls, walked down through the
beautiful fields, and passed the hedges (I never
saw anything so pretty as the hedges) to hunt
up some places, first we called on a Mrs. Ravel,
not knowing what she was. They told us she
knew so much, and she did, but much it was,
too many lies, a perfect hornet's nest, and we got
out as soon as possible; from there we came first
to Uncle Joseph Young's house, and it is a 
beautiful place! The largest evergreens I ever
saw, a fountain and a great many flowers; the
house, they said, was almost the same; two
story brick, with frame top, with little windows
and green shutters. From there we saw Brig-
ham Young's old house, story and a half brick—
west wing looks old, but there are some people
living there. We next went to the most interest-
ing place, the old Nauvoo Mansion. Mrs. Hudson
lives there. She took us through the house.
We first went in the parlor, with its large,
black mantle-piece; next the long dining-room,
where they told us they brought Joseph after he
was killed. * * *
*	It was full of old things, some china,
and everything was old all over. * * *
From there went to the kitchen where the old
well was, and had a drink out of the same old
well. They told us there had been no change
made in any part of the house. The water was
refreshing. * * We saw at the right
hand side of the kitchen the room that Mother
Smith had occupied; everything looked very old
and worn out. She took us up stairs into the
Prophet Joseph's room and into the closet where,
it is said, he used to hide, but she could not open
the secret door that leads up on the roof; she
told us of it, and said her son knew how to do it,
but we saw no door; we went in all the rooms,
and saw the same old stairs. Oh! how much we
thought of you, and wished you with us, but we
were afraid it would be too much for you. From
there we went to the Riverside Mansion, once
the "Nauvoo House," that was just started


[Column 1]
when you left. Some of it has been taken
down, and the main building is finished, or part-
ly so; Bydeman, once Emma Smith's husband,
and his wife live in it; its walls made me think 
of the Council House walls, they were so thick;
the first story is built of white stone just like the
Temple, and the walls of the first floor of one of
the wings of the same stone is still standing, it
alone is 123 ft. long, so you can imagine how
long they intended it to be. The other stories
are of brick, and all speak of how remarkable,
it is, that they should have stood so long. The
staircase is of walnut. The river is just a few
steps from the west door; the oak trees are still
standing; trumpet vines and the flowers have
climbed to the top, and looks beautiful. We 
bought a picture of Joseph Smith, and saw pic-
tures of the whole family. In their parlor was
the old secretary owned by Hyrum Smith, and
it is elegant, but very old fashioned; an old
checker table that Joseph owned, also a bureau
that was very handsome, and an old center
table elegantly carved. We then went across 
the street to Joseph's old home and drank out of
that old well too, and saw the grave-yard. The
house is just the same old house. *
*		We saw where they said he and his
brother lay; the grave is between two locust
trees; we also saw the little fruit frame
that stood over the grave for years; next we 
saw his old house, it was well preserved, not
used for anything. From there we went to
Aaron Johnson Esq's. old house. The old print-
ing office was moved away up the river, but we
saw the house, and went all through it. they
took the old fire-place away last winter, but the
rest is much the same; the sweetest young
married woman lived there; she was so kind,
and felt so sorry for us because we cried. She
had a sweet little baby about as large as Belle's,
with blue eyes. I cannot express my feelings
here, I was completely broken down, and could
think of nothing but you, my own sweet mother,
and of your sorrow. Next we went to H. C.
Kimball's, and it is a grand looking house; right
in the center of the old house is a white rock
about two yards in length with H. C. K, 1845, 
engraved upon it. There are some Germans liv-
ing in the house; they were very kind to us and 
took us all through the house; we went in the
large room * *	and the two bedrooms;
up stairs in all the rooms, and into the third
story, and up on to the observatory; there is the
most beautiful view from there I ever saw. I
thought of Aunt Helen, and I don't blame her
for speaking so often of her home, for it is beau-
tiful. I brought her a piece of a large evergreen.
They have built a wing on and the whole
place looks lovely; there is also a large block of
redstone on the east side of the house with the
same ingraving on. Next we went to where
Belle's father, Bishop Whitney, lived; the house
is all gone, and the prettiest place in Nauvoo
stands on the ground where his home stood; the
old back steps were there still. Then we went
to what used to be the old graveyard; some of 
the old graves are still left in the people's lots,
and others are moved to the new cemetery.
From there we went back to the hotel. Mr
Smith, the old man that took us around, told us
that all Joseph's prophecies had come true but
one, and that was about the "Mormons" coming
back. He said Joseph said it should become a
vineyard, and the Mormons should drink the
wine, and at that time there were no grapevines
there. Now it is indeed a beautiful vineyard;
miles and miles of grapevines and nothing else,
and we had some of the wine in St. Louis. He
also said it should be deserted, and should be as
a dead city; and oh, that is true! There is some-
thing about the death-like stillness that you
cannot express. I forgot to tell you that we went 

[Column 2]
out to Joseph Smith's farm and drank out of 
the very old well. Oh, such a day, I never ex-
pect to see anything like it again; such feelings,
ma; they are beyond all description. Every step
I took I wondered if you had ever been there,
and oh, how I did long to speak to you, and ask
you questions! It seemed as if you must be
there, and I would turn around to look. I never
had such a strange feeling. I could not find
father's house, but saw the Masonic Hall, where
they told me he once had his office, during some
trial, but there was an old gentleman, a Mr.
Morrell, that called on me last night, and was
to call this morning, that could have told me
everything, but we came away so early that I
could not see him again. We had to come on
this steamer for there was no telling when
there would be another, and we feel anxious to
get on our way esst. I should love to see you
all. There is never one moment that you are
not in our thoughts. I wanted so much to say
my letter was from Nauvoo, but it was so near,
just as we left, that I guess I might say so. * *
I do not forget any of you in my prayers, but
constantly pray for the health and safety of you
all. Give our love to all the folks and to all the
children. I shall try and write up my first
steam-boat ride for you. We are going from St.
Louis to Chicago, and then I expect to go direct
to Massachusetts. Good night, dear heart.
	Lovingly,
	LOUIS WELLS.


38	WOMAN'S EXPONENT.

			AWAY FROM HOME.

	By courtesy of Brother Geo. H. Taylor,
supt. of 14th Wd. Sunday School, we are
enabled to publish the following interesting
epistle from Elder Samuel P. Richards.

	I well remember with what feelings it
was that I arose before the school to bid
good bye to my mates, and the school with
which I had been connected ever since its
first organization in the ward; realizing at
the time, that I was to leave probably for
years, and possibly for ever, to go forth and
labor in the vineyard of the Lord, in a for-
eign land and among a people speaking a
language foreign to my own, and to pro-
claim to them the truths of the everlasting
Gospel, trusting in God alone for assist-
ance.
	My associations with the Sunday School
were always pleasant and many are the
good principles and ideas I there learned,
that have been useful to me, and which I
never shall forget.
	I left home, relatives and friends on the
first day of Nov. 1873, accompanied to Og-
den by some of my relatives, whom I bid
good bye at the depot at 7.15, p. m. when
the cars started and we sped swiftly on the
way.
	Although the separation from those near
and dear to me left feelings of sadness in
my heart, yet it was not long before they
were expelled by the excitement with
which I was surrounded, and through my
desire to see the new scenery and every
thing that was of interest to be seen on the
way, for it was a beautiful moonlight night,
and objects at a considerable distance, could
be seen almost as distinctly as at noon-day.
	Our little party consisted of six persons.
viz. R. G. Lambert and wife, H. S. Wool-
ley and wife, B. Stringham Jr. and myself,
all young, the eldest of the company being
but 23 years old.
	We were all of a somewhat lively dispos-
ition and consequently did not hug sorrow,
which somewhat surprised the passengers
on the train, for numbers of them asked us
"how it was that we, leaving our homes
under such circumstances, could do so and
feel so light hearted?" Of course they did
not know as we did, that we were going ac-
cording to the call of the true servants of
God, to do His will and His work, and that
He did not require us to pull long faces and
to go with downcast countenances.
	To enter into minutiae and tell about all I
saw on that short, swift ride of two days
and two nights, over nine hundred miles
of road of the most beautiful scenery,
would take up too much of my time, and the
space of this letter, besides tiring your pa-
tience. Suffice it to say that every minute,
almost, exhibited some thing new and in-
teresting to one who had never before left
home in the valleys of the Rocky Moun-
tains.
	The second day of our traveling we pass-
ed a great deal of beautiful scenery through
Truckee, from where large quantities of
lumber are shipped to S. L. City, and over
the Sierra Nevada mountains. Over that
part of the road, there are a great many R.
R. stations where little towns have sprung
up and where the trains stop for a few min-
utes at each place. Shortly before reach-
ing a station the engine whistle would
blow which would apprise the people of the
near approach of a train. Hardly would
the gratings of the wheels of the stopping
cars cease, when numbers of boys and girls,

[Column 2]
who probably never had the privilege of
attending Sunday school, would be seen
running to and fro with baskets on their
arms, crying out to the passengers to buy
their fruits; for they were little fruit ven-
ders.
	When we arrived at Sacramento, and
while the train was stopping for half an
hour, I went on board a large steam boat
standing in the Saeramento, near its bank.
	There were also many other vessels near
by and these were the first that I had ever
seen. It was also the first time that I had
ever seen a really large river and city,
and also so many people gathered together
at one place; with the exception of when
the semi-annual conferences were held in S.
L. City. But the Sacramento R. R. depot
is t[--]s thronged nearly every day.
	At 7.30 p. m. on Monday the 2nd day
from home, the cars drew up at "Oakland"
ferry, on the opposite side of the harbor
from San Francisco and we—the passengers
—were ushered, or huddled rather, into
what at first appeard to be a sheep pen, in-
closed by a high picket fence, but which we
soon found out to be the entrance to the
harbor ferry boat.  The way we went in
there was like driving sheep into a corral,
for there were a great many people waiting
to go in and the entrance, or gate, was a
narrow one, admitting only one person at a
time, for no one was allowed to go in who
did not have a ticket to present to the keep-
er.
	At the wharf on the opposite side there
was an immense crowd assembled and it
was with difficulty that we followed our
guide to the coach that was to take us to
the hotel. It seemed but a minute 'til I
found myself on top of that coach with a
number of other,s and was riding through
crooked winding streets and cross roads, in
the midst of rumbling vehicles and clatter-
ing of horses hoofs over the pavement of
rough cobble stones, and we came to a stand
in front of the hotel.
	What a relief it seemed to get into a
quiet room where we could hear ourselves
talk. I almost imagine I can hear that
rumbling noise now...We previously ex-
pected to spend but one day in San Francis-
co, but the sailing of the vessel, which was
to take us, was postponed from Wednesday
to Saturday. We passed away the time
in looking about the city and visiting places
of note and interest.
	'Twas here that I first saw, in large num-
bers the news boys and boot blacks, of
whom most Sunday School children in Utah
have heard so often. It was really amus-
ing to see and hear them parading the
streets at their different avocations. One
thing I did not notice in the metropolis of
California, but which is prevalent in most
large cities, and that was many beggars in
the streets asking for alms.
	Our time was both pleasantly and profita-
bly spent in the large city, and the rustic
country lad saw many things to open his
eyes and cause him to wonder. My young
Sunday School friends can never realize how
many people there are in the world, 'til
they have the privilege of visiting some of
the large cities of the world.
	Saturday afternoon, Nov. 8th, we went
on board the Bark "Comet," which was to
carry us across the great Pacific ocean to
our destination. Sails were raised, and a
small vessel, called a "tug" was hitched
along side of the "Bark" and hauled it out
past the "Golden Gate," the name given to
the entrance to the harbor. There are many
beautiful sights to be seen in San Francisco
but that from the deck of a vessel while leav-

[Column 3]
ing the city and sailing out through the
bay is really grand to behold.
	While the "tug" was fast to our vessel, it
glided smoothly over the surface of the
sea, and everybody on board seemed to feel
first rate; but just as soon as the "tug" let
go, the vessel began to roll and rock, and
then, O! what a change came o'er their (the
passenger's) dreams. One by one they be-
gan to descend into the cabin, and to hunt
for their berths, or beds, and long before
we were out of sight of the "Golden State,"-
the once crowded deck of the vessel was al-
most entirely deserted. This was the re-
sult of sea sickness, something which af-
fects nearly all, who live on land entirely,
when they first go to sea.
	Not being troubled with sea sickness my
self I could not sympathize with those who
were; but it is such a disagreeable thing
that those who are afflicted with it do not
care, at the time, whether the "ship sinks
or floats" or whether they "live or die."
All but two of our little party were sick,
but in the course of three or four days they
were well and hearty, and we all enjoyed as
best we could, the balance of our long tedi-
ous sea voyage.
	On the morning of the 17th day out from
San Francisco, we arose from our berths
and found ourselves in sight of land, the
vessel sailing busily along the island of
"Oahu," the principal of this, the "Sand-
wich Islands," group. This made every
heart glad, for all were weary of the sea
voyage and wishing to get to land.
	Of course the vessel was immediately in
an uproar, the sailors making preparations
to enter the harbor and the passengers pre-
paring to go ashore. Before we came in
sight of Honolulu, the pilot boat was seen
sailing toward us, for the pilot had pre-
viously been signaled to come aboard and
take the vessel in.
	Having been sent to labor amongst the
inhabitants of these islands, I was, of
cource, naturally anxious to see what the
people looked like and also to hear their
language. I therefore drew near where the
boat came along side of the "Bark." Imag-
ine my astonishment on beholding some
half dozen large, dark-skinned, naked na-
tives; at least their only article of clothing
was what is termed in native, a "malo,"
tied about their loins. When talking it
seemed to me that what they said was one,
long, continuous word, uttered in one breath,
and the question immediately sprung up
within myself; "I wonder if I shall ever be
able to understand any one, who speaks as
fast as they appear to talk?" Then remem-
bering the promise of the servants of God
"that I should obtain a knowledge of the
language of the people" the question was
speedily answered.
	When we left home (S. L. City) the trees
had shed their leaves, winter had put on
its own peculiar robes, and we had seen the
snow laying on the ground. But on arriv-
ing here it seemed like passing from win-
ter to spring; for here the grass was green
and growing on the hills, the leaves green
on the trees and the flowers in bloom in the
neat, pretty gardens.
	It was the sudden change that made
every place in Honolulu, (the harbor city
and capital of this group) and on the island
appear so beautiful. It was, to us, like en-
tering into a paradise.
	As soon as the native saints heard of our
arrival they flocked to where we were stop-
ping, to see us; but as they could not speak
a word of English, nor we a word of Hawa-
iian, we could not converse with them
only by motions until they brought in an in-


terpreter, when we told them all the news
from the "Valleys of the Mountains."
	We rode on horse back 32 miles to Laie,
the head quarters of the church on these
islands, and over the most beautiful part of
that island. The natives, men, women
and children, gathered around as soon as we
arrived to see the new comers, and brought
us "Leis" (wreaths of flowers strung like
beads) with which they nearly covered the
wives of the brethren; and they also sang
a variety of native songs for us.
	Our reception was indeed a pleasant one,
the only regret we had being, that we could
not utter a sentence of native language.
	We reached Laie on the 28th of Nov., and
on the 13th of the following month (Dec.) I
left the brethren and sisters (from Zion,) and
the pleasant associations with them, and
started off to live amongst the natives and
study their language. One of the brethren
who understood the native tongue accom-
panied me to a little village called Kawai-
kepal," about 22 miles from Laie, and after
holding a meeting with the saints, introduc-
ing me to them and seeing me provided
for, he returned and left me alone with the
natives.
	Such questions as "Wasn't you home sick?
Didn't you get the blues? Were you not very
lonesome there all alone with no one to talk
to but natives?" Might arise in the minds
of some; but I can truly say, in answer to
such questions, no, not at all.
	Had I had nothing to do I certainly would
have been lonely and home sick, but I had
plenty to occupy my time in studying the
language, committing words and sentences
to memory, and in trying to converse with
the natives for practice.
	Whenever a tired or lonely feeling would
come over me I would take a stroll and hunt
for some one with whom to try and con-
verse, and all such feelings would soon be
expelled.
	In this way I spent three months study-
ing, at that one place, returning to Laie pre-
vious to our April conference, and a very
short three months it seemed to me, my
mind being so engrossed in seeking for the
language, that the time passed very rapidly.
	I presume that most of the Sunday School
scholars, at least the larger ones, have heard
at some time or other, about the principal
article of native food called "Poi," and have
formed the opinion, or conceived the idea,
that it is a very disagreeable thing for a
white person to eat. Let a person who had
never tasted, look at it and he would natur-
ally think so; but when a person once gets
used to it there is no food, which I can think
of, that is more pleasant and agreeable; es-
pecially when hungry.
	It is made of a vegetable that grows in
the water and is somewhat similar to the
beet in shape, but which grows with the
larger end downwards. After the vegeta-
ble has been cooked with steam, being pla-
ced over red hot rocks and then covered
over tightly to prevent the steam from es-
caping, it is then pounded up fine on a
board with a rock, after which it is mixed
with water and formed into a paste of suit-
able thickness to suit the persons eating it.
The natives always eat it with their fingers,
It is eaten with fish fowl or meat of some
kind.
	We seldom find knife, fork or spoon, at a
native house, and not being accustomed to
eat with our fingers, we therefore have to
take such articles along with us in our trav-
els. "Poi" is not the only article of food;
but there are potatoes, squash melons, and
a variety of delicious fruits. It may seem
strange to the young people of S. L. City,
to be told that water melons are now ripe
here in the month of December, which is
almost the middle of winter there, but it is
so. I have, myself, eaten water melon here
this month, and there are ripe fruits of
some kind, fresh and growing, to be had all
the year round.
	We have no such thing as snow or frost
on these islands, it is summer all the year
round; consistently it is only by thinking
of the months that I can remember when it
is winter at home.
	There are many peculiarities of the na-
tives that would be interesting to relate, 
but I have not time to enlarge upon them.
The people generally, are kind and very
hospitable, and in all my travels I never
was refused hospitality but two or three
times when I asked for it. They are al-
ways free to give when they have, especi-
ally anything to eat; and it is a custom
with them, when ever a stranger or neigh-
bor comes to their place, to ask him, or her
to eat, providing they have anything, and
as it is not etiquette to refuse, a native will
sometimes when travelling around, eat from
three to ten times a day. I have often had
to eat when I really did not wish to, to
avoid giving offense.
	Natives seldom take thought for to-mor-
row, but if they get a sumptuous supply to-
day they turn in and eat it all up if they
can, and let the morrow take thought for
itself. Through this way of living they are
often compelled to go hungry.
	The men and boys usually do all the cook-
ing, and the women and girls work in the
"Lois" (where the food is cultivated in wa-
ter) and in the potato patches along with
the men.
	This people are generally very fond of
animals and it is no unusual thing to see
them keeping pigs, dogs, cats, chickens,
turkeys and geese, and allowing them to
run in and out of the house just as they
please; but when we visit and stay with
them they drive all the animals and fowls
out of doors.
	I have slept in native houses when there
were dogs, cats, and pigs within, besides
any quantity of little animals such as mice,
cockroaches, fleas and mosquitoes. Natives
can live and be happy in the midst of all
these things.
	How thankful to the Lord the children of
the Latter-Day Saints shoudl be, that they
have neat, clean, comfortable houses in
which to live, and kind parents to care for
them and teach them cleanly habits.
	Not so with the children of this people;
they run in a manner wild; go where they
please; when they please; and do just
about as they like. The parents have very
little control over their children.
	According to the law of the land every
child, when old enough, is compelled to at-
tend school so many hours a day; conse-
quently nearly every body, old and young
can read and write; but when out of school
their time is spent in play, for they seldom
do any work while attending school, which
is until they are from 12 to 16 years old.
	If it was not that they are dependent on
their parents for food, they would not work
at all or obey their parents commands. But
as it is they have to work a little and give
some heed to their parents in order to get
their food. Unless the young girls and
boys go to some English or high school, they
are not taught to be industrious, to do any
neat or useful work, learn trades or keep
themselves neat and tidy.
	Of course there are honorable exceptions
to this general rule but they are very few.
I believe the children living on Laie dress
better, are more cleanly, better behaved,
and know more about useful and beneficial
employment than do the children of any
other place on these islands, outside of the
English schools where they make it a point
to teach these things.
	Again, there is some little respect shown
there, to virtue and morality, which is not
the case with the mass of the people through-
out these islands. The almost total disre-
gard to these great and Godly principles is
the great cause of the astonishlingly rapid
decrease in the population of these islands
from over eighty thousand in 1850, to about
twenty thousand at the present time. An-
other cause is, that they have the true and
everlasting Gospel preached in their midst,
but they disregard its mandates, will not
obey its principles, nor will they hearken to
the councels and instructions of the ser-
vants of God laboring in their midst; there-
fore the wrath of the Almighty rests up-
on them.
	I have traveled around every island of this
group, where people are living, and we are
allowed to go; and many times have the
natives pointed out to me places where
there were cities, villages and lands that
were thickly populated with natives when
Geo. Q. Cannon and a number of the breth-
ren came down here in 1850, to open up the
Gospel of Life to the people, that now are
desolate and barren wastes.
	When the remnants of Israel living on
these islands, are gathered out to the land
of their inheritance, their numbers will be
very few indeed unless the Lord works a
wonderful change with the people.
	Since October conference I have been
traveling continually from one island to an-
other, returning to head quarters at the end
of each six months, to attend the confer-
ence, rest a couple of weeks or so, and then
start off again.
	Our labors amongst the outsiders, in pro-
claiming the Gospel to them is, to look at it
naturally, almost ineffectual, for they have
heard it so often, and have set their minds
against it through fear of their priests, that
they do not want to hear it any more.
	We therefore cannot do much but travel
amongst the sains, or the members of the
church, and teach and encourage them to do
right, live their religion, and keep the com-
mandments of God.
	It is that there are a few souls, who are
honest and trying to keep the command-
ments of the Lord, that gives us encourage-
ment in our labors. Much good can be done
by setting a good example if in no other
way.
	I have often attended the public meetings
of the Calvinists and Catholics the only
Christian sects established among the na-
tives, but never had the opportunity grant-
ed me of speaking in their churches but
twice. The priests fear to grant us the priv-
ilege lest we convert their members and
they (the priests) thereby loose the dollars
and cents that they get from them (the
members). They labor for money, and that
only.
	Br. Stringham (my fellow laborer) and
I recently visited the leper settlement on
the island of "Molokai." It almost seems
that the Lord foresaw the calamity that was
coming upon this people, and therefore pre-
pared that wonderful and applicable natu-
ral prison for those of the loathsome dis-
ease; for there are but two ways of entrance
or to egress from the place, and those are by
sea and down a very high and steep moun-
tain path.
	We slept at the settlement one night held
meeting with the members of the church,
and preached to an audience of lepers. It


is a sorrowful and heart-rending sight to see
the condition in which those poor human
beings exist, for it is a mere existence.
Not only are grown people affected with it
but also children and even babes. On my
former visit I saw them in every form of
the disease, from the first outbreaking on
the babe to the poor helpless being who
was within a few hours of undergoing that
great separation of soul and body, for then I
visited the hospital wards; but the last vis-
it I avoided the wards, not wishing to again
behold the awful sight. God will punish
those who disregard His laws.
	Bro. J. H. Napela, whom many may re-
member having seen in Utah in 1870, and
who is still at the settlement attending on
his wife, wishes a kind remembrance to all
with whom he made acquaintance while in
Salt Lake City.
	There are now seven hundred lepers
at the settlement, and about that many
more not yet gathered in from amongst the
people. Many having the disease are secre-
ted by their friends.
	As I have to write in a rather uncomfort-
able position, laying flat on my stomach on
a native mat, for in the native houses we
seldom ever find either stools, chairs or ta-
bles, I begin to feel tired. And I fear that
the length of this letter will wear out your
patience. What I have written I sincerely
hope will be of interest to all who pursue or
hear it read; for I have penned just such
thoughts as came to my mind that I sup-
posed would be of interest.
	I would say to the children of the Sun-
day School: Continue to be punctual attend-
ers; hearken to and carry out the counsel
and instructions of your teachers; store your
minds with useful knowledge, by reading
good books, and especially the publications
of the church; cease to read all fictitious
novels for they will never do you any good;
always be obedient unto your parents and
God will bless you.
	If opportunity offers I may at some fu-
ture day, again write to the school. *	*


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.	143


144		WOMAN’S EXPONENT.

['[WomansExponent | Vol6 | 1877 | March | Issue: 19 | Title: DUTY OF PARENTS. | Author: Unknown]

		DUTY OF PARENTS.

	This is a subject I have reflected much
upon, and I feel that I would like to give
some of my views in regard to it. Not that
my ideas will be anything new, but we
sometimes need reminding and awakening
to our duties and obligations. Realizing as
I do the importance of the day in which we
live, and knowing that Satan is at work
