﻿PRINCESS MARY, DAUGHTER OF
              CHARLES I.
                      ______
          BY HANNAH T. KING.
                      ______
The royal family of Stewart were remarka-
ble for their beauty, for many fine qualities,
but most of all for their misfortunes! The
Princess Mary, daughter of Charles I, was
an exception in the catalogue, and yet little
has been historically written of her; while
other princesses’ lives have been traced on
the page of history again, and again; she had
a little sister Elizabeth, who died at Carris-
brook Castle while a child, and Queen
Victoria has had a lovely monument erected
to her memory in St. Thomas’s Church, Isle
of Wight. It will be remembered that
Charles I. married a beautiful French Prin-
cess, named Henrietta Maria, the youngest
daughter of Henry IV of France, and his
Queen Mariede Medices; she was not consider-
ed a very wise or suitable wife for an English
king, partly on account of her being a zealous
Roman Catholic, and that her training and
education unfitted her for so important a
position; but she was beautiful, and the king
loved her, and she returned his love. Her
eldest son, afterwards Charles II of England,
was born at Greenwich; his mother declared
him to be an ugly, though a very fine child,
and so solemn in his infant ways, that she felt
he was far wiser than herself. The next ar-
rival was a little daughter, born at Whitehall,
London, on the 4th of Nov., 1631, and was
warmly welcomed by both parents, but she
was a very delicate child, and so caused them
much anxiety; on this account they had her
baptized when only a few days old, by the
name of Maria, after her grandmother; they
were very poor at this time, and it suited their
circumstances to have a private christening, as
the Parliament would not vote more supplies
for them. The child was provided with a
nurse, and the Countess of Roxburgh was her
governess from the first day of her life! When
two years old, an establishment was arranged
for her, which certainly did not indicate pov-
erty; besides a nurse and three watchers, a
lady of the bedchamber, a seamstress, a tailor
and shoemaker, a gentleman usher, two pages,
a laundress, three grooms, four footmen, five
kitchen servants, a coachman, and more im-
portant than all, a good physician, Dr. Mayer,
whose skill and care were supposed to have
saved her life when quite an infant, and con-
sequently was much esteemed by the king,
her father, who was obliged to go to Scotland
soon after Mary’s birth, and so was particular-
ly anxious that her health should be strictly
attended to in his absence; the diet provided
for the little lady’s nursery was more substan-
tial than is procurable for children in this age,
being “manchet and fine bread, mutton or
chicken with all,” and the breakfast must have
been early, as it is reported the children dined
between ten and twelve, their dinner was game
and meat of various kinds, custards and sweet 
tarts, and Gascony wine; on fast days fish
instead of meat, supper the same, but not quite
so heavy. They lived at St. James’s Palace,
London, the greater part of the year, which
was a favorite place with the queen, their
mother, and she loved to walk in the beautiful
garden with the children, carrying the baby
in her arms, singing to it, and talking and
laughing with them.
  Charles I. was anxious that his children
should all speak English as their mother-
tongue, though his wife gave him some trouble
by her preference for her native  language, but
as her sons grew up and visited the French
court, they were unable to converse except in
English! The queen was, of course, a Roman
Catholic, and used to give  Mary rosaries and
crosses, and to take her into the chapel for
mass; when Mary was eight years old the king
found out what was going on, and positively
forbade her being taken there again, and in-
sisted that nothing should ever be said to her
against the protestant faith. She grew to be
a pretty child, and a devoted daughter to her
kind and good father her brother Charles
was her favorite companion.
  A very important event in Mary’s life was
brought about in consequence of her grand-
mother, Marie de Medicie, visiting England;
she came without being invited because it
happened to be convenient to her; this visit
was not an agreeable one, though the queen
was pleased to present her children to her
mother: and she told her “the king had made
her a happy wife, happy mother and a happy
queen!” But even at this time trouble was
gathering around the king, who, while this
happy reunion was taking place, was marching
at the head of his army against his Scotch
subjects, causing his family much sorrow and
anxiety; little Mary began to notice day after
day the grief on her mother's face and the
tears shed by her governess, the countess of
Roxburgh, who had been a second mother to
her, and whom she loved very much, but
whose Husband and son were fighting against
the king; and they missed their pleasant
walks at Richmond or Hampton Court, and
thought it very strange and disagreeable never
to be allowed to go out of doors without a guard
of armed soldiers; even at night her sleep was
disturbed by the tramp of the sentries at the
gates, and the poor child began to look care-
worn like the queen, her mother; at this time,
though so young, a way was already being
made for her to escape from the evils that were
coming on her father’s house; for Marie de
Medicie was the bearer of a message from the
Prince of Orange, asking her in marriage for
his son. Charles I. was very willing to listen
to such proposals for his younger daughters,
but he had more ambitious views for Mary,
besides which he loved her so much, that
he shrank from parting with her.	But
he found that large offers of wealth
were made for the bride, and that the Prince
of Orange would undertake to assist him in
his struggle with his rebellious subjects, and,
moreover, the little girl, who perhaps under-
stood what she had seen with her parents,
that difference in religion caused much misery
between husband and wife, told her father
that she should prefer being married to a


[Column 1]

﻿protestant partner. This decided the matter,
and in April, 1641, the young prince came
over, escorted by twenty vessels, and landed
at Gravesend, where he was met by a royal
equipage, and driven through London to
Whitehall where Charles and Henrietta re-
ceived him most affectionately; Mary was not
there, for she was in bed, poor child, at Somer-
set House with a feverish cold and swollen
face, but her bridegroom of sixteen begged so
hard to be permitted to see her, that the king
and queen took him thither, and the prince
was introduced to her by them, and he kissed
the little girl in spite of her swollen face, and
told his father in his first letter that she was
sweet and lovely; although young, he was very
manly in his manners and was highly accom-
plished, spoke four European languages, a good
mathematician, well acquainted with history,
both of his own country and that of others,
and had long been admitted to sit in the coun-
cils of the state, and Mary, from sympathy
with the trials of those she loved, was older
than her years. Arundel House in the Strand
had been fitted up for his reception, and he
had a private key to the garden of Somerset
House. A frequent interchange of visits took
place, and marriage was proposed and earnest-
ly insisted upon; the king objected in consid-
eration of the youth of his daughter: but, alas!
heavy clouds had long been gathering around
him and his throne, and he had much desired
to find a safe asylum for his beloved family,
for Charles was a devoted husband and father;
it was now proposed by the Prince of Orange,
the prince's father, that the marriage should
be solemnized, that the young princess should
still remain under the care and jurisdiction of
her mother for two years, that the queen
should accompany the young bride to Hol-
land, and at the expiration of the two years,
she should pass to the house of her husband.
Charles at last bowed to a dire necessity that
surrounded him and his family; the marriage
took place with the usual court ceremonies, as
soon as preparations could be made ready.
A royal vessel was duly fitted out, and then
came the cruel separation, the king was per-
fectly unmanned at parting with his almost
worshiped child, for having a mind beyond
her years she had been the solace and comfort
of his troubled life; he repeatedly clasped her
in his arms, blest her and wept bitter tears
over her; probably he had a presentiment that
their separation was final. As long as the
vessel was in sight he stood on the shore mak-
ing fond signals of adieus, he then called for
a swift horse that he might rapidly ride to a
high eminence that still commanded a view of
the ship that bore away his heart's treasures!
Never to meet again on earth! Time passed—
the final tragedy of regicide is history. Mary
mourned the sad, sad finale of her beloved
father, but her mother and her husband blest
and consoled her; the two years with her
mother was religiously kept sacred and then
she was placed in the beautiful home that had
been fondly prepared for her by the hand of
love. Alas, a very few years elapsed before
this lovely and chaste union was dispelled by
the cruel hand of death, the young prince
was seized with malignant smallpox, and left
Mary a widow at nineteen.
	At the restoration of Charles the Second,
he invited his widowed neice and her boy to
come and make her home in England, she
accepted the kindly invitation, and Whitehall,
the home of her childhood, was appointed for
her residence. (A window is still shown in
that fine building from which Charles I.
emerged to walk to the fatal block, from
whence he was ushered into eternity.)
	Mary had not resided long in the beautiful
home of her childhood, when she was attacked
by smallpox, which in a short time terminated
her life in the very bloom of womanhood,

[Column 2]

leaving her son, who in after years ascended
the throne of England by the title of William
III, having married Mary, the eldest daugh-
ter of the deposed King, James II. Parlia-
ment desired that her name should be conjoint-
ly blended with his in the government of the
kingdom, and hence they ascended the throne
unitedly as William and Mary.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


"]

Name: 12_said_man_little_asked
Representative_Docs:
['[WomansExponent | Vol3 | 1874 | January | Issue: 16 | Title: DIVERSIONS. | Author: Unknown]

			DIVERSIONS.

	A WOMAN is generally more economical
than a man, because her "waist is small-
er.
	A CALIFORNIA compositor made an edi-
tor say about a contemporary that it stood
"in the front rank of inferior journals."
	MRS. Partington reading of the strike of
the wire drawers, remarked: "Ah, me!
what new fangled things won't they wear
next.
	AN Irishman returned from his travels
gallantly compared his landlady to Vesuvi-
us, because "she was a fine old crater."
	"THE honeymoon is well enough," said a
prudent belle "but what I want to see be-
yond that is the promise of a fine harvest-
moon.
	AS to the familiar problem—What is
home without a mother?—a Brooklyn boy
says that it is a very pleasant place provided
she leaves the key of the pantry behind
her.
	"PAPA, are you growing taller all the
time?" "No, my child; why do you ask"
"'Cause the top of your head is poking 
through your hair.
	THE name "Kalakaua," King of the
Sandwich Islands, is pronounced Kollow-
kow-ah but some pronounce it Calico, and
others Kill-a-crow.
	WE are told that the smallest hair throws
a shadow. Of course it does, it throws a
shadow over your appetite when you find
it in your victuals.
	THERE is still another point in favor of
narrow-gauge railroads. When the locomo-
tive runs over a man two-thirds of the
body is left in good shape for identification.
	IN summing up the household necessa-
ries, a young husband asked, "Now what
portion are you to find?" "I'll find the
fault" replied the animated wife.
	ALBANI never speaks aloud on the days
preceding the nights when she is adver-
tized to sing, and in various other respects
she is highly calculated to make some man
happy in the domestic circle.
	PEDAGOGUE:—First little boy, what is
your name?" Little boy—"Jule." Peda-
gogue—"Oh, no; your name is Julius.
Second little boy, what is yours?" Second
little boy—"My name is Billious."
	A DALLAS man is so extremely verdant
as to advertise for a lost umbrella—inno-
cently supposing it was taken by mistake.
Such faith in human nature is inexpress-
ively touching considering the state of the
weather.
	A LADY went into a carpet store recently
and pointing out a carpet asked the proprie-
tor what it was. "Brussels," says the pro-
prietor. "Brussels," quoth the lady, pass-
ing her hand over it, "Seems to me the
brustles don't stick up much."
	"BOY," said a traveler to a disobedient
youth whom he encountered, "don't you
hear your father speaking to to you?" "Oh,
y-a-a-s," replied the youth. "But I don't
mind what he says. Mother don't neither,
and twixt she and I, we'ev about got the
dog so he don't.
	IN one of the Cape Cod towns, a young
scholar, the first day at school, was asked
her name by the teacher, and replied. Her
father's name was the next question, and
she did not know his first name. The

[Column 2]
teacher then asked her, "What does your
mother call him?" "You Jackass!" said
the child.
	YOUNG lady picks up the morning paper
and reads advertisement: "A splendid assort-
ment of ladies' corsets just opened at"—She
throws the paper down in disgust. "Yes
they must have been a splended assortment
of ladies that would go down there to have
their corsets opened! They ought to be
sent to prison, they and the rascals too!"
Excuse young lady, she was at a party last
night, and it didn't close till four.


', '[WomansExponent | Vol2 | 1873 | May | Issue: 23 | Title: DIVERSIONS. | Author: Unknown]

DIVERSIONS.
	SKY-LIGHTS.—Sun, moon and stars.
	WHAT, word is always pronounced wrong?
Wrong. 
	HOTEL-KEEPERS are people we have to
put up with. 
	WHAT has a cat that nothing else has?
Kittens. 
WHEN is a dog like a fixed doctrine?—
When it is a dog-ma. 
	A DOG well-dressed wears a collar and
pants in the summer.
	IT has been asked, When rain falls, does
it ever get up?  Of course it does;—in dew
time.
	STRANGE to say, no matter how well paid
a dentist may be, he will always look down
in the mouth.
	"I HAVE lost flesh,” said a toper to his
companion.  “No great loss,” replied, the
other, “since you have made it up in spir-
its."
	“When a fellow is too lazy to work in
this section,” writes a correspondent from a
village in Canada East, “he paints his name
over his door, and calls it a tavern, and makes
all his neighbors as lazy as himself.”
	IRISH TELESCOPE—An Irishman was one
day observing to a friend that he had a most
excellent telescope.  “Do you see yon
church,” said he, “about half a mile off?
It’s scarcely discernible; but when I look at
it through my telescope, it brings it so close
that I can hear the organ playing.”	
	﻿Irascible old man—“Conductor, why didn’t
you wake me as I asked you? Here I am,
miles beyond my station.”—Conductor—“I
did try,sir, but all I could get out of you was,
'All right, Maria; get the children their
breakfast, and I’ll be down in a minute. ”
	A TROY paper thus states a Trojan joke:
“A dentist of this city became emotionally
insane while repairing a front tooth for a
pretty woman, and kissed her. She imme-
diately went home and told her husband,
who went around that way the same eve-
ning, and borrowed $500 of the dentist on
long time and without interest.” 
	A GUARDIAN of the peace in New York
made his first essay as a ‘mounted police-
man,’ one day last week, by grasping the
horse’s tail and attempting to climb up that
way. The surgeon subsequently remarked,
that no horse ever had a finer opportunity,
or took advantage of it with such infinite
scorn of the consequences.
	A MARKET girl sold a gentleman a fine fat
goose, warranting it to be young.  It turned
out, when roasted, to be unmanageably
tough.  The next day the gentleman said
to the market girl, “That goose which you
sold me for a young one was very old.”—
“Certainly not,” said the girl “don’t you
call me young?”—“Yes.”—“Well I am but
nineteen, and I heard mother say often, that
that goose was six weeks younger than me.”
	A MAN was brought up by a farmer, and
accused of stealing some ducks. The farmer
said he should know them anywhere, and
went on to describe their peculiarity.
“Why,” said the magistrate, “they can’t be
such a very rare breed! I have some like
them in my yard.”  “That’s very likely,
your honor,” said the farmer; “these are
not the only ducks of the same sort I have
had stolen lately!”
	“DEAR old Aunt Mary,” said a school
girl, “don’t see very well, and last Sunday
she was buzzing around getting ready for
church, looking for umbrella, specs, over-
shoes, and last but not least her prayer book.
The latter she thought she secured by grab-
bing something off her bureau at the last
moment, but when she got to church it
proved to be my music box, and the old
lady, in trying to find her place in this un-
common book of prayer, touched the spring
and it went off in fine style to the tune of
“O Jim Along, Jim Along Josey.”
	THE following is the verdict of a negro
jury: “We, de undersigned, bein' a Koro-
ner’s jury to sit on de body of de nigger,
Sambo, now done dead and gone afore us,
hab been sittin’ on de said nigger aforesaid,
and find dat de same did on de night of de
fourteenth of November come to def by
fallin’ from de bridge ober de riber in de said
riber and brokin his neck, where we'find he
'was subsequently drowned and afterwards
washed to do riber side, whar we suppose
he was froze to def.”
	ONE night a judge, a military officer, and
a priest, all applied for lodging at an inn
where there was but one spare bed, and the
landlord was called upon to decide which
had the best claim of the three.  “I have
lain fifteen years in the garrison at B.,”
said the officer.  “I have sat as judge twenty
years in R.,” said the judge.  “With your
leave, gentlemen, I have stood in the min-
istry twenty-five years at N.,” said the
priest. “That settles the dispute,” said the
landlord. “You, Mr. Captain, have lain fif-
teen years you, Mr. Judge, have sat twenty
years but the aged pastor has stood fivc-
and-twenty years, so he has the best right
to the bed.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]
', '[WomansExponent | Vol2 | 1873 | April | Issue: 21 | Title: DIVERSIONS. | Author: J. G, B,"]

			DIVERSIONS.
	THE man question.—What is he worth?
	WHY is a new-born baby like a gale of
wind? Because it begins with a squall.
	WHAT is that which goes from New York
to Albany every day and yet never stirs?—
The turnpike.
	WHAT is the difference between a jewel-
ler and a jailer? One sells watches and the
other watches cells.
	A LITTLE boy asked a lady who made her
teeth."My Creator," she replied. "Well,"
said the youngster, "Dr. —— made my ma's,
and they beat your'n clean out o' sight."
	A LADY having brought a suit to recover
a chignon from a hair-dresser with whom
she had left it for repair, her little boy
wanted to know "if that chignon was what
is called 'hair-at-law?'"
	MARYLAND girls wont marry in the full of
the moon, believing they would have ill-
luck through life, but a Massachusetts girl
wouldn't let forty full moons stop her ten
seconds.
	A YOUNG dandy, who sported an enor-
mous mustache, asked a lady what she
thought of his looks? "Why," said she
"you look as if you had swallowed a pony,
and left the tail sticking out of your
mouth."
	AT a collection recently made at a charity
fair, a lady passed the plate to a very wealthy
man who was well known for his stinginess.
"I have nothing," was his curt reply. "Then
take something, sir," she answered, "You
know I am begging for the poor."
	"Do you enjoy going to church now?,,
asked a lady of Mrs. Partington. "La, me,
I do," replied Mrs. P. Nothing does me so
much good as to get up early on Sunday
morning and go to church, and hear a popu-
lous minister dispense with the gospel."
	"I DON'T like these shoes," said a lady
customer, "because the soles are too thick."
"Is that the only objection?" blandly asked
the salesman. "Yes," was the reply.
"Then, madam, if you take the shoes, I can
assure you that the objection will gradually
wear away."
	THE first Marquis of Huntley, on his first
arrival at court, forgot the usual tribute of
obeisance, and was asked why he did not
bow? He begged his Majesty's pardon, and
excused his want of respect, by saying, he
was just come from a place where everybody
bowed to him.
	"A MEMBER of our Legislature," says an
Ohio paper, "who was on a dead-head excur-
sion out on the plains, lost his pass, and had
to walk seventy miles before he could find a
man who had sufficient confidence in his ap-
pearance to lend him money to telegraph
home to his wife to sell the cooking-stove
and remit him the proceeds at once."
	A SUBURBAN minister applied to a ticket
agent on one of the railroads for a "clergy-
man's ticket," and on the official expressing
a doubt  to his clerical character, exclaim-
ed, "If you don't believe I'm a clergyman,
I'll read you one of my sermons!" The
agent gave him a ticket without insisting
upon the proof.
	SMART reply.—A humorous fellow being
subpoenaed as a witness on a trial for an as-
sault, one of the counsel, who had been no-
torious for brow-beating witnesses, asked
him what distance he was from the parties
when the assault happened; he answered,
"Just four feet five inches and a half."—
[Column 2]
"How came you to be so very exact, fellow?"
said the counsel. "Because I expected some
fool or other would ask me," said he, "so I
measured it."
	A WASHINGTON paper tells this good
story about Hon. S. S. Cox, the irrepressible
Congressman from New York City. The
other day Cox was unusually active, spurty
and irrepressible in the House. He was
continually at the front, "catching on the
fly," and rampaging round generally. Fi-
nally, when the House came to an agreement
to proceed with the debate on the salary bill
in twenty minute speeches, Cox despatched
a page with the following note:
	"Dear Mr. Speaker.—Put me down for
twenty minutes.				S. S. C.
	To which the Speaker replied;
	"Dear Cox.—I would be delighted if I
could keep you down half that time.
J. G, B,"


']

Name: 13_water_sugar_eggs_household hints
Representative_Docs:
["[WomansExponent | Vol3 | 1874 | January | Issue: 16 | Title: HOUSEHOLD HINTS | Author: Unknown]

HOUSEHOLD HINTS

	Grandmother's Gingerbread.—Cup and a half of
molasses, cup rich sour cream, teaspoonful salara
-tus, tablespoonful ginger, mix a little stiff.
	German toast.—To one egg, beaten well, add one
cup sweet milk or cream, season with a little salt
and pepper. Cut in slices. stale bread, and dip in
the milk to moisten, and fry in butter on a griddle.
This we think is an extra nice dish for breakfast.
	Good Johnny Cake.—Beat up one egg eith one-
third of a cup of sugar, add one tablespoonful of
melted butter, and one pint of sweet milk, two
and a half cups of meal, half cup of flour, rea-spoonful of cream-of-tartar and half teaspoonful of
soda.
	Sponge Gingerbread.—One cup of sour milk,
one cup of molasses, one-half cup of butter, two
eggs, one-half teaspoonful of salaratus, one table-
spoonful of ginger, flour to make it thick as pound
cake. Put the butter, molasses and ginger together
and make them quite warm, then add the milk,
flour and salaratus and bake as soon as possible.
	Sponge Cake.—The following recepie for sponge
cake is pronounced excellent: Ten eggs; the same
weight of sugar and one half the weight of flour.
The grated rind and juice of one lemon. Beat
yolks and sugar to a cream; then stir in gradually
and very gently the flour, and the whites of the
eggs, well beaten; add lemon.
	Doughnuts.—One teacupful of sugar, one tea-
cupful of sweet milk, one egg, one teaspoonful of
soda, one teaspoonful of cream of tartar, one tea-
spoonful of sweet cream or lump of butter the size
of a hickory nut and spice to taste, roll thin and
cut with a round tin, making a hole in the center
with a thimble; fry until a light brown.
	Breakfast Cake.—In absence of potatoes; or, to
be eaten with butter while warm. Equal parts
sifted corn meal and flour; stir or sift in carefully
while dry one teaspoonful of cream of tartar. Dis-
solve in milk (sufficient to mix, and form a batter
that will pour easily into baking tins), one level
teaspoonful each of carbonate of soda and salt;
sweeten to taste. Lastly, add one or two (accord-
ing to supply) well-beaten eggs to one cake, and
bake in a quick oven to a nice brown. Do not
scald and spoil your meal.
	How to Cook Oatmeal.—First, be sure to get new,
fresh oatmeal, as if it becomes damp or old it is
bitter. Put one quart of water into a tinned stew
pan; salt sufficient to be palatable; stir in carefully,
so as not to have it lumpy, three or four handfuls of
oatmeal. Put it over the fire and stir continually
until it has swollen all it will, using care not to
have it burn on the bottom. When it has swollen
all is will, add more water, and then put the stew
pan into a kettle of hot water and leave it cooking
several hours—the longer the better, as the longer
it is cooked the softer and more jelly-like it becomes.
Having the stew pan in another kettle of hot water
prevents its burning on the bottom, and you are re-
lieved from constant stirring, It is good with
milk, syrup, or sweetened milk; or even with but-ter alone.


[Column 1]
", '[WomansExponent | Vol3 | 1874 | December | Issue: 13 | Title: HOUSEHOLD HINTS. | Author: Unknown]

		HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	To Preserve Fruit Jellies from Mold.—Cover the
surface one fourth of an inch deep with fine pulver-
ized loaf sugar.  When thus protected, the jellies
will keep for years in good condition, and free from
mouldiness.
	Small Bread Pudding.—Boil one pint of milk
with a piece of cinnamon and lemon-peel; pour it
on two ounces of bread-crumbs; add two eggs, half
an ounce of currants and a little sugar: steam it in
a buttered mold for about an hour.
	Plain Baked Eggs.—Butter a dish that will bear
the heat of the oven; break the eggs carefully into
it and season with a little butter, salt, and pepper
if desired; putting the butter in small pieces over.
Place in a slack oven till set, and serve.
	Bread and Butter Pudding.—Butter a dish well,
and sprinkle some currants all around it; then lay
in slices of bread and butter; to two eggs well
whipped add one pint of boiling milk, sweeten
with sugar and pour it over the bread and butter.
Bake in a hot oven.
	Toast and Eggs.—Break the eggs into a stewpan
and add a little salt, pepper and fresh butter; set
the stewpan over a moderate fire and stir round
with a spoon, being careful to keep every particle
in motion until the whole has become a smooth and
delicate thickish substance; have ready convenient
sized crisp pieces of toast, pour the eggs upon
them and serve.
	Cottage Cheese.—Place thick, loppered milk on
the stove, and let it heat thoroughly, not to cook,
or it will be hard. Pour into a hot cloth, and let
it drain until dry; season with salt and butter.
Should it be rather dry, moisten with milk or
cream, and make into balls. Some prefer what is
called "smearkase." Prepare the milk as stated
above, but, instead of making into balls, thin with
sweet cream and add a little pepper.
	To Clean Floor Oil-Cloths.—Soap should not be
[Column 2]
used in cleaning oil-cloths. To half a pail of hot,
soft water, add half a cup of washing fluid, or a
piece of sal-soda half the size of an egg, with a
scrubbing-brush or broom rub hard, putting on only
sufficient water so that it will not run off; wipe dry
with a soft cloth; it will look as bright and shining
as when new. This is a good way to clean com-
mon paint, as it easily removes smoke, grease, or
dust, and does not destroy the gloss or varnish. It
is also useful in washing windows.
	Rice Milk.—Wash a tablespoonful of good rice,
drain and put into a stewpan with a pint of milk
upon the fire; when boiling place it at the corner
to simmer until the rice is quite tender, (for inva
lids it must be a pulp); sweeten with sugar and it
is quite ready. Or if preferred it may be sea-
soned with a little butter and salt. It should be
about the thickness of well made gruel, and it is
very nutritious especially for an invalid who has
been long ill.
	Poached Eggs,—Put a pint of water in a stew
pan; add half a teaspoonful of salt, place it over
the fire and when boiling break your eggs into it as
near the surface of the water as possible, let them
boil gently about three minutes; care should be
taken not to boil too many together; if the yolk
separates from the white it may be presumed that
the egg is not fresh, though the same thing may
happen through awkwardness in poaching. After
they are taken carefully from the water, placed in a
warm dish and drained, lay over them some pieces
of butter, and place before the fire or in a warm
oven for a moment only. Some people think to
add a small quantity of vinegar to the water in
which eggs are poached is an improvement. 
	For Cleansing the Hair and Scalp.—In the
warm days of summer, with dust and perspiration,
the heads of people, especially of laborers and
children, get very dirty, and common washing will
not remove this. Here is a recipe, given by a no-
ted lecturer on hygiene: In a pint bottle put a
spoonful of olive oil and add two ounces of best
spirits of ammonia or hartshorn; shake. Then
mix with three ounces of alcohol and, when thor-
oughly mixed fill the bottle with soft water. Re-
member to put in the ingredients in the order named
or you will have "stuff," and no chemical union at
all. To apply, take a spoonful or two of this, with
a little warm water; dip a sponge or rag in and
wet the scalp thoroughly, now rinse off in a plenty
of warm water, and you will be surprised at the
amount of dirt, though you may have thought your
head perfectly clean. For the heads of young in-
fants it is just the thing, as it instantly removes the
dirty scurf without pain or trouble. It should be
rather weaker for a babe than for an adult.


[Column 1]
', '[WomansExponent | Vol3 | 1874 | March | Issue: 19 | Title: HOUSEHOLD HINTS | Author: Unknown]

		HOUSEHOLD HINTS

     	Milk Stains.—Milk stains on serge dresses may
be removed by steeping the part in warm water.
	Jelly Cake—One cup butter, 1 cup sugar—4
eggs—11 cups flour—beat the eggs separately. 
	To Make Muffins.—Two eggs, two quarts of
flour, a pint of sweet milk, one pound of butter,
a gill of yeast, a teaspoonful of salt. 
	To Make Grandmother’s Gingerbread—Cup
and a half molasses, cup of rich sour cream, tea-
spoonful saleratus, teaspoonful of ginger; mix a 
little stiff.
	Clove Cake.—One cup molasses—1 cup sugar—
1 cup butter—1 cup buttermilk—3 eggs—3 cups
flour—1 tablespoon cloves—l of cinnamon—l tea-
spoon saleratus in milk—raisins. 
	Apple Sauce.—Two quarts of water, a pint of
molasses, a root of ginger, and boil altogether
twenty minutes; put in while boiling one peck of
pared, cored and quartered apples.  Stew till ten-
der. 
	Waffles.—One quart of flour, one pint of sour
milk, one teaspoonful of  soda, four eggs, a piece
of butter the size of a large egg, and a little salt.
Bake in waffle irons.  Sour cream and less butter
improves them. 
	Colors to Harmonize with Mahogany.—The
American Artisan says "mahogany must not be as-
sociated with red, scarlet or orange, otherwise its
effect is destroyed.  It harmonizes with almost
every other tint that is used in upholstery."
	Beef Stew.—Six pounds of the flank of beef,
cut in small pieces; boil in two quarts of water
until tender.  Then put in a dozen potatoes, a
dozen onions, and four turnips; cover it so that the
steam shall not escape; salt and pepper to taste. 
	Grape Jam.—Boil the Grapes in just water
enough to make them tender, strain them through
a colander, then in  one pound of pulp put one
pound of sugar; boil this half an hour; the com-
mon wild grape makes a nice jam. 
	Cream Cake.—Beat two eggs with one cup of
sugar, add one cup of thin cream, a teaspoonful of
salcratus and one of salt; flavor with nutmeg and
lemon or other spice, mix as stiff as will pour into
pans. A handful of currants improves it. Try
it. 
	Lamb Stew.—Take half a shoulder of lamb,
boil it in two quarts of water two hours.  Then
put in potatoes, onions if desired, and turnips cut in
quarters; two teaspoonfuls of salt and pepper to
taste.  Ten minutes before serving put in the
dumplings. 
 	Cream Pie. —One and a half cups of sugar,
three cups of flour, one cup of milk, two eggs, two

[Column 2]
tablespoonfuls of cream tartar, one teaspoonful of
saleratus. Cream for inside, one pint of milk,
one-half cup of sugar, a little butter and salt, two
tablespoonfuls of flour (or corn starch), Flavor
with lemon. (Make two pies). Stir into the
milk while boiling till sufficiently thick.
	Taking Care of Boots.—One who has experi-
ence is convinced that a coat of gum copal varnish
applied to the soles of boots and shoes, and repeat-
ed as it dries, until the pores are filled and the
surface shines like polished mahogany, will make
the soles waterproof, and also cause them to last
three times as long as ordinary soles.
 	Rice Pudding with Fruit.—Swell the rice with
a very little milk over the fire then mix fruit of any
kind with it, currants, goosberries scalded, pared and
quartered apples, raisins, or black currants, and
still better, red currant jelly, with one egg to bind
the rice; boil it well, and serve with powdered cin-
namon and sugar.
	Oatmeal Pancakes.—Stir into four cupsful of
cold water a teaspoonful of salt, a large handful of
graham flour and oatmeal till you have a thin bat-
ter.  Bake at once on a very hot griddle; the Gra-
ham flour improves the pancakes, as they are
more easily turned and not quite so dry as when
oatmeal only is used.  When preferred, one cup of
water can be left out, using instead of it a cupful
of buttermilk and a teaspoonful of soda. 
	Shin of Beef Soup.—Have the shin well
cracked up; put it to boil in five or six quarts of
water: boil it five or six hours; skim it very often.
Cut up very fine, half a white cabbage; chop two
turnips and three onions if desired; put them all
into the soup with pepper and salt, and boil it two
hours; take the bone and gristle out before serving.
If you have some raised dough, make up a dozen
balls, the size of a nutmeg, drop them into the
soup and let them boil half an hour.


[Column 1]

['[WomansExponent | Vol4 | 1875 | June | Issue: 1 | Title: HOUSEHOLD HINTS. | Author: Unknown]

HOUSEHOLD HINTS.

	Tomatoes.—After stewing them a few minutes
and just before dishing them, add a little flour and
sweet cream, pepper salt and butter as in other
dishes.  Another good way to prepare tomatoes is
to slice them rather thickly, salt pepper and roll in
flour, then fry brown in butter, kept a boiling heat
all the time.
	American Yorkshire Pudding.—When scraps of
bread and meat have accumulated, instead of mak-
ing "hash," chop the meat finely, soften the bread
with boiling water, mix the two ingredients to-
gether add an egg or two some salt pepper and
sage. Bake in an ordinary pan; thus you save all,
and have a dish superior to "hash."
	Lettuce.—Gather some nice heads of lettuce ten-
der and crisp; wash and cut in quarters or shreds;
to a proportionate quantity of cream add a small
bit of butter and pepper and salt, heat this to a boil-
ing point and pour over the lettuce covering it for a
few moments.  This dish is said to be a good sub-
stitute for green peas before they are in season.
	Preparing Coffee.—Put the gronnd coffee to
soak over night in enough cold water to wet it all,
in the morning add more water; put it over the fire
and let it boil fifteen or twenty minutes, then fill
in what boiling water is necessary, and put the cof-
fee-pot on the stove-hearth to settle for fifteen min-
utes.  Coffee thus prepared will be clear and am-
ber-colored, and if left over will heat again better
than if settled with eggs.
	Cucumber Pickles.—Pick and wash the cucum-
bers of not more than three days growth, pack in a
keg or jar in close layers, over each layer, place a
layer of salt in which alum has been well mixed,
say a tablespoonful to each pint of salt; do not add
any water.  When wanted for use freshen them.
Scald in strong vinegar spiced to the taste. Drain
the pickles dry and cover with fresh vinegar add-
ing to each pint of the vinegar a tablespoonful of
sugar. The vinegar which is first poured over
them will inbibe too much of the salt to be agreea-
ble to the taste, and will lose much of its strength
while the fresh vinegar poured over them al-
ready sour, with the addition of the sugar, will
preserve them in full strength.


[First column]
		﻿HYGIENIC NOTES.
	SICK HEAD ACHE Is sickness at stom-
ach, a tendency to vomit, combined with 
pain in some part of the head, generally the
left side. It is caused by there being too
much bile in the system, from the fact that
this bile is manufactured too rapidly, or is
not worked out of the system fast enough
by steady, active exercise. Hence sedentary
persons, those who do not walk about a great
deal, but are seated in the house nearly all
the time, are almost exclusively the victims
of this distressing malady. It usually be-
[Column 2]
gins soon after waking up in the morning,
and lasts a day or two or more. There are
many causes; the most frequent is, derange-
ment of the stomach by late and hearty sup-
pers; by eating too soon after a regular
meal, (five hours should, at least, intervene;)
eating without an appetite; forcing food;
eating after one is conscious of having had
enough; eating too much of any favorite
dish; eating something which the stomach
can not digest, or sour stomach. Any of 
these things may induce sick head-ache;
all of them can be avoided. Over-fatigue or;
great mental emotion of any kind, or se-
vere mental application, have brought on
sick head-ache, of the most distressing char-
acter, in an hour; it is caused by indulgence
in spirituous liquors. When a person has
sick head-ache, there is no appetite; the
very sight of food is hateful; the tongue is
furred; the feet and hands are cold, and
there is a feeling of universal discomfort,
with an utter indisposition to do anything
whatever. A glass of warm water, into
which has been rapidly stirred a heaping
tea-spoon each of salt and kitchen mustard,
by causing instantaneous vomiting, empties
the stomach of the bile or undigested sour
food, and a grateful relief is often experi-
enced on the spot; and rest, with a few hours
of sound, refreshing sleep, completes the 
cure, especially if the principal part of the
next day or two is spent in mental diversion
and out-door activities, not eating an atom
of food (but drinking freely of cold water
or hot teas) until you feel as if a piece of
plain, cold bread and butter would "taste
really good." Nine cases in ten the cause of
sick head-ache is in the fact, that the stom-
ach was not able to digest the food last in-
troduced into it, either from it being un-
suitable, or excessive in quantity. When
the stomach is weak, a spoonful of the mild-
est blandest food would cause an attack of
sick head-ache, when ten times the amount
might have been taken in health, not only
with impunity but with positive advantage.
	Those who are "subject to sick head ache"
cat too much and exercise too little, and
have cold feet and constipation. A diet of
cold bread and butter, and ripe fruits or 
berries, with moderate continuous exercise
in the open air, sufficient to keep up a very
gentle perspiration, would, of themselves,
cure almost every case within thirty-six
hours. Two teaspoonfuls of pulverized
charcoal, stirred in half a glass of water, and
drank, generally gives instant relief.
	RHEUMATISM Affects the joints, the
hinges of the body, in such a way, that the
slightest motion of the ailing part gives
pain. A creaking hinge is dry, and turns
hard. A single drop of oil to moisten it
makes a wonderful change, and it instantly
moves on itself with the utmost facility.
Rheumatism is an inflammation of the sur-
face of the joints. Inflammation is heat; this
heat dries these surfaces; hence, the very
slightest effort at motion gives piercing
pain. In a healthy condition of the parts,
nature is constantly throwing out a lubrica-
ting oil, which keeps the joints in a per-
fectly smooth and easy-working condition.
Rhematism is almost always caused—in-
deed, it may be nearer the truth to say,
that it is always the result of a cold damp-
ness. A dry cold, or a warm dampness, does
not induce rheumatism. A garment, wet-
ted by perspiration or rain, or water in an-
other form, about a joint, and allowed to dry
while the person is in a state of rest, is the
most common way of causing rheumatism.
A partial wetting of a garment is more apt
to induce an attack than if the entire cloth-
ing were wetted; because, in the latter case,
[Column 3]
it would be certainly and speedily ex-
changed for dry garments. The very mo-
ment a garment is wetted in whole or in
part, change it, or keep in motion suffi-
cient to maintain a very slight perspiration;
until the clothing is perfectly dried.
	The failure to wear woolen flannel next
the skin, is the most frequent cause of rheu-
matism; for a common muslin or linen or
silk shirt on a person in a perspiration, be
comes damp and cold the instant a puff of
air strikes it, even in mid-summer. This is
not the case when woolen flannel is worn
next the skin.
	This troublesome affection is cured by
keeping the joint affected wound around
with several folds of woolen flannel; second,
live entirely on the lightest kinds of food,
such as coarse breads, ripe fruits, berries,
boiled turnips, stewed apples, and the like.
If such things were eaten to the extent of
keeping the system freely open, and exer-
cise were taken, so that a slight moisture
should be on the surface of the skin all the
time; or if, in bed, the same thing were ac-
complished by hot teas and plentiful bed-
clothing, a grateful relief and an ultimate
cure will very certainly result in a reasona-
bly short time. Without these, the disease
will continue to torture for weeks and
months and years.
	Inflammatory rheumatism may, for all prac-
tical purposes, be regarded as an aggravated
form of the common kind, extended to all
the joints of the body instead of implicat-
ing only one or two. For all kinds, time,
flannel, warmth, with a light cooling diet,
are the great remedies.
	AIR, SUNSHINE AND HEALTH.—A New
York merchant noticed, in the progress of
years, that each successive book-keeper lost
his health, and finally died of consumption,
however vigorous and robust he was on en-
tering his service. At length it occurred to
him that the little rear-room where
the books were kept opened in a back-
yard, so surrounded by high walls,
that no sunshine came into it from one year's
end to another. An upper room, well light-
ed, was immediately prepared, and his
clerks had uniform good health ever after.
	A French lady became ill. The most em-
inent physicians of her time were called in,
but failed to restore her. At length Dupey-
tren, the Napoleon of physic, was consulted.
He noticed that she lived in a dim room, in-
to which the sun never shone; the house be-
ing situated in one of the narrow streets, or
rather lanes of Paris. He at once ordered
more airy and cheerful apartments, and
"all her complaints vanished."
	From these facts, which cannot be disput-
ed, the most common mind should conclude
that cellars, and rooms on the northern side
of buildings, or apartments into which the 
sun does not immediately shine, should nev-
er be occupied as family-rooms or chambers
or as libraries or "studies." Such apart-
ments are only fit for "storage," or purpos-
es which never require persons to remain
in them over a few minutes at a time. And
every intelligent and humane parent will
arrange that the family-room and the cham-
bers shall be the most commodious, lightest
and brightest apartments in his dwelling.
—Hall's Journal of Health.


THERE is seldom a line of glory written
upon a man's face but a line of suffering
runs parallel with it; and they that read the
lustrous syllables of the one, and stop not
to decipher the spotted and worn inscription
of the other, get the lesser half of the lesson
earth has to give.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


', '[WomansExponent | Vol3 | 1874 | February | Issue: 17 | Title: HYGIENIC NOTES. | Author: Unknown]

HYGIENIC NOTES.

	TO PREVENT A CHILL.—A sitz bath of
hot water with the feet also in hot water,
continued until a thorough sweat has been
taken. Then get into cold water all over,
or have cold water dashed all over you im-
mediately upon getting out of the hot
bath.
	SLEEPING.—Young children should not
be allowed to sleep in the same bed with
aged people. By physical contact, the vital-
ity is absorbed out of the one body into the
other, producing sometimes the most alarm-
effects to the child. No child should be put
to sleep with a person whose health is in
any way impaired.
	NEUBALGIA.—This painful disease has
been cured instantaneously by sponging the
affected part first in water as warm as could
be comfortably borne, and immediately af-
ter in cold water, and then rubbing hard
and briskly with a coarse towel. Rheumat-
ic pains have been repeatedly removed in
the same manner. This treatment will also
remove the inconvenience of cold feet.
	REMEDIES FOR COUGH.—Tea made from
slippery-elm. Or a pint of milk with a lit-
tle coal thrown in, simmered to half a pint.
An excellent syrup is made by simmering
together for half an hour, a half pint of mo-


lasses, the same of vinegar and a quarter
of a pund of raisins when cold a few drops
of laudanum may be stirred in.
	WASHING THE BLOOD.—The Journal of
Health says that the blood can as well be
washed as any other portion of the human
system; but to drink tea, coffee, chocolate,
cocoa, beer or cider does not tend to purify
the blood. On the contrary, such infusions
befoul and defile the blood. But by drink-
ing pure water often, but in small quanti-
ties, the entire inner system, including the
blood, may be thoroughly washed between
Saturday evening and Monday morning,
after which the outside skin should be well
washed.
	INCIPIENT CONSUMTION.—Dr. James C.
Jackson says, in the case of incipient con-
sumption, the patient should eat unleavened
bread, or unbolted wheat flour in some sim-
ple form of preparation. If milk is eaten it
should be skimmed.  Little sugar and no
salt should be used.  No tea, coffee or con-
diments, nor any kind of medicines should
be taken. A sitz bath three times a week
and a wash-off over the whole body the
other three days should be taken, the first
in a comfortably warm room, the latter in a
warm room. An abdominal bandage should
be worn. House labor, especially kitchen
work should be avoided, and pleasant ex-
ercise taken in the open air. Excitement
of all kinds should be avoided, and plenty
of sleep secured.


', '[WomansExponent | Vol12 | 1883 | March | Issue: 20 | Title: SOME INTERESTING FACTS. | Author: DR. E. S. BARNEY]

SOME INTERESTING FACTS.

BY DR. E. S. BARNEY.

	Bathing may be considered from a two fold
point of view; as hygiene, or as a means of
preserving health, by keeping the skin in a
state fit for performing its functions, and as a
therapeutic agent of great power, or for the
cure of diseases.
	In a general sense it means the immersion of
the body in a fluid different in kind or temper-
ature from the common air. Water is the or-
dinary medium employed for baths, but vapor
and heated air are likewise in use for the pur-
pose.
	The great use of bathing must be obvious
when we consider that the skin is not to be re-
garded as a mere covering of the body, but as
an organ, the healthy condition of which is of
vast importance to the well being of the whole
frame, but especially of the stomach and liv-
ing membrane of the lungs, with which as mu-
cous membrane, it has the closest sympathy;
in which, if chronic inflamation exists, a cold
bath should not be used. When we consider
the anatomy of the skin, that in each papilla
of the skin is located the terminal extremity of
a nerve, then we can realize why a restless
child can so quietly sleep after the soothing ef-
fect of a short warm morning both; and a
tired, fevered man after a hard days work can
arise in the morning so refreshed and invigora-
ted, having had a cool or warm bath the night
before; it lowered the temperature, removed
the soreness of the flesh, the irritableness of
the nerves.
	The temperature of the human frame, in a
state of health, continues nearly the same in
whatever circnmstance it is placed. The tem-
perature of an adult is generally from 97° to
98 1\2°; the latter is called normal. In some
cases of disease it is above this standard, in
other conditions it sinks far below. The func-
tion by which the body is enabled to preserve
this uniformity of temperature are intimately
connected with the nervous system, and the
respiratory organs by which heat is developed.
The skin performs the function of regulating
it, especially in reducing it when too high.
When the body is placed in a medium, or low-
er temperature than itself, the heat is abstract-
ed more or less rapidly from the surface, which
would have the effect of chilling it were not
this counteracted by the internal development.
(Should never use cold baths at night after the
fatigue of the day, but warm water instead, as
the frame then requires soothing.) When sur-
rounded by an aëriform medium much higher
than itself, the exhalation from the surface,
both of the skin and lungs are greatly aug-

[Column 2]

mented; that from the skin being thrown off in
sensible and insensible prespiration, that from
the lungs in the form of vapor, the evapora-
tion attending these processes occasion a reduc-
tion of temperature. The quantity of water
thus disposed of in twenty-four hours is esti-
mated at 1 1\2 pints. The skin is capable of re-
moving, when acting in harmony with the laws
of life, in the same length of time, by the esti-
mate of Lavoisier, 20 oz. of waste; the reten-
tion of this from unchanged clothing and other
causes results in injury to the system; if this
channel is neglected it becomes dry and harsh
and unfit for its functions; some other organs
must then supply its place; as the kidneys, but
this organ may have too much to do, thus
working for both, and may become diseased;
in fact neglect of cleanliness is the most fertile
source of disease, both of the skin itself and
other parts. Cleanliness in our habitation is
as necessary to health as personal cleanliness.
Some people are too indolent to bathe at all,
and some at different times of the day before
and after breakfast, another will persist in tak-
ing a cold shower bath before breakfast; it may
thus invigorate or debilitate. There is a pro-
cess in the system which is called the excreting
process; now if this is deficient in its action and
a retention in the system from closed pores,
and a glued substance over those pores, then
the health becomes impaired. Remove the
scales of the dead epidermis and the effete ma-
terial and prevent its absorption, and keep the
pores open, especially in fever; then apply oil
to soften the skin and allay irritation of the
nerves and aid nutrition by absorption.
	The sea ebbs and flows twice a day, but the
great river of life ebbs and flows once in twen-
ty-four hours, attaining its maximum height at
12 m., and its minumum at midnight, hence,
at 11 a.m. is a good time to take a bath before
dinner. 
	How often should a healthy person bathe?
Twice a week in winter months, and every
other day in summer months. Young child-
ren breathe but imperfectly through the lungs,
hence, depend on the skin for a portion of the
arëation of the blood, and they should be
bathed every day in warm water, in a warm
room, and not exposed to cold, always using the
thermometer, for if too hot water is used it wiil
be very depressing. Delicate, feeble persons
of all ages require a higher temperature of a
bath, and a shorter stay in it than others, as
85° or 90°. Baths, when properly conducted,
are a luxury, and a child will crave it and
leap from its mother's lap with joy on its ap-
proach; it only injures them by improper appli-
cation; hence, children should be accompanied
in their baths with an adult, in order to regu.
late the manner of their bath. From the child
that has his face and neck washed every morn-
ing in cold water you seldom hear a complaint;
But a person wearing the "boa," and is doubly
wrapped is constantly exposed. View the sai-
lor, who accustoms himself to an open collar,
his skin is prepared to resist exposure of the
open sea. Nothing is better to harden and
toughen the tender skin than water and air;
this fact being recently considered, the surgeon
does not hasten to wrap, but exposes the ex-
tremity of an amputated limb. The cold bath,
when used by a person in health, increases the
tone of the habit, strengthens the digestive or-
gans, and by diminishing the sensibility of the
whole system, especially of the skin, ren-
ders it less susceptible to atmospheric impres-


[Column 1]

sions from cold, wet and sudden changes of
temperature—thus contributing to what is
termed a robust, or athletic constitution;
hence, the beneficial effects of using a cold bath
daily, and that in the early morning when just
out of bed by surgeons, and those exposed to
frequent changes of climate. The coldness
and length of the bath should be used accord-
ing to the vigor, and debility, and age of the
person. When the shock, as a stimulus to the
nervous system in case of debility, is the prin-
ciple desired, the water should be very cold,
and the time of remaining in the bath only mo-
mentary; the water may be even dashed on,
and immediately and thoroughly dried to bring
on the reaction; by reaction I mean a sensation
of warmth, after the cold of the first immer-
sion. The fluids are repelled from the surface
to the internal organs, which are thus stimula-
ted and roused to action, in consequence of
which the blood is propelled through the whole
capillary system, more particularly of the sur-
face, carrying with it life and renovated ener-
gy. At first the skin shrinks, becomes cooler
and paler, and the respirations are more hur-
ried than before; but this is almost immediately
succeeded by an equally universal sensation of
warmth, or a reaction; if the water is left be-
fore a second attack of cold or chill the pulse
becomes fuller and stronger than before immer-
sion. The skin recovers the plumpness it has
lost, and is succeeded by a feeling of buoyancy,
and an increased animal power. No second
glow ever succeeds the second chill, above men-
tioned, but the debility increases with the
length of immersion.
	A cold duck is sometimes used to sluggish
ulcers.
	If to obviate the shock instantly applied to
the body, and the object is to obtain a refresh-
ing action as in febrile diseases, and inflammation,
water should be applied at first, not suddenly,
only cool and with a sponge, repeating the ap-
plication till the desired effect is produced.
	The amount of reaction depends on the cold-
ness and length of time. One says that a cold
bath is stimulating; another a sedative, or even
debilitating; so it may be, but the truth is, it is
the mode of using it; whether salt or fresh, hot
or cold, and the condition of the patient, the
time of a bath should not be more than ten or
twenty minutes. After a cold bath there
should be a reaction, if not it is injurious; if
the strength of the patient is exhausted then
it would be debilitating; while to another, in
full vigor, it would be a tonic in the highest de-
gree, and this is in proportion to the vigor of
the circulation on the surface. With feeble
circulation and cold extremities, should not
use the cold bath, unless improving the circula-
tion and warmth, before entering by an inter-
nal remedy or exercise. The whole effect of a
bath may be distinguished into, 1st, the shock,
the effect of which is stimulating; 2nd, the cool-
ing effect; 3rd, the counteracting or astringent
effect; 4th, the reaction.
	Cold bathing may be applied so as to insure
one or the other of these actions over any of
the rest, according to circumstances, when all
are not desired. The most important circum-
stance respecting a bath of any kind is its tem-
perature.
	Cold, sea bathing is a sedative, an indirect
tonic. The warm bath is a relaxant. The hot
bath a stimulant.
	Cold bath is from 33° to 60°; cool, from 60°
to 70°; temperate, 75° to 85°; tepid, 85° to 92°;
warm, 92° to 98°; hot, 98° to 112°; vapor, 100°
to 130°, and upwards. Tepid bath, used for
invalids and children, and for cleanliness, 20
or 30 minutes. Warm baths are slightly stim-
ulating, then soothing, and allay irritation of
the nerves, mends the pulse and appetite. Hot
baths are a powerful stimulant and one should
never remain in till exhausted; 5 or 10 minutes
arouses the nerves and general vital action.

[Column 2]

	Time of a bath for young children, 3 min-
utes. A child at 20 weeks, the temperature
should be above blood heat, 98 1/2°, or not less
than 92°. At 10 months, 90°. At 8 or 10
years, not below 78°, which is considered sum-
mer heat. In a high fever a person can be lift-
ed in a sheet and laid in a cold bath at 90°,
and cooled down to 68°, or about 20° less than
when placed in; it then produces the cooling ef-
fect instead of the shock, which might produce
too great a reaction for a debilitated person if
at once placed into the cold. Water can be
sprinkled on them from a watering pot, while
they are lying on an oil cloth, and hot bottles
can be placed at the feet at the same time. If
heat is abstracted from the body too rapidly it
may cause an exhausting effect on the part of
the system to keep up the temperature.
	One German professor said, when the pa-
tient's temperature was 104° to put them into a
bath of 94° and reduce it to 68°. Another
said, put them in 10 minutes, at 68°, and re-
peat it so as to keep down the temperature.
	In Vienna hospitals they put a patient into a
bath of 65° for 20 or 30 minutes, and repeat it
seven or eight times in twenty-four hours, and
it was reported that it was a very successful
treatment. If there is slight chilliness felt the
patient must be moved immediately to a warm
bed. Some of the hospitals in Germany put
their patients into a bath and let them stay so
long that the skin is fairly parboiled in appear-
ance. Rain water is nice for a bath, or a little
soda added to our common running water for
a soft bath. Bathing to prevent disease as a
medicine, it can be used as a tonic, a cool spray
regularly applied so as to produce the tonic ef-
fect. Heat, suddenly applied, will cause mus-
cular contraction. A nerve in a muscle dip-
ped in water 110° will contract violently.
Baths, as means of administering medicine, by
placing in the bath such substances as will
stimulate the skin. As an antipyretic cold
bath, vinegar has a good effect. Friction baths
can be used wet or dry—dilute alcohol, ammo-
nia, a little red pepper, either will be accept-
able. Keep a bottle filled with alcohol, sprin-
kle a little on crash, made in the form of
gloves, and with them rub the body, one's self
or by assistant. Some patients are chronic, or
aenemic, and too weak to produce a glow by
friction, then it must be done for them; for if
the glow is not produced after a bath it does
harm. Massage has proven to be of great val-
ue, sometimes, in cases of dyspepsia; the pa-
tient needs active action. Formerly cold baths
in lung diseases and typhoid fever, were contra
indicated, but not now considered so; yet in
case of ulceration of the bowels in typhoid fe-
ver, the moving of a patient in bathing might
cause perforation and serious results, and a pa-
tient being low, then the shock might paralyze
the heart, and death, or the lungs might be-
come engarged by a cold bath, and death take
place. Immersion in cold water to a delicate
person is not without possible injurious effects,
and may be followed by depression. A deli-
cate person should take a good breakfast an
hour or two before bathing. Care should be
taken in this as in all other therapeutic agents.
Wine in some cases can be given, as it relaxes
the capillaries of the surfaces, and while the cold
bathe drives the blood internally and contracts
the capillaries, the wine may benefit in this
case by dilating the capillaries, and thus invit-
ing the blood to the surface.
	In pneumonia and pleurisy the Germans use
cold compresses. An acid bath is used in some
Heptaic (liver) disease, 3 pints of muriatic acid
and 2 pints of nitric acid; use 1/2 teacupful to 2
gallons of water. Mercurial baths are good in
some cutaneous diseases. Electric baths are
good in paralysis; they excite or stimulate all
the functions, especially the circulation and
secretion. Iodine baths to produce a aubefaci-
ent, sometimes even to the pealing off of the

[Column 3]

skin, 3 quarts of Iodine, and twice as much
of Iodite of Potash, to 1 gallon of water. 
Vapor or Russian baths, water is placed over
a hot stone, and a patient will pass from them
to a cool or cold bath with pleasure. Used in
rheumatism and cutaneous affections, or a hot
duck bath of 110°, in rheumatism is used.
Water baths are used as an eliminative in dis-
eases of the kidneys; nothing removes the
watery secretions more effectual than the Tur-
kish bath, used with care, nothing is better, un-
less it is a Gabaranda sweat. A Turkish bath
is where there is a gradual increase of temper-
ature, after which the patient undergoes sham-
pooning.
	The skin is to the man what the foliage is to
the tree; a vast, external lung, with millions of
pores, of glandules and of papillae, and an im-
mense net-work of arteries and nerves. It is
the complement and supplement of the internal
lungs. With the aid of the Turkish bath the
skin breathes and transpires in place of the
lungs. There is an analogy between the skin
and mucous membrane, as the internal skin is
a continuation of the external, at the continu-
ity of which the epidermis is changed to epith-
elium. The external skin, modified by the
Turkish bath, becomes the modifier of the in-
ternal skin; hence a bath modifies and im-
proves digestion.
	Sweaty hands and feet are improved by
bathing them in cold water and then rubbing
them with a small quantity of the Tincture of
Belladonna. Cold feet are inproved by bath-
ing them in cold water, then apply rapid fric-
tion.
	Cold Sit Baths are in some complaints brac-
ing or tonic, twenty minutes at a time. If on
taking cold there may be Uremie convulsions,
sometimes just before death, where crystals
form on the body. A man had these crystals
form on his mustache as fast as wiped away, he
had convulsions and died in a few hours.
Baths, then, should be used to remove from
the blood urea and uric acid, as they are pois-
onous. In Eczema a person will have oxylu-
ria; whether one is the cause of the other is
not proven, but there is a condition of the skin
that hinders oxydation.
	What of sea bathing? The amount of good
derived from these watering places is principal-
ly from the change of air and scenery, diet,
etc, more than the bath. The exposure to cold
and the unsuited temperature of the water, and
the long remaining in the water, and that, too, 
sometimes when the body is over heated, is
positively injurious. Sometimes it has result-
ed in congestion of the internal organs, cramps
and spasms of some of the muscular organs, or
the muscles themselves, and in some cases pa-
ralysis of a greater or less portion of the body,
and the rays of the hot sun on the head while
in the water is also deleterious, sometimes pro-
ducing what is called sun stroke, and this may
result in paralysis. On the whole more harm
results from sea bathing than good, and young
people come in contact in these places with
a degree of immorality and vice. Sea bathing
is more beneficial where the water is shallow,
and has become warmed by the rays of the sun.
	A person bathing all over is less liable to
take cold than when only a portion of the body
is bathed. A sponge bath and a shower bath
are similar in effect. In no disease is a cold
bath more beneficial than in the intervals of
asthma. The chest may be sponged daily with
water, or vinegar and water; with friction it is
also good for common cough—but not suited
to those who have a tendency to consumption.
Its principal action is a preventative of catarrh
and cold. The best time for a spray is on get-
ting out of bed, when there is no perspiration;
it increases the appetite and digestion. Spong-
ing is more proper for delicate persons than a
shower bath, its effect is nearly similar. Ex-
clude the cold air.

['[WomansExponent | Vol15 | 1886 | January | Issue: 16 | Title: WOMAN'S VOICE. | Author: SARAH SUDWEEKS]

WOMAN'S VOICE.

	I would like to place a few of my thoughts on
paper, peradventure it should meet the thoughts
of some one else.
	In the year of 1852 I received the Gospel of
Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, knowing that it
was the power of God unto salvation to all those
that will believe and obey the same in all things,
as fast as it is made known to them. It was on
the 6th day of June that I went down into the
waters of baptism; I then met with many a conflict;
it was then that I upheld the principle of plural
marriage. I did not know that plural mar-
riage was in the Church at that time, but I said
that all good men in olden times had more wives
than one, and why not now? I thought it was a
good principle; it has always looked right to me,
and I have tried to live in accordance with the
same, and live according to the dictates of con-
science, trusting in the God of our salvation for
the outcome. Light and truth must be carried
unto all the world, that every ear may hear the
sound thereof, and the world left without an excuse,
and to clear the way before the consummation of
all things, which is fast hastening on.
	Every principle in its proper place, inasmuch as
the Gospel requires it—faith and repentance, and
obedience to every principle and commandment
that pertains to the salvation and exaltation of all
the human family that dwell on the earth, inas-
much as they will obey the same, and live accord-
ing to these requirements. I know that the
principle of plural marriage is a righteous principle.
I upheld it before I ever heard it was practiced in
this dispensation. It is upheld in the Bible so
plainly among the patriarchal order of families, as
they were acknowledged by the Father of the
Faithful in that day. The Scriptures speak of
faithful Abraham, and represent him as the
"Father of the Faithful." He did all things that
the Lord required at his hands, and it was ac-
counted to him for righteousness, and his children
followed after him. It was said that "I am the
God of Abraham, Isaac and Jacob." They were
called Abram and Sarai, (Genesis, xvi, 3). "And
Sarai, Abram's wife, took Hagar, her maid, the
Egyptian, after Abram had dwelt ten years in the


[Column 1]

land of Canaan, and gave her to her husband
Abram to be his wife." (xvii, 5). "Neither shall
thy name any more be called Abram, but thy
name shall be Abraham, for a father of many na-
tions  have I made thee; and God said unto
Abraham, "And as for thy wife, thou shalt not call
her name Sarai, but Sarah shall her name be."
Now, you see that the Lord visited them and
talked to them and ate with them and changed
their names. The Lord did not forsake Abraham
and Sarah because he (Abraham) had two wives,
but He blessed him, and told him that his posterity
should be as numerous as the sands on the sea
shore, or the stars in the firmament, that they
could not be numbered. Now Abraham took
Keturah for a wife to enlarge their families, that they
might increase according to the blessing that was
promised him. If we expect to have the blessings
of Abraham, we must do the works of Abraham,
for Abraham, in the Scriptures, is called the
"Father of the Faithful," of which I want to be
one.
	PLURAL MARRIAGE was an ancient order,
though men ignore it now. It was when the Lord
visited His servants the prophets. Men say that
we have no need for the Lord to talk with us, for
such things are done away now; but if He will
not speak to us there must be something wrong; it
must be that we are in the wrong, not the Lord
but I would like to be as good as the ancient peo-
ple of God, and be worthy to go where they
are, I would like to talk with them and enjoy my-
self with them, for I expect to know them and en-
joy their company, which we cannot do if they
will not speak to us, or if we are not worthy to be
spoken to; nor can we learn of their ways or
walk in their paths, that we may be like them. I
would rather be taught of the ways of the Lord
and walk in His paths, let them be ever so thorny,
for without trials what are we? Not worthy of
His notice. I could have no joy if I knew no
God and no hereafter. How could we advance in
the things of the kingdom?
	 I rejoice in knowing that there is a future state,
with all of its organizations, and I realize that I
am responsible for the knowledge that He has
given and blessed me with. Inasmuch as baptism
is necessary, so is the laying on of hands for the
gift of the Holy Ghost, and all the rest of the
ordinances of the Gospel. Plural marriage is as
necessary as any other principle of the Gospel.
If I did not believe in practising plural marriage,
I should not think that I was worthy of any, for
if one principle is wrong the others are wrong also.
How would we feel if we should pass behind the
vail and see those who were not allowed any
privilege to speak to us? I should not expect the
privilege of their society. We must do the work
that will claim the same blessings, then. Where
are all the faithful Prophets and Patriarchs of old,
as well as modern? I want to be worthy to meet
them and enjoy their company; I love them, and
the words that they have taught. We can go to
the Bible and read the words of Abraham, that
good man—he said that he married his sister.
(Gen. xxii, 12). "And yet, indeed, she is my sister.
She is the daughter of my father, but not the
daughter of my mother, and she became my wife."
Now this goes to prove that his father practised
plural marriage also. Abraham, whom the Lord
especially favored, had more wives, and his grand-
son is the same Jacob that, when traveling on his
journey, (Gen. xxviii, 11), "lighted on a certain
place, and tarried there all night, because the sun
was set; and he took of the stones of that place
and put them for his pillow, and lay down in that
place to sleep." (12). "And he dreamed: Behold,
a ladder set upon the earth, and the top of it
reached to heaven, and behold the angels of God
ascending and descending on it." (13). "And, be-
hold, the Lord stood above it, and said, 'I am the
Lord God of Abraham, thy father, and the God of
Isaac; the land whereon thou liest, to thee will I

[Column 2]

give it, and to thy seed." (15). "And thy seed
shall be as the dust of the earth, and thou shalt
spread abroad, to the west, and to the east, and to
the north, and to the south, and in thee and in thy
seed shall all the families of the earth be
blessed."
	Do we belong to the families of the earth?
Mark, He said that all the families of the earth
should be blessed. Now, where is one that it does
not reach? Have we not all sprung from the
patriarchal families, and how can we expect to get
along without obedience to the same law? There
is no blessing promised without obedience. Jacob
worked seven years for Rachel, and was deceived
and got Leah, and then he worked another seven
years for Rachel, and Leah and Rachel each had a
handmaid, and in time they gave each of them to
their husband for wives. This shows to me that
they loved their husband. Jacob, whom the Lord
named Israel, was the father of twelve sons, one
of them was called a dreamer, and his brethren
sold him. He was taken to Egypt, and he became
a savior to all his father's household, and had do-
minion over all things, and all Canaan, where the
famine reached them; and how many of us are of
the seed of Joseph, through the loins of Ephraim,
on whom greater blessings were conferred under
he hands of his grandfather, whom the Lord
talked with, and said his name should be called
Israel from this time forth.
	Moses was conversant with the Lord, and he
was the great law-giver of Israel. In his laws
especial provisions were made for polygamists; it
is not mentioned as one of the crimes for which
penalties were provided.
	Elkanah was a polygamist, yet his son Samuel
was a great prophet and judge in Israel. He was
born and lived under the special favor of God.
	David, king of Israel, was chosen of the Lord.
(1 Samuel, xvi, 12-13). "He took Abigail and
Ahinoam, and they were also both of them his
wives." 1 Samuel, xxv, 72-73. "He took him
more concubines and wives out of Jerusalem."
(2 Samuel, v, 13), we are further informed that,
"David did that which was right in the eyes of the
Lord, and turned not aside from anything
that he commanded him all the days of his
life, save only in the matter of Uriah, the Hittite."
(1 Kings, xv, 5). In this passage we have an as-
surance that David did right in taking all his wives
and concubines except in one instance, for which
he was severely chastised. When Nathan, the
prophet, reproved him for this sin, he said to him,
"In the name of the Lord I gave thee thy master's
house, and thy master's wives unto they bosom."
(2 Samuel, xii, 8).
	I might quote the passages from the beginning
of the Bible to the end thereof, and all would prove
these things, the purposes of the Lord, which are
one eternal round. Search the Scriptures; in
them ye think ye have eternal life, but they are
they that testify of these things. It has not for-
bidden these things for which many have to endure
the penalty of a broken law, and imprisonment
without a just cause, and with a conscience void
of offense toward the laws of man. God has com-
manded and we must obey. In this life only can
we prepare for the next existence, and because
we try to keep the commandments of God, our
fathers, husbands, brothers and sons are placed
before the tribunal and unjustly condemned, and
doomed to stay in prison for months or years, and
they will and do submit for conscience' sake. We
have endured many things, and hope to endure all
things. We feel sorry for our persecutors; they
know not what they do; they are measuring arms
with the Supreme Being, who has said He would
not be mocked nor hold them guiltless that oppress
His Saints. He has said that offenses must come,
but woe! unto them by whom they come. Jesus
said, "Judge not, lest ye be judged, condemn not,
lest ye be condemned." He said, "Let him that

[Column 3]

s without sin cast the first stone at her, and they
all fled."
	We have no hardness in our hearts toward our
oppressors; their lot is hard; they will condemn
themselves in that day when we all shall meet at
the bar of God; where none can say that God is
not just. We pity them, and say, "Oh Lord! have
mercy on them, for they know not what they do."
We fear not, for the Lord is at the helm of the
ship Zion, and He can steer it through the storm
that rages wild and fierce; we have nothing to fear
if we are faithful to our God. We must be tried
and proven until we are like gold seven times
tried in the furnace, and the dross is all con-
sumed.
		SARAH SUDWEEKS.
	Junction, Piute Co., Dec. 29, 1886.
