		ALPHEUS C. LOCKE.

	ST. GEORGE, Utah, June, 1879.
TO MR. AND MRS. ALPHEUS C. LOCKE:
	My Dear Bro. and Sister:—Your letter,
bearing date of January 2nd, did not reach
me until February 9th; I cannot account for
the delay. The mail ordinarily reaches here
from the east in twelve days. I will make no
excuse for my own delinquences, but will now
proceed to answer though for lack of oppor-
tunity am not skilled as yourself in the use of
language. But I shall presume upon the many
years of my past experience of the kindness of

[Column 3]

both you and yours, to believe you will over-
look all such imperfections as may appear, and
see only my main object, which is to elucidate
truth.
	First of all, you have my thanks for the
just compliments you pay to the "Mormon
women;" but why not raise these same "noble
women" fully up to your standard of refine-
ment? Am glad, however, to see the one
whom you have made brought so fairly to the
front; but will you please tell me where you
got the pattern? Can she be a daughter of
Abraham? If so, you have painted her in
different colors from the way she looks as God
made her, as I view her; the feature of selfish-
ness is far too prominent to fairly represent
the model which He has placed in the fore-
ground among the "holy women," where Peter
points at her as a beacon to light up the path-
way of virtue and refinement for every pure
woman in all coming generations. This is the
model I choose to pattern after, and such are
the women who have assembled in mass meet-
ings in every settlement in the Territory to
declare the sentiments of the main body.
	You seem to be in doubt as to whether every
plural wife is "satisfied and happy." My dear
brother, can you assure me that every mono-
gamic wife lives in the enjoyment of perfect
felicity? Again, if the class of "mockers" had
only passed away with Ishmael (which, unfor-
tunately for me, they did not) I should now
have less cause to pity myself when I think
how, as a child, I was subject to such usage,
and also to a sly pummeling, which came in
seasonably to give variety to the exercises.
	It is possible you may think these childish
bickerings of eight brothers and sisters were a
peculiarity of the family to which I belonged,
and not at all common in monogamic families
generally. However this may be, I will not
stop here to determine, but will surely say that
my experience goes to show that the practice is
not wholly confined to the children of plural
wives. Now, in this connection we will look
at the main question. as you have put it "Is
this Mormon view of domestic life the correct
one?"
	You do not believe in new revelation, so, if
you will not run away from it, I will try and
meet you on your own ground (the Bible) not
only in this regard but also on some points,
which I will hereafter bring to your notice.
	In the first place, then, is not marriage a
divine institution? If so, is it not recorded as
a part of that Scripture which is given by
inspiration of God and pronounced profitable
for correction and instruction in righteousness,
that the man of God, not the world, may be
perfect—thoroughly furnished to all good
works? If so, can he be thoroughly furnished
to all good works and be perfect? (mind you,
I do not say he cannot be saved in some condi-
tion) without observing this institution. You
will certainly acknowledge that, according to
the obedience to God's laws so will the reward
be. Does not God also command this same
man to multiply? And was this law or com-
mandment ever abrogated? If the Lord ever
did go back on His word in this regard, will
you please show me the record? Yes, say you,
Paul says, "I would that all men were as I 
myself." Now, Paul was unmarried, and so
it follows, then, that if that apostle spoke all
things by inspiration, there should have been
no marriages since his day; But Jesus says,
quoting from yours, "Occupy till I come." Of
course the men of that day could say in reply,
"My Lord, how can we occupy till then, seeing
we shall all be dead?" Jesus says again. "Feed
my lambs." Is not this bidding them to gener-
ate, the result of which would be the perpetuity
of the race of mankind; but here comes Paul
again, and says, in substance, I have been
talking with God, about this matter, and He
tells me (or I think I have His Spirit dictating


[Column 1]
what I say) that you must all stop generating
and marrying; of course, which will enable
you to dispense with the "feeding of lambs,"
and He has also concluded that, after all, it is
of no consequence whether any are found
"occupying" or not; at least, brother, would
not this state of things be the result of Paul's
teachings if carried out? But Paul was a
man, and, when not speaking by inspiration,
appears as liable to mistakes as some other good
people, for that he was merely uttering his own
opinion in connection with the "shortness of
time," appears plain to be seen.
	But God, who saw the end from the begin-
ning, has frustrated the designs of Paul in
this matter, by placing in the make-up of hu-
man nature a disposition to carry out His own
purposes of creation.
	I will not, however, do you the wrong to
suppose that you, with your imaginary faith in
Paul's theory of First Cor., 7-29, ever carried
it into practice, for how can I, as the sequel
has shown?
	If I understand you aright, you place the
teachings of Paul and those of Joseph Smith
in contra-distinction to one another; and well
you may, for while Paul is teaching merely his
human opinion, Joseph Smith is teaching the
revealed will of God, both old and new. Now
Joseph ceased to live in the year 1844; he
suffered the same fate of most other prophets,
being murdered by the hands of wicked men;
but I suppose the period including the "short
time" covers at least part of the ground since
that date, and yet during this same time you
have, if I mistake not, taken to your own fold
two of Christ's sheep and several lambs, which
fact makes it look to me as if the rope with
which you have attempted to hang the Mor-
mons had somehow slipped around your own
neck; but you are not the first or only one who
has professed to adopt Paul's theory, for in
1854 some of the good advent brethren were
so exercised thereon that in Lynne a special
meeting was one night appointed after nine
o'clock, where I happened to be present and
heard the people talk Paul till midnight, when
they returned to their homes. Whatever their
conclusions were, I never knew, but the cir-
cumstance was in due time followed by an in-
crease of family among the married, much to
the chargrin of one, Bro. Lyford and wife, of
Salem, who on the discovery in their own case,
immediately isolated themselves by removing
to Danvers, because of their dread of meeting
with the holy indignation of their friend and
brother in the faith, N. Florentine, who, as
shortly appeared, was in the same predicament.
In fact, I have yet to see the person who has
carried out Paul's theory fully since 1843 on
account of the "shortness of time"
	But further, you say that the practice of
plurality was never pronounced a duty or means
of salvation for either sex. This expression
of yours serves to again remind me that you
are not a believer in Joseph Smith as a
prophet, or in the divinity of the Book of
Mormon, but you say you do accept the Old
Testament prophets of God, and for this I am
glad, because we can together listen and hear
what some of them have to say on that score.
We will begin with Moses. Did not he declare
that if a man's brother die, leaving no children,
the living brother should take the widow and
raise up seed to his dead brother. Now, sup-
posing the living brother already has a wife,
can he in that case refuse to obey the one man
who was appointed by God as the great law-
giver? And what if the widow should refuse
to accede to those terms, could they both be
saved all the same as if they had been obedient
to the command of God as given by Moses?
Even had they obeyed every other and still
have, continued to live in violation of this
single one, would they not have been pro-
nounced guilty of all? And supposing such 
were the case, would not the salvation of each

[Column 2]
have depended, at least in this instance, on
their marrying? for sin, we must remember,
is the transgression of the revealed will of
God; and suppose you not that when Jesus
came He found the Jews living in plurality?
and yet we hear not a word from Him against
it; on the contrary, He even forbids divorce,
except  adultery, which shows that more
than one wife was lawful in His day. So your
hint concerning things which were "permitted
under the law, having been discontinued under
the Gospel," does not seem to include poly-
gamy; indeed, I marvel, my dear brother, that
one so well informed as yourself should so
lightly esteem one of the greatest blessings
ever designed for the human race by an alwise
Creator.
	To be sure your crusade appears chiefly
against polygamy, for you ask "how a man filled
with the Spirit of God could desire more than
one wife?"
	Perhaps that question can be better answered
by one of your own sex, as I will bye and bye
attempt to show you. Being myself a woman
you will excuse me if my sympathies in this
matter are entirely on her side, for tell me,
now, is not the system of plurality a fine thing
for women? It is in my opinion only a
false education that prevents a responsive "yes"
from the majority of women. Besides utility
in more than one respect to them--how other-
wise can "every woman have her own husband?"
for right in your own State of Mass. there are
seventy thousand more females than males (see
Dr. Lewis, in "Our Girls," page 150.) It ap-
pears to me that throughout the world general-
ly the females out number the males.
	You probably remember the record in the
second book of Samuel 12th chapter, and 8th
verse, where God by the mouth of the Prophet
Nathan, gave a number of the wives of Saul
into the bosom of David, who, as you will see,
must have had the Spirit of God at that time,
he being a "man after God's own heart,"
and yet God, whom you will allow to be a
wiser judge than myself or any man could be,
saw fit to add to the number which this spiritual
minded man, David, already possessed; one
would think in these days that then, this "good
man" ought to have been satisfied, and if he
was not he could have had more had he ob-
tained them lawfully, God's word being the
standard, but he was not to steal another man's
wife, nor shed innocent blood, both of which
crimes he committed, and these were the crimes
for which he suffered; a heavy penalty, but not
for desiring more wives than one. Of course
you must now admit that in more than one
instance a plurality of wives was more than 
permitted.
	Again, you inquire "how you could, with
your present  convictions, surround yourself
with the things of this world in order to in-
crease the number of your wives and family?"
I would also ask if you have not always
done what your law allows you in that line?
Of course you are squinting a little at the
Mormons again; so I will say that being driven
from their homes in a civilized and Christian
country into a desert, where their enemies were
so sure, they would starve to death that they
taunted them with the offer of a thousand
dollars for the first bushel of wheat they would
raise upon the arid soil they lack, in some
instances, many of the comforts and the
beauties of your surroundings--you see their
motive in fleeing here was not to aggrandize
themselves, but to keep the law of God, which
they understand requires of them one tenth of
all their income (whether it be a bushel of 
wheat, or less) to help on His cause, and having
faith in Him more than in all the world, be-
fore the sun set the day they arrived in Salt
Lake Valley they had planted potatoes and
corn, and the next day they "smote the rock"
and brought water from the mountains, which
the Lord had filled, and with which they have

[Column 3]
irrigated their lands and raised the bushel of
wheat (which, like the widow's barrel of meal,
so famous for its never failing qualities) has,
by the blessing of God, not only kept them in-
dependent of their neighbors for bread, but
has also fed some of their enemies. (I would
here remark that they never saw the color of
the money which was offered for the bushel of
wheat, neither did they expect to.) At the
time of the great fire in Chicago, President
Brigham Young sent them $1,000, and the
collections from the Mormons altogether, as I
am informed, amounted to several thousands.
This fact, and also the account of their giving
five hundred to the yellow fever sufferers at
one time last year you may have seen recorded
in the Eastern papers.
	As I was not in Illinois at the time this peo-
ple migrated there, I cannot take oath as to
whether they were warlike or not, but I can
say that in all my intercourse among them I
have seen no such spirit manifested to their
enemies; on the contrary, I have noticed that
in all their preaching and teaching, and in all
their writings a spirit of the utmost forbear-
ance is inculcated.
	May I hope that I shall not weary you while
I say a few words on the subject of the resurrec-
tion.
	It is stated in the New Testament, John 5-
21, that the Father raiseth up the dead and
quickeneth them, which would seem as if no
other person had anything to do with it. But
hark. Even so, just as His Father did it, the
Son quickeneth--who? Everybody? Whom
He will. So we see already two persons en-
gaged in the work, and yet it says again, in
verse 25th, the dead shall hear the voice of
the Son, which is to raise them to life, as if
the Father had nothing whatever to do with it;
but in verse 19, of the same chapter, it says
that even Jesus could do nothing only as He
learned how by first seeing His Father do the
same, and, being the Son, He is heir not only
of His glory, but also of His power, to raise
the dead. Now, Christ, being taught by and
assisting His Father, would it be strange if He
should confer the same power on His younger
brothers, by teaching them how to do the same?
Perhaps you will say this cannot be the design
of the Almighty, or it would be so recorded;
but who can say it has not been? There are a
number of books referred to in Scriptures
which are not preserved between its covers.
This fact is an argument to my mind in favor
of the necessity of new revelation, for how else
can we be instructed in all things which go to
make a man perfect? Certainly the books re-
ferred to were intended for that purpose. Be-
sides, it is stated in those we have that if all
the things which Christ did and said were
written, the world itself could not contain the
books; so that some truths are barely hinted at,
like, for instance, the baptism for the dead, a
work, by the way, which I know no people in
the world to be doing except the Mormons.
	Again it says Christ shall judge the world;
and is it any more marvelous to suppose that
the Saints shall assist in raising the dead than
that they shall judge the world--and thus it is
written.
	I can find as much consolation in the one
idea as the other, believing each to be true,
and both surpassingly glorious.
	You argue that Joseph Smith cannot be a
true prophet, because the Scriptures do not
describe him as such. There is evidence
enough in them to satisfy me, and if I should
write you again I will give you the references;
I fear I may weary you now. and I wish to 
take further notice of some other of your ob-
jections; but I will just remark that I am not
aware that the "young men and maidens" were
not so minutely described as to be recognized
by everyone. Some supposed they were a
parcel of drunken people, instead of the peo-
ple of Joel's prophecy, and how was it wi[---]


Christ? Did every one believe He was the
Son of God? Besides other vile epithets heaped
upon Him, he was called a "gluttonous man
and a wine bibber," and the most pious persons
that could be found got up a crusade against
Him and His followers,and the work of annihi-
lation is still going on now. 
	Your reference to the "Mormon" faith that
Christ had wives—this idea is merely inferen-
tial. You will recollect that at the marriage
in Cana of Gallilee, Jesus was called "the
Bridegroom" The bride is not here mentioned,
but who, I ask, but a wife would, even in these
degenerate days, come publicly and wash a
man’s feet with her tears and wipe them with
the hair of her head? Christ did not seem to
think the marriage ordinance so degrading to
Himself, but what He could sanction it with
His presence. Who was it I wonder, that so
dragged Him down to the level of mankind
as to induce Him to submit to be circumcised
and baptized? Was it not His own choice?
Did He not say it was becoming in Him to
fulfill all righteousness? Well, then, the ordin-
ance of marriage is righteous, or it is not;
Which horn of the dilemma will you take?
Perhaps He respected His Father’s wisdom
and judgment in framing such a law, and con-
sidered it as pure and holy as any other, and
one of the most sacred, and it is only wicked
men, such as many who sit in Congress, who
would seek to break down what God has
joined.
	Such men as I have just described know
little and care less how many hearts may
bleed, or how many homes may be made deso-
late by the breaking up of families in the
separation of husbands and wives, parents
and children.
	Have you ever reflected, my dear brother,
upon the consequences to this people if this
law just made—which is made by ungodly
men—should be carried out? Just think of
more than fifty thousand happy wives sudden-
ly made widows, some of whom have taken me
lovingly to their heart and home. “God setteth
the solitary in families." Believing He placed
me here, is it any wonder that my sympathies
are wholly with the people in this matter?
Knowing your kindness I am sure it must
undergo a great change ere you can look
placidly out from your pleasant surroundings
amid your loved and loving family, and see
those whose hearts are just as tender, their
affections as deep, and homes as dear to them-
selves for the love that is sheltered beneath
each roof, and not feel sorrow and indignation
at a law which brings naught but evil to an
innocent, industrious and happy people.
	Though your religious belief differs from
theirs, I feel assured your influence will not go
to rob them of all that life holds dear. Their
practice of polygamy affects no one who does
not wish it, and is no crime, except as pro-
nounced so by man-made law; God’s law does
not condemn it,but is on their side; so I believe,
and were I thirty years younger. I would test
the system practically; and you should have
experience therein.
	I am very sorry we are obliged to differ so
widely in our religious views; I would like it
better if, like this people, you could, according
to the Scriptures, "Walk by the same rule and
speak the same thing." I am,
		Very truly your friend,
', '[WomansExponent | Vol11 | 1882 | August | Issue: 6 | Title: LETTER ON PLURAL MARRIAGE. | Author: NANCY ARETE CLARK]

LETTER ON PLURAL MARRIAGE.

Miss Esther M. Parker, Greenville, Penn.
	Dear Cousin: Although I so recently
answered your last letter, I trust you will
grant me pardon for thus unceremoniously ad-
dressing you again, but as I only merely
touched upon the course our enemies are pur-
suing against us as a people, I wish to give
you a few of my views on the subject, not as a
retort to the many vile and libelous slanders so
widely circulated about us, but to tell you
truths as I know them. When I see men,
who are, or should be, placed by the voice of
the people at the head of the government, so
far lose sight of their duties as guardians of
the constitutional laws of this great Republic,
as to enact special legislation to deprive a por-
tion of her children (no matter how few) of
the inalienable right to worship God accord-
ing to the dictates of their own consciences
and then seek to rob them of the greatest
boon given to man to protect him in his liberty
as a responsible being, the right of franchise,
I cannot hold my peace. This right was be-
queathed to us by our forefathers who suffered
untold privation, fought, bled and many of
them died for their country's cause, while our
foremothers labored in poverty and distress at
home, bearing the deep mental anguish of
heart that none but true, patriotic wives and
mothers could bear, it kindles the fire of old '76
in my blood, which I am ever proud and happy
to say runs through my veins as uncontamin-
ated, as when in the veins of my veteran father,
who bore arms in the war of 1812. He ever
taught his children that the spirit of our
glorious constitution was heaven-born; and that
God inspired Columbus to the discovery of
this continent, and had reserved this land for
the express purpose that our Pilgrim fathers
and mothers might find an asylum from perse-
cution; but what a fearful digression from the
standard of justice and right that a Washing-
ton and his compeers so devotedly erected,
when clergymen, politicians, and the press all
claim, that they now are taking action against
us because we believe and practice polygamy.
	Now my dear cousin this is not true, al-
though they fain would make it appear so to
the nations abroad, and thus deceive many
otherwise well-meaning people of our own
nation, for well do I remember the time in
the year 1833 when my father and
brothers were hunted day and night by our
enemies, and only saved their lives by conceal-
ment, while my mother with a large family of
little children in connection with many others

[Column 2]
in like condition, was driven into the wilds of
the frontiers by a mob whose faces were paint-
ed as black as Ethiopians—and this at the
point of the bayonet, from Jackson Co., Miss-
ouri, barefooted through the crusted snow cut-
ting our feet until our path was a trail of blood
and however much we may deplore the fact
this mob was led and urged on by a divine,who
a few Sabbaths before had professed to be a
follower of the meek and lowly Jesus. Was
this because of our belief in polygamy? No,
truly no: for the principle of plural marriage
was not then known or even heard of in our
Church. Why then did they become so in-
furiated against us? I know no other reason
than that the angel, which John the revelator,
saw flying in the midst of heaven, having the
everlasting Gospel to preach to them that dwell
on the earth, had come and fulfilled that mis-
sion by appearing to Joseph Smith, the great
modern prophet, and giving him authority to
preach the Gospel as did Jesus and his Apostles
when on the earth; and it come so opposite to
their preconcerted creeds, that they sought to
destroy it, by persecuting the Church of God.
And that same spirit that caused them to do
so then, is now most assuredly urging them on
to deeds of great wrongs.
	As you are aware by my former letter to
you that I am a firm believer in and do sustain
the principle of plural marriage—the celestial
law or higher order of marriage, which not
only unites husbands and wives for time but
for all eternity, which last clause is the crown-
ing point for all. I will explain more fully, if
possible, my reason for so doing, and being a
firm believer in the Bible, I will take that as
my guide. I find by searching its pages where
God said to Abraham the father of the faith-
ful, (in whose bosom all good Christians are
praying to repose) "I will bless them that bless
thee and curse them that curse thee, and in
thee shall all families of the earth be blessed;"
Now this we understand is the promise pertain-
ing to Christ the Redeemer, who should come
and be crucified to atone for the sins of the
world, that as many as would hearken to his
word, might be brought back into the presence
of God; also in Galatians, "Now to Abraham
and to his seed were the promises made," and
He saith not unto seeds as of many, but as of
one, and of thy seed which is Jesus Christ,
and all who read the Bible know that Abraham
had more than one wife. Again I find the
same promise made of Christ that he should
come through the seed of Jacob; a man hav-
ing four wives, and of these plural wives came
the twelve patriarchs, whose names John the
revelator tells us are to be written on the
twelve gates of the holy city, even the new
Jerusalem. In Kings I read that David was
a man after God's own heart, and through his
loins a chosen seed should be raised up even
Jesus Christ the Redeemer. The Apostle Paul
in his day tells the people how the Lord said,
I have found the son of Jesse a man after
mine own heart which shall fulfill all my will,
of this man's seed hath God, according to
promise, raised unto Israel a Savior, Jesus.
Isaiah confirms the same by telling us, in
that day there shall be a root of Jesse which
shall stand as an ensign for the people, to it
shall the Gentiles seek and his rest shall be
glorious. Now I learn from the Bible that
Jesse, the father of David, was the son of Ruth
the plural wife of Boaz and that David his
son had many wives, yet in nothing did he dis-


[Column 1]
please the Lord only in the case of Uriah and
his wife. In Revelations it says one of the
Elders said unto me "weep not behold the lion
of the tribe of Judah hath prevailed to open
the book and loose the seven seals thereof. I
am Alpha and Omega the beginning and the
end; the first and the last. Again he says "I
am the root and the offspring of David the
bright and morning star, which makes it very
plain to my understanding that God had great
respect for those who believed and practised
plural marriage, and indeed preferred that
lineage for his holy son Jesus to come through,
and as he is the root of David, who was a man
after God's own heart, it becomes a most pos-
itive proof to me that Jesus Christ, is the
chief-corner stone and author of this principle;
therefore understanding and believing the
Bible as I do, reason and my own conscience
forces me not only to adopt the principle of
plural marriage in my faith, but I must prac-
tise the same. I also read in John, Chapter 8,
where our blessed Savior says, "if ye were
Abraham's children you would do the works
of Abraham." In Luke he says, there shall
be weeping and gnashing of teeth, when you
shall see Abraham, Isaac and Jacob, and all
the prophets in the kingdom of God, and ye
yourselves cast out. But this is not all, as I
have previously told you by letter. God has
commanded us through Joseph Smith, our
martyred prophet, that we must obey this law,
as did his ancient servants and handmaidens
that we may become one with him, or where
He dwells we cannot come. For us as a people
to ignore or set aside this principle would be
to incur the displeasure of an offended God.
I know those who are calling for special legis-
lation against us, say plural marriage is de-
generating and debasing, but I refer you to
where Jesus said to his disciples, "verily I say
unto you that ye which have followed me in
the regeneration, when the Son of man shall
sit in the throne of his glory, ye also shall sit
upon the twelve thrones judging the twelve
tribes of Israel: Now let me ask what is this
regeneration he speaks of? Is it the mode of
doing away with the patriarchial order of
marrying more wives than one? No, that
cannot be for Jesus says himself, that a house
divided against itself cannot stand. And he
would thereby destroy himself for He was
born of that peculiar lineage; and all mankind
would thus be left without a Redeemer, and
therefore lose their salvation, then of necessity
in order to follow him in the regeneration and
become one with him we must be adopted into
the same lineage, and to the works of Abraham
and those to whom the promise of Christ was
given.
	The anti-polygamists say there are no true
homes where polygamy is practised. What a
strange contradiction in sinful man, praying
Congress to enact laws to tear our family ties
asunder, thereby casting hundreds of faithful,
virtuous wives and their children on a cold un-
feeling world, branding them with disgrace
and illegitimate birth, and at the same time
praying God that when they die he will take
them to Abraham's bosom,—Abraham who
was in every sense of the word a practical
polygamist. "O, consistency thou art a jewel"
It is also asserted by our enemies in Utah that
we are an oppressed people, by priestly domin-
ance and power; and that the women of Utah
are ignorant and down trodden, living in a
state of slavery and serfdom to their husbands
and the priesthood. This, dear cousin, is,
believe me, all false, and those who declare it
know in their hearts it is not true. There may
be homes that are not happy ones, and some
women who are tyrannized over by their hus-
bands. Let me ask you are there not similar
instances in other denominations, both in our
own country and abroad? If not we most
heartily congratulate them. But if there is

[Column 2]
such a condition of affairs near their own fire-
sides we would advise them to give heed to the
words of Jesus; "Pluck the beam out of thine
own eye that thou mayest see clearly to take
the mote out of thy brother's eye (or sister's as
the case may be.) It is also claimed by our
oppressors that the right of franchise for the
women of Utah is monopolized for partisan
purposes. This is also untrue. I have cast
my vote at every election in the proper place
and at the proper time ever since the privilege
was granted in 1870, and I can truthfully de-
clare I have never been instructed in regard to
my choice of candidate, neither have others of
our Church. I have read histories of the
nations from the first period of man's existance
on the earth, to the present time and I fail to
find anywhere on the pages of history, in any
age of the world where women have stood on a
higher plane or broader basis of liberty and
virtue than do the women of Utah, religiously,
socially and politically.
	With the deepest regard for the rights of
humanity, I subscribe myself as ever,
			Your sincere friend,
					NANCY ARETE CLARK.
Farmington, Utah
	Feb, 19, 1882.


']

['[WomansExponent | Vol10 | 1881 | October | Issue: 9 | Title: THE CHILDREN'S FAIR. | Author: Unknown]

THE CHILDREN'S FAIR.

	The Primary Fair of the Associations of Salt
Lake County was held in the Social Hall in this
city, and was open for exhibition five days, com-
mencing Thursday, Sept. 22, and continuing on
the 23d and 24th, and meeting with such excel-
lent success, was re-opened on Tuesday and
Wednesday, the 27th and 28th ult. Mrs. Ellen
C. S. Clawson and her Counselors, Mrs. Camilla
Cobb and Annie D. Watson, spared no pains to
make the affair as complete in every particular
and department as it could be made. The Pres-
idents of the several wards that had articles
from their respective Associations on exhibition
also lent a helping hand, by managing each her
own department, and thus facilitating the heavy
labor of Mrs. Clawson and her associates.

[Column 2]
	It would be utterly useless to attempt to men-
tion all the work of the little folks, and incon-
sistent under the circumstances to give names
or make comparisons, but there was much to be
admired and to arouse an ambition in others to
"go and do likewise."
	Every conceivable article of ornamentation
that could be made by little girls was there to
be seen in one or the other of the several spaces
allotted to the different wards. There were six-
teen quilts, of a variety of patterns, all well
made and nicely quilted. There were one or
two dresses very neatly made by girls about
thirteen years old; doll dresses by those much
younger, and aprons, pillow cases, and a few
other articles of this kind, lots of stockings of
home knitting, also mittens and dolls' stockings;
hoods for children, very handsome, crocheted of
wool, and one very elegant shawl made by a
child twelve years old.
	Almost innumerable mottoes, worked upon
perforated card-board in every form, wool, tarle-
tan and wax flowers in abundance; tidies of
every form and size, and of all available mate-
rials; several specimens of fine pillow lace and
ornamental embroidery, and work of all des-
criptions. Cocoons raised by the little girls, too,
of an excellent quality, which really deserve
special mention. Paintings on silk and wood
were also very creditable indeed.
	Then there was a department for cookery
where the "little women" displayed handsome
cakes, many of them frosted and ornamented,
pies, bread, butter, preseves, jellies, and so forth;
some of these articles were very "fair to look
upon," but as we were not permitted to taste,
one could not judge so well of their quality.
	There were vegetables of almost all kinds
which had been raised by the little boys, pota-
toes, corn, pop corn, turnips, -beets, squashes,
nearly every variety; and pea-nuts which were
really quite a curiosity to many. The boys had
sleds, book shelves, wagons, tiny cradles, and
clothes-horse and other such articles, and a pair
of shoes, the first shoe-making of some industri-
ous boy; there was ornamental plaster work too,
done by little boys, and many other things we
have not space to mention. Mrs. Clawson can-
not help but feel gratified with the success
of the Fair, and it has been so highly appreci-
ated by the people that it has been allotted space
in "The Utah Territorial Fair," to be held in
this city, commencing October 3d.
	The admittance to the Primary Fair was only
10 cts. for adults and 5 cts. for children, yet the
aggregate amount was considerable, and Mrs.
Clawson informs us that after paying the tithing
out of the funds, $50.00 has been donated to the
Logan Temple and the remainder reserved to
appropriate towards prizes for the coming exhibi-
tion another year.
	We would suggest to the children that next
year they exhibtt more needle work, plain and
ornamental, also darning, button-hole mak-
ing, and straw work, and also that
the little girls and boys both write essays
and poems; they will have a year to think
of it and we will offer now a suitable prize,
to any girl or boy under fifteen years of age who
will write the best original poem or essay; and
the decision to be made by five competent dis-
interested critics.
	Several persons have given prizes and render-
ed valuable aid to the commendable work of the
Primary Associations. President John Taylor
gave the use of the building to the Fair, and H.
B. Clawson, Esq., gave much assistance to the
ladies in getting up and carrying out the entire
arrangements.
	We trust the little folks will be encouraged
by the success of their work to persevere in
making and improving in all useful em-

[Column 3]
ployments. Industry is the road to wealth, and
to honor as well, and the children of Zion have
much to do and may they be able to do their
work well is my prayer.
	We tender our thanks to Mrs. Clawson, and
the ladies associated with her, for substantially
remembering the WOMAN'S EXPONENT in dis-
tributing the delicacies left from the Fair.
Many thanks for the same.


', '[WomansExponent | Vol20 | 1891 | July | Issue: 1 | Title: PRIMARY FAIR, SOCIAL HALL. | Author: Unknown]

PRIMARY FAIR, SOCIAL HALL.
	The Primary Fair of this Stake was held
in the Social Hall June 18, 19, and 20th,
under the direction of Ellen C. Clawson and
her Counselors. Several of the wards were
unrepresented, but of the Associations that
were represented there was a very good show-
ing of work. It was utterly impossible to
observe all the nice things even, but among
those that we did notice were of boy’s work,
Johnie H Boberg, Draper 9 years old,ladder,
rake and boot jack, Willie Turner South
Cotton Wood 13 years old, a puzzle rattle
cut in wood, also Geo. C. Brown, of the same
place, ladder and rake. Earl Cummings 13th
Ward this City, 3 brackets, Josiah Midgley 7th
Ward,13, tin bucket, Charlie Spencer 7 years
old, crochet covered ball, Stanley Partridge


[Column 1]

aged 15 years, doll settee and chairs cut wood-
work; Charles Madson 4th Ward, 13 years
old, small lounge made and upholstered; stone
work carving of letters, by Eddie aged 11 and
Elias Ashton aged 10, 15th Ward, Ray Jacobs
17th Ward 10 years old, paper weight and
penholders cut from cigar box and gilded very
tasteful; hearth rugs and woolen patchwork
by Ralph Grames 3rd Ward, aged 9 years,
bedstead for doll, Geo. J. Fox 14th Ward aged
13, a very complete miniature pile driver by
Johnie Bowers 7th Ward, he also melted the
lead and shaped it. Brackets and scroll work
Walter Wallace 7th Ward, 13 years old, Ray
Williams Mill Creek, double harrow 12 years
old, Forestdale in miniature by Alma
Bullock of the 16th Ward, 10 years old was
the most attractive piece of work made by any
of the little boys. It was perfect in ap-
pearance though very small, streets laid out,
shade trees, houses exact even to the sign and
advertising, the proportions were all good it
was made principally of pasteboard and painted
in imitation of Forestdale itself. It was quite
a novel idea. Lee Richards of the 20th ward
had a very handsome satin banner on exhibi-
tion, ornamental painting and letter-
ing, certainly very creditably done and
artistically finished off for a little boy. showing
considerable talent in this direction.
	The work of the little girls was in very
great variety and it is impossible to more than
give a passing notice to a few of the most
uoticable. One quite attractive little thing
was a large bunch of paper snowballs hanging
pendant so true to nature that several of the
visitors thought that they were real, these
were made by Chloe Hunter aged 9 years,
water lilies in waxwork, Annie Gundson aged
12 years, Ardelle Cummings aged 12, had
several specimens of drawn needle work that
were very pretty and quite creditable for a
child. Sophia Youngberg, Draper, aged 14
had some very fine plain hand sewing, work
on ribbon done in arasine very handsome and
fine work, Phronia Nelson Draper. aged 11
years, Gertrude Winder aged 13, handsome
lamp-mat made of ribbon fastened with cord
of chenille, May Gilbert banjo and mandolin
silk work, covered pasteboard, there were
very odd and quite pretty. Rhoda Miller,
Mill Creek, aged 10, babies bibs worked neatly,
Edna Jackson paper platting very nice aged
7, Olive Cushing 11th ward, 7 years old
woolen scarf crocheted. Louie Coulam 11th
ward 9 years old stocking darning very neat.
Sophia Youngberg aged 14, yarn' home spin-
ning, very fine, Evelina Youngberg 7 years old,
very fine darning. Fannie Coulam 11th ward
12 years, old cut fitted and made a dress waist
sewing very good, button holes well made.
Hannah Thatcher 9 years old, cut fitted and
made in goodstyle a doll's dress. Pearl Carlisle,
Mill Creek, 15 years old, large, heavy table cover
embroidered on felt in chenille, arasine,
riberdine. very elaborate in design and quite
elegant as a whole, showing talent, patience,
and neatness. Birdie Carlisle younger sister,
a table scarf handsomely embroidered. Belle
Weiler 4th ward, 13 years old a pretty apron
made by hand trimmed with novelty lace and
insertion done very neatly. Free hand draw-
ing by Alice Clawson 14 years old, these
copies and drawings showed considerable
talent; cross stitching on gingham by little
girls six and seven years old, horse shoe
watch case, covered and filled in with silk
filled and wound round with ribbon. This
was a very attractive article and as it hung
attracted considerable attention. Laura Jones
16th ward, large sofa cushion of silk, pieced
log-cabin pattern. Janette Pitt aged 9 years,
carriage robe, made of woolen pieces in form
of rosettes raised and worked in colors. Ella
Keyting 17 years old elegant silk banner,
ornamental, delicately painted and mounted.

[Column 2]

Table cover from the 17th ward Primary
intended for the Logan Temple, embroidered
in the four corners. Louie Groesbeck 12,
corner marguerites, Cathie Dougall sun-
flowers, Geneva Ellerbeck, poppies; Stella
Jacobs, fuchias, Alice Taylor 17th ward,
10 years old darning, mending, marking,
tucking, in fact of all kinds of needle work,
very good indeed. Ray Grant 13th ward,
handsome handkerchief case made when
eleven years old. Edith Dinwoody 7th ward,
aged 14, painting on China very well executed.
Ray Chamberlain 8th ward, 6 years old,
paper cutting kindergarten work very pretty.
Bessie Frost 8th ward, 13 years old, apron
hand sewing, neatly done, crochet trimming
fine and even. Eva Sheets 8th ward, 14
years old a handsome bunch of artificial
roses. Minnie Oakey, 8th ward, aged 12, 3
owls on a thorny branch very natural attracted
a great deal of notice.
	Bessie Binder [--]th ward, 13 years old, a
child's white dre[--] hand sewing with 23 tucks
all even and ne[--] stitches. Clara Hanson 7
years old croche[-] work: cream colored scrim
apron and croche very odd and pretty, pillow
shams worked in birds and snnflowers crochet
lace very deep, edged with colored floss to
correspond with the embroidery, sofa pillow
and head rest, embroidered with chenille and
arasine, in moss roses and pansies several
specimens of penmanship, and dozens and
scores of other things too numerous to mention.
Machine embroidery a swan in the water
among the rushes was quite pretty; this was
by Lutie Ashton 9 years old. There were
only two essays notwithstanding the call made
through the papers and the offer of a prize.
	There were some very well executed paint-
ings in oil by Kate V. Moris 15 years old, 15th
ward. The best was a copy of Conway
Castle in North Wales; Scene in the Yellow
stone Park painted when she was only 13
years old; Council Bluffs a copy from one of
Fred Pierce's paintings taken when she was
14. Two plaques painted in flowers, one of
roses, leaves just falling. and one of poppies;
also a larga piano cover painted quite
elaborately.
	In addition to these there were many other
things well deserving mention if our space
would permit, besides cooking of an excellent
quality and in great variety. The Fair
was a success in every respect and the children
deserve great credit.
			E. B. W.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


Mrs. Martha D. Lincoln, of Washington
D. C., better known as "Bessie Beech," be-
gan her literary work at sixteen, contribut-
ing to the Morning Star of Dover for several
years. Since then, she has been a contributor,
correspondent and reporter for many papers.
For several years past she has been engaged
on "Central Figures in Science," a book now
nearly finished. If she ever makes a collec-
tion of her "Beech Leaves,"they will cover al-
most every event of prominence in social life
at the capital for twenty years after the war.

	An effort will be made by influential
women in New York to send one of their
number to sit in court whenever a woman is
tried, in order to give her the protection that
women so much need under such circum-
stances. An official who was consulted said,
"But, ladies, you will hear much that will be
unfit for ladies to hear." After attending a
trial, one of the ladies said to him, "We
heard nothing unpleasant." "Oh," he said,
"of course not--when ladies are present." If
the presence of good women would purify the
atmosphere of our courts, it is a strong argu-
ment for having some motherly woman present
whenever an unprotected girl is to be tried.


', '[WomansExponent | Vol17 | 1888 | September | Issue: 8 | Title: PRIMARY FAIR. | Author: Unknown]

PRIMARY FAIR.
	The Children's Fair was open for four days,
commencing on Wednesday, the 12th inst., and
continuing until the end of the week. There was
quite a creditable representation made of the little
people's work. As it is utterly impossible "to tell
it all" we shall not make the attempt, but only
particularize some of the very best articles on ex-
hibition.
	Of the country wards we noticed Big Cotton-
wood, Mill Creek, Draper and North Jordan had

[Column 2]
a variety of useful and ornamental articles, nicely
arranged on the tables. From Big Cottonwood
there was a hammer and chisel in iron work.
made by little boys, and so well done, that no one
could help admiring the workmanship; also
bread and pies by little boys and very
fine potatoes and other vegetables, raised
by them. A map of the United States, drawn and
colored by a little girl, showed very good
ability; there was also, from quite small boys of 
that ward, a ladder and rake, and from a girl old-
fashioned heavy woolen gloves, hooked, patchwork
quilts, plain sewing, ornamental needlework, and
many pretty things beside.
	Draper Ward, there was a very well made and
perfect shoe made by a boy; also vegetables
raised by boys, and cooking done by them; from
one of the girls a piece of home-made carpet, well
woven; the same little girl can put a piece into the
loom herself.
	North Jordan Ward had wax flowers, some
other handsome work, but the most unique article
was a bridle made of cord and horsehair, by a
little fellow who had also made the machine in
which it was manufactured; the bridle is very
strong and quite a curiosity.
	Mill Creek Ward, velvet pincushion worked in
the finest silk, very elaborately, and several other
articles of value. 
	Third Ward, Salt Lake City, had so many very
good things on their table, that it was rather
difficult to select what was most appropriate to
mention. In patchwork there was some over and
over hand sewing by a little girl four years old;
that child deserves to be praised; comb case cut
in wood work by a little boy, horseshoe covered
with velvet, edges corded, very neatly done, for
ornament, knitted tidy, made in flowers, very
handsome throw downs, in beautiful colors;
a patchwork quilt cover, neatly made, each seam
carefully pressed, the work all done by a small
boy; some drawings in very good taste by a boy
of the same ward.
	Of the city wards, there were many useful and
ornamental articles, made by little girls and boys,
all of them quite young, ranging in age from four
to fourteen, but as it is quite impossible to particu-
larize, we can only give a few, and say there were
many more just as good.
	Second Ward, very odd and handsome white
apron, elaborate crochet inserting and edging,
little girl.
	Fourth Ward, a cup and saucer in crochet work
by a little girl, that attracted the attention of all
who came in; flower painting, by a little girl from
the same ward, well done; also underclothing in
plain hand sewing.
	Sixth Ward, home-knit stockings, little girl;
card receiver in stars, made of thread on cut
pasteboard and bound, bows of ribbon; ribbon
work, banner worked on velvet, and other articles.
	Eighth Ward, a handkerchief bag made of a
towel, very pretty, by a little girl; sun-flowers,
large sized ones, so perfectly natural that any one
might pick them up thinking they were real; also
pretty wax flowers, and many other things.
	Tenth Ward made a good representotion of
various articles; fruit very good, and a handsome
quilt.
	Thirteenth Ward had an Essay on the First
Principles of the Gospel, written by a little girl
which deserves special mention, as to correctness
of style and expression; also from that ward a
very handsome ornamental banner, silk and tinsel
work, and a child's apron in colors, done in etch-
ing, and a handkerchief and reticule in novelty
lace.
	Fourteenth Ward had some hand sewing on
underclothing, very neatly done, and child's dress
made by hand, tucking, embroidery and button
holes, all very good indeed; wool flowers, hand-
somely framed, and very unique yellow and scarlet
tidy, crochet work; also daisy mats and crochet
lace.

[Column 3]
	Fifteenth Ward, there was quite a number of
useful and ornamental articles; there were the two
small trunks made by quite young boys; they were
examined carefully, and pronounced excellent
work; some very neat stocking darning by little
girls; very pretty and showy crazy quilts.
	Sixteenth Ward, an engine complete on wheels
made by a little boy (U. N. Narrow Guage); also a
wooden sled, by a boy, and a wheelbarrow from
another.
	Seventeenth Ward, a handsome reticule, made
of a silk handkerchief; crochet skirt, handsome
tidy, velvet and ribbon tidies, aprons, etc.; doll's
bed, made and trimmed, spread, shams and the
ruffles on the sham sheet composed of 72 scraps of
embroidery, neatly and regularly put together, by
a little girl; this was one of the curiosities. 
	Eighteenth Ward, some pencil sketches, by a
very little girl; also ornamental snowball, made of
white paper.
	Twenty-first Ward made quite a display. A
little wooden cradle, by a boy, was quite well
made; also a wooden knife box, made and painted;
there was very nice hand sewing by the little
girls, small dresses for children, made by hand,
pillow slips, etc. Of tidies, quilts, stockings,
cookery, preserves, and many odd things, there
was a great variety, and all very good.
	Certainly a great deal has been accomplished
by the children through the agency of the Prim-
aries, and the perseverance, patience and untiring
energy of the sisters who have had charge of
these helpful associations. They most assuredly
deserve the praise and the encouragement of all
interested in the rising generation. Theirs is a
great and noble work, and they should have all the
aid and assistance needful to help them in their
arduous labors.


']

["[WomansExponent | Vol13 | 1884 | October | Issue: 10 | Title: THE DESERET HOSPITAL. | Author: Unknown]

THE DESERET HOSPITAL.
			[line break]
	About the middle of July the Deseret Hos-
pital, which had been previously situated in the
Twelfth Ward of this City, was removed to a
building in the Seventeenth Ward, for several
years occupied by and known as the Deseret
University Building.  It is a very desirable lo-
cation for an institution of this kind, standing
at the corner of 2nd West and 1st North Sts.
There has been quite a number of improve-
ments made in the building, and it is really a
pleasant home for the afflicted, though there are
other improvements quite as necessary that will
be accomplished as soon as means can be ob-
tained to defray the expenses.  To those per-
sons who desire to aid the suffering and allevi-
ate misfortune, here is an opportunity to be-
stow of this world's goods and help in establish-
ing a place where the sick and injured  can re-
ceive the best medical and surgical skill and the
most efficient care and nursing towards their
recovery.
	The President of the Hospital, Bishop H. B.
Clawson, visits the institution himself every
day and leaves no means in his power untried to
promote its best interests.  The Board of Di-
rectors, who have been associated with the work
from its commencement, meet regularly for the
transaction of business, and the ladies of the
visiting committee visit every week and report
to the Executive Board.  There is a competent
resident Surgeon in the house, and physicians
who are well known in the medical fraternity,
visit regularly and take a deep interest in the
welfare of the same, there is also a steward and
matron, with competent nurses and other helps
who are under the direction of the matron.
Morning and evening prayers are regularly at-
tended to, and all patients who are able are ex-
pected to be present when prayers are offered.
On Sunday, meetings are held in the large re-
ception room, and the sacrament administered
to all in the house who  are under the direct
supervision of the Bishop of the Ward.
	Of late there have been several donations of
fruits, flowers, money and other articles; all
these things are very acceptable and there are
no doubt many who might give without impov-
erishing,and it would be a great blessing to help
forward a work of this kind.
	The ladies who established this Hospital in
May, 1882, and have worked faithfully with and
for it from the beginning, know better than any
one else possibly can, what a struggle it has been
to obtains means sufficient to keep up with the regu-
lare expenses.  These ladies, among whom we
must not forget to mention the venerable Presi-
dent, Sister Eliza R. S. Smith, who resigned


her position last April, have been untiring in
their efforts to make the Hospital a success in
every respect, never weary in well doing, and
although their labors were seemingly more or
less unappreciated, notwithstanding they gave
so much time and thought and actual labor free-
ly, yet they have persevered and are still deter-
mined to continue in this benevolent enter-
prise.
	At the commencement of the organization a
method was adopted of taking memberships of
one dollar per year, which has been very suc-
cessful with the ladies, and the money thus ob-
tained gives to the institution a little margin,
which is always necessary in establishing any-
thing of this kind.  Although the first year the
Association was composed of ladies exclusively,
it was afterwards decided to take gentlemen
members on the same terms.  Since that time
some brethren have come forward and joined,
but, so far, comparatively few.  If this could be
made general it would be one of the very best
methods to help the institution in its infancy,
for there are very few who could not afford to
give one dollar per year to such a noble work as
the care, nursing and treatment of the sick.
There are many, indeed most of those, who ap-
ply for treatment, who cannot pay, and as a nat-
ural consequence, unless the Hospital is helped
by free donations, it would naturally fall behind;
at the present time there is due to the Hospital
from patients for medical treatment and board,
considerably over two thousand dollars, and a
very great amount of charity work has been
done since the institution was established.  The
managers of the Hospital will thankfully re-
cieve any donations whatever, fruits, fresh or
dried, vegetables of any kind, or produce, any-
thing in fact that the liberal and kind-hearted
public feel to give will be acceptble.
	President John Taylor and his Counselors, the
Twelve Apostles and their Counselors have giv-
en their names as members of the Deseret Hos-
pital Association, also A. M. Cannon, Prest. of
this Stake, and his Counselors, and Prest. W.
W. Cluff of Snmmit Stake, and several Bishops
and other brethren.
	At the Priesthood meeting in this city a short
time ago this subject was talked of and a vote
taken upon it, and it may therefore be expected
that the brethren of this Stake will come for-
ward and join the Association.  The officers in-
vite those interested in this matter to pay a vis-
it to the building that they may see for them-
selves how the affairs are conducted.

DESULTORY NOTES.

	We are often asked why there is not more in
the EXPONENT. concerning the Deseret Hos-
pital. To give definite particulars that the
public might like to know would occupy consid-
erable space, and would scarcely be of sufficient
importance to give so large a share of a very
small paper, which is published in the interest
of women generally, both young and old, and
not one particular organization or department
of either charity, industry, or politics. We
may say, however, with all safety at the pres-
ent time, that thus far the Deseret Hospital has
prospered. With the many difficulties to sur-
mount that ever attend the establishing of an
institution of this character, and, taking the
whole matter into consideration, it cannot but
be said the ladies so far have done well. To
be sure they were most of them inexperienced
in hospital work, but time, patience and per-
severance accomplish wonders when the heart
is interested, and certainly the ladies of this
city and adjacent cities and counties have mani-
fested a true spirit of benevolence and liberality
in bestowing of their means, and a desire to
help carry on this enterprise, which is most
praise-worthy and admirable. There has been
obstacles to encounter and emergencies to meet
which were not anticipated in the beginning,
but such things are incidental in all depart-
ments of life and work, and therefore, scarcely
need commenting upon. The people were ap-
pealed to for free donations to assist in furnish-
ing and fitting up the building, and the res-
ponse was general from both men and women,
as well as prompt and generous.
	A great many have feit very anxious to help
forward the institution by giving public enter-
tainments; perhaps, because they knew more
of the needs of such an institution, and it is
customary to aid in this way, and certainly
it has been a great blessing and help to the
Hospital. The proceeds of the concert given in
the large Tabernacle last July, under the direc-
tion of a very able Committee, Elder George
Goddard, Chairman and general manager of the
affair, did considerable towards helping this in-
stitution in its incipiency. Since then a Pri-
mary Concert has been given in the Elevnth
Ward in this city, and the proceeds handed over
to the Hospital, $41, from the children, exceed-
ingly good indeed for the little folks; of course,
a great deal was due to the excellent manage-
ment of Mr. Louie Felt and her associates in
getting up the entertainment.
	The Unity Club, which waa organized in
November, 1882, have made a donation of $500
(five hundred dollars) to the Deseret Hospital
for the benefit of that institution. It is certain-
ly most commendable in these ladies to work
and aid with material help, a place for the sick

[Column 2]
and injured to be cared for and made comforta-
ble as they could not be in their own homes.
Tne money given was the proceeds of entertain-
ments and parties, for which the members of
the club worked with all diligence and enthu-
siasm, realizing they were laboring in a charita-
ble cause for an institution just in its infancy,
struggling for permanent establishment.
	For years the sisters actively interested in
the Relief Society in this Church have seen and
felt deeply the need of a place of this kind.
But where to obtain the means to found a hos-
pital seemed to be the difficulty in the way of
its accomplishment. There did not appear
beforehand, at least in the minds of many, so
much apprehension about maintaining it were
it once practically started. However, having
undertaken this benevolent enterprise, the
ladies of the Executive Board find that it will
require continuous application to meet the
monthly expenses, as there are necessarily more
patients that can only pay a small sum towards
their care and treatment, than those who can
pay in full the expenses for board and medical
attention.
	Every person must know what it is to go into
an empty house and furnish it with everything
for comfort and convenience, and besides the
furnishings such as are necessary for family use
in a home, a hospital must have a dispensary
supplied with medicines, and also instruments
for operations of all kinds. In this respect the
Deseret Hospital is well fitted up. The instru-
ments were purchased in New York City, and
are of the very best material and manufacture.
	All the apartments and wards are neatly and
suitably furnished, and the accomodations are
such as the patient might enjoy in a home,
more than like an ordinary hospital. All the
people employed there strive to make the
house home-like and its inmates happy. Ser-
vices of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-
day Saints are held in the reception room every
Sunday afternoon, and the sacrament is admin-
istered under the direction of the Bishop of
the Ward and his Counselors. The sick have
the privilege of the administration of the Elders
of the Church, which is certainly an advantage
and blessing to the suffering, which the Latter-
day Saints can appreciate.
	The medical board is ample for all emergen-
cies. Dr. W. F. Anderson, the regular visiting
surgeon, has an established reputation as a
physician, of more than a quarter of a century’s
standing in this community. Several lady
physicians are on the visiting and consulting
board who are well and favorably known as suc-
cessful practitioners and a house surgeon, who,
though a young lady, has already won laurels
in the profession, and made hosts of friends in
the calling she has chosen.

﻿DESERET HOSPITAL.
The dedication services of the Deseret Hospi-
tal were held at the building on Fifth East
Street, in this city, Monday, July 17th, at
11 o’clock a.m. Of the First Presidency of
the Church John Taylor and Joseph F. Smith,
were present, also President Wilford Woodruff
and Apostle F. D. Richards. Presidency of
Salt Lake Stake, Angus M. Cannon and Joseph
E. Taylor, Mayor of the city, Wm. Jennings,
Elders C. W. Penrose, L. John Nuttall, and
Joseph Horne. The President and officers of
the institution, also physicians in charge, mat-
ron and others. Dr. Seymour B. Young read
the programme of the services. Professor C. J.
Thomas had charge of the music. Prayer by
President Woodruff, and after singing and prayer
Dr. Young read the circular and appeal.
	President John Taylor said his sympathies
were extended to the ladies in their labors of
love for the establishment of this hospital,
where the sick of the Lord’s people could be at-
tended and have the benefit of the ordinances
of the Church as well as skilful treatment.
Accidents were numerous in these troublous
times and sickness prevalent. It was right that
we should have a place where the sick could be
efficiently tended. Several of the ladies pres-
ent had acquired a knowledge of those princi-
ples which were needful in proper attention to
the sick, which he was pleased to recognize, for
it was in accord with our religion to acquire all
useful information to deal with disease, and
physicians should not be bound by rigid rules,
but act as directed by the Lord, because there
were so many phases of the same diseases and
so much difference in the constitutions of man-
kind. At the same time we were commanded
of the Lord to “seek out of the best books words
of wisdom,” and to seek knowledge by learning.
Yet we must not forget to call in the aid of
faith, but while we acquire all the intelligence
possible to be attained, at the same time we must
exercise faith that the blessings of the Lord
might be upon our efforts. The officers of the
Association and all connected with it, had his
best wishes, and he felt that they would have
the blessing and favor of God. They should not
neglect to avail themselves of all possible sour-
ces of knowledge, but seek for it in every direc-
tion. He closed by blessing the officers, physi-
cians and attendants, and all connected with
the hospital.
	President Joseph F. Smith endorsed the re-
marks of President Taylor. This was a step
quite necessary to be taken. It was in the in-
-terest of the afflicted and the poor. He would
have been pleased if the institution could have
been started in a better place. It was but a
small beginning,  yet it was better than none at
all, and he expressed the hope that success
would attend it, and that perfect union, har-
mony and good feeling would prevail among all
who had the conduct of its affairs, so that the
good spirit of the Lord might be with them,
and that while they exercised all the skill that
was possible, they might be so sensitive to the
whisperings of the spirit of the Lord that they
would be able to obtain the power of God for the
benefit of the sick, and that they might be
guided in the channel of the best success. He
believed that the Latter-day Saints, had the
right to obtain the blessing and direction of the
Lord In all their affairs, and especially in such
labors as those for the benefit of the afflicted;
He invoked the blessing of the Lord upon the
efforts of the institution, its officers and all who
should receive the benefits thereof.
	Apostle F. D. Richards offered the dedicatory
prayer,
	President W. Woodruff coincided with what
had been said, and congratulated the sisters
upon the progress of another labor of love con-
nected with the Relief Society. That society,
like other features of the Church and Kingdom
of God, was organized to stay. He endorsed
with all his heart the principles enunciated of
looking to the Lord. We needed salvation.
Men’s ideas of medicine changed materially.
The course of practice pursued when he was a
boy would not be followed now. He related inci-
dents in illustrations of this, and showed the
necessity of the guidance of the Almighty in
the treatment of the sick. he was thankful
that we would now have a place for the treat-
ment of the afflicted, where the Elders could
walk in and freely administer the ordinance for
the healing of the sick, and he felt that this
institution would grow and increase and ac-
complish the purpose desired.
	President Angus M. Cannon was gratified
that the Sisters had been led to take the course
which had resulted in the establishment of this
hospital. They had been very faithful in their
efforts in that Relief Society which was estab-
lished by the Prophet Joseph Smith. Here was
a place where the afflicted of the Lord’s people
could be watched over in a proper way and
could be administered to by the good Samari-
tans. God had set His hand to establish Zion
in its beauty and power, and he felt that this
beginning would be looked back to as the germ
of something much greater, which would re-
flect credit upon all engaged in it. He trusted
that some extensive grounds would soon be ob-
tained. He related an incident in his experi-
ence, showing the necessity and blesssing of
reliance upon the Lord for healing of the sick.
he hoped the Institution would be sustained by
the faith and means of the people. 
	Mayor Wm. Jennings considered that this
hospital was needed in this Territory. The
Elders had done a good work. We had become
a large community, and people of all classes
were among us, also many who were afflicted
from various causes. In some of the isolated
places of the Territory there was lack of medi-
cal skill, and here was a place to which cases
might be brought for treatment which they
could not obtain where they lived. Here the
sick could be treated in a way that they could
not be among persons not of our faith. He
hoped the ladies would keep in view, as he be-
lieved they would, the fact that this hospital
was not for speculation but for relief. He hoped
this building would prove large enough for many
years to come. It would have his support, both
by his means and faith.
	Elder C. W. Penrose said there was no need
for any remarks from him concerning the objects
of this institution nor the benefits to follow from
it; these had been well set forth by those
speakers who had preceded him. He would say,
however, that the institution had his sympathy
and support, and he believed it would be suc-
cussful. Some persons might wonder why Lat-
ter-day Saints who believed in healing by faith,
needed a hospital. He showed that faith and
science were not incompatible. That, according
to the revelations of God, knowledge was to be
obtained by study and also by faith; so with the
treatment of the sick; the means ordained of
God as remedies should be used and at the same
time faith be exercised. They who had suffi-
cient faith could be healed by faith. They who
had not faith were to be nourished and treated
with proper remedies, and these, it was written,
should be used with “prudence and thanksgiv-
ing,” with judgment and skill, but "not by the
hand of an enemy.” Therefore skill should be
acquired by study. Women had always been
recognized as good nurses, but only of recent
date as skilled physicians and surgeons. He was
glad at the progress of public opinion. He con-
gratulated the ladies on the good work they had
accomplished.
 The next in order was the setting apart of the
physicians and matron by President John Tay-
lor, and Joseph F. Smith and others. Dr. Ellen
B. Ferguson was blest and set apart by Prest.
Taylor, Dr. Romania B. Pratt, President Smith
officiating. Mary E. Greene Van Schoonhoven
Dispensary clerk by President Woodruff. Mary
Ann McLean, Matron, by Apostle F. D.
Richards. Benediction was pronounced by
Counselor Joseph E. Taylor.
	Mayor Jennings manifested his interest in
this benevolent institution by handing over to
the President a check for five hundred dollars.
There is every prospect of success in this humane
and much  needed enterprise if the brethren and
sisters respond to the appeal. It requires a great
exertion on the part of those engaged in this
work to establish an institution of this kind by
donation. There is everything needed, and any
donation of furniture, bedding, household uten-
sils, crockery, cutlery, etc., will be gratefully
received. It is hoped that the sisters in the dif.
ferent Stakes of Zion will feel it a pleasure and a
duty to become members of the Hospital Associa-
tion, and have their names enrolled with those
more actively engaged in this good and noble
work for the suffering and distressed.
