["[WomansExponent | Vol2 | 1873 | September | Issue: 7 | Title: ADVICE TO LADIES UPON COSTUME | Author: Unknown]

ADVICE TO LADIES UPON COSTUME

	In no way has civilized man played more
fantastic tricks, and sacrificed his reason
more entirely to folly, than in the matter of
dress. The clumsy and inconvenient gar-
ments of the savage, are attributed to his
ignorance of domestic arts; but what can be
said in excuse for civilized man, when he
wears shoes that project half a yard beyond
his feet, or exchanges his own locks for
a periwig filled with powder and pomatum;
when the graceful motion of a lady’s head is
sacrificed to the stiff movements necessary
in balancing a tower of linen and wire,
half a yard high, with draperies that flow
from the top of it to the floor; when the
wavy lines of a female form are disguised
under a stff circle of whalebone, which im-
prisons the body from the hips upward, and
a buckram cage so surrounds the lower limbs,
that she can with difficulty walk or sit.
Some false standard of beauty, invented
perhaps to conceal some deformity, is set
up, and then the very bones and mucles of
the perfect body must be made to conform
to it. When this is carried so far as it is in
the case of small feet in China, its absurdity
strikes us at once; but we may find, nearer
home, instances of a standard as false, and
consequences even more fatal to health and
happiness than the little feet of the Chinese.
	The history of national costume in the
civilized countries of Europe shows, that for
many centuries the progress of art and
manufactures only led to greater extrava-
gance in dress, and more preposterous
fashions. One enormity was only displaced
to make way for the opposite extreme, as
in the case of the peaked-toed shoes already
mentioned; these were followed by shoes of
only the length of the foot, but as broad as
they were long. At one time men’s coats
were so short that they resembled boy’s
jackets of the present day, and soon after
they were so long and full that they looked
like female attire. Women's sleeves were
sometimes made so long that they were tied
in knots, to prevent the wearer from tread-
ing on them, and nine yards of cloth was a
moderate quantity for each sleeve; and then
they were made as tight as skin, and
reached no father than the elbow. It would
seem that for centuries the whole ingenuity
of a nation was taxed to invent monstrous
forms of clothing, as well as inconvenient
and useless appendages, and that comfort
and ease were the things most of all to be
avoided in dress.
	After the general fashion of hoops had
passed away, and when they were gazed
after, on gala days only, as a raree-show, it
was difficult to imagine rational beings
reconciled to them, and considering them
an indispensible part of female attire, or to
believe that they were once so universal in
London, that maid-servants were seen
washing down the door-steps in hoop--petti-
coats. Yet such was actually the case before
they were left off by the higher classes.
	This reflection upon past fashions should
make us look with jealous eyes on those
which prevail in our day, in order to dis-
cover whether we are not in the practice of
something which will appear equally absurd
to future generations. The French nation
has long been the arbiter of fashion for most
civilized countries and the political revolu-
tion of the last century was accompanied by
a revolution in dress almost as signal; for it
banished wigs and buckles, powder and
pomatum, stiff stays and full petticoats,
long waists, and high-heeled shoes. One
extreme led to another, and the ladies who
had been encased in whalebone, bukrum.
and abundance of quilted petticoats, stepped
forth as Grecian goddesses, without any
corsets, any petticoats, any fulness to their
garments, or any heels to their shoes.
White muslin dresses of the scantiest
dimensions, drawn closely round the figure,
with the shortest possible waists, and not a
fold or a plait that could form any drapery,
were the order of the day in France and
quickly spread into England. Only look
at the portraits in the historical pictures of
that period—such for instance, as the coro-
nation of Napoleon—and you will be so
shocked at the tight sleeves, short waists, and
narrow skirts of the women, that it will be
difficult to believe that such a style of dress
ever looked well.
	The influence of fashion is so strong in
corrupting the eye, and perverting the taste,
that it has led some persons to doubt the
existance of any true standard of beauty as
applicable to costume; but as long as some
forms of dress, when out of fashion, look
better to us than others, we may fairly con-
clude that there are some immutable princi-
ples of taste connected with the subject,
and that those articles which we admire,
after they have ceased to be the reigning
mode, conform in some degree to true taste.
Such for instance, is the simple cap called
after Mary Queen of Scots. When the pre-
vailing fashions are most opposed to the flat
shape of the Mary-Queen-of-Scots cap, it still
appears to us beautiful; and when adopted
by a modern fine lady, as her costume in a
portrait, or her dress at a masquerade ball, it
is pronounced to be highly becoming. Now,
this is not the case with the monstrous
constructions of gauze, ribbon, and wire,
that was called caps fifty years ago; nor with
any of those head-dresses, which outrage
common sense, and set all proportion at de-
fiance. As soon as the enormous horse-hair
cushions, over which the locks were combed
and plastered with powder and pomatum,
went out of fashion, that style of head-dress
was condemned as hideous.
	Now, if there are principles of true taste
involved in the mysteries of a lady’s toilet,
is not the study of them worthy of a refined
and intellectual being; and would not her
time and thoughts be better spent in con-
forming her style of dress to them, than in
eagerly following every change of fashion
dictated by the love of novelty, apart from
real beauty?—“English Paper.”

	Paint.—To get rid of the smell of oil
paint, plunge a handful of hay into a pail
full of water, and let it stand in the room
newly painted.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT


", '[WomansExponent | Vol6 | 1877 | April | Issue: 22 | Title: THE LITTLE HEALTH OF LADIES. | Author: FRANCES POWER COBB]

THE LITTLE HEALTH OF LADIES.

BY FRANCES POWER COBB.

*	*	*	*	*	*	*	*	*

	We have now touched on the subject of
dress, which plays such an important part
in the health of women that it must here
be treated somewhat at length. A little
girl in a London Sunday School, being ask-
ed by a visitor "why God made the flowers
of the field?" replied (not unconscious of
the gorgeous paper poppy in her own bon-
net), "Please, ma'am, I suppose for patterns
for artificial flowers." One might antici-
pate some answer scarcely less wide of the
mark than that of this sophisticated little
damsel, were the question to be put to not
a few grown women, "Why do you wear
clothes?" Their most natural response
would obviously be, "To be in the fashion."
When we have visibly wandered a long
way from the path of reason, the best thing
we can do is to look back to the starting
point, and find out, if possible, where we
have diverged. In the matter of raiment
that starting-point is not hard to find—in-
deed, to mark it is only to state a series of
truisms.
	Human clothing has three "raisons

[Column 2]
d'etre," which, in order of precedence, are
these:
	I. HEALTH.
	II. DECENCY.
	III. BEAUTY.
	HEALTH demands—
	1. Maintenance of proper temperature of
the body by exclusion of excessive heat
and cold.
	2. Protection from injury by rain, snow,
dust, dirt, stones to the feet, insects, etc.
	3. Preservation of liberty of action to all
the organs of the body, and freedom from
pressure.
	DECENCY demands—
	4. Concealment of some portions of the
human frame.
	5. Distinction between the habiliments of
men and women, sufficient to avert mis-
take.
	6. Fitness for the age and character of
the wearer.
	7. Concealment, when possible, of any
disgusting personal defect.
	BEAUTY demands—
	8. Truthfulness. The dress must be gen-
uine throughout, without any false pads,
false hair, or false anything.
	9. Graceful forms of drapery.
	10. Harmonious colors.
	11. Such moderate consistency with pre-
vailing modes of dress as shall produce the
impression of sociability and sauvity, and
avoid that of self-assertion.
	12. Individuality: the dress suiting the
wearer as if it were an outer body belong-
ing to the same soul.
	Glancing back over the above table, we
find this curious fact: The dress of men in
all Western nations meets fairly all the con-
ditions of health and decency, and fails on-
ly on the side of beauty. The dress of wo-
men, on the contrary, ever variable as it is,
persistently misses the conditions of health;
frequently violates the rules of decency;
instead of securing beauty, at which it aims
first instead of last, achieves, usually ugli-
ness.
	It is to be remembered for our consola-
tion and encouragement that men have ar-
rived at their present good sense in dress
only within two or three generations. A
hundred years ago the lords of creation set
beauty above health or convenience, just
as the ladies do now, and peacocked about
in their peach-blossom coats and embroid-
ered waistcoats, surmounted by wigs, for
whose stupendous discomfort even a seat
on the judicial bench can scarcely reconcile
the modern Englishman. Now, when the
men of every European nation have abjur-
ed such fantastic apparel, we naturally ask:
Why have not the women followed their
example? Why is the husband, father
and brother, habited like a being who has
serious interests in life, and knows that his
personal dignity would be forfeited were
he to dress himself in party-colored, be-rib-
boned garments, and why is the wife,
mother, or sister, bedizened like a macaw,
challenging every observer to note how
much of her time, thoughts and money,
must have been spent on this futile object?
The answer is one which is not pleasant to
make, discreditable as it is to both sexes.
The women who set the fashions dress for
admiration; and men like women who dress
to be admired; and the admiration given
and received is a very poor and unworthy
admiration, not much better than a salmon
gives to a glittering artificial fly, and hav-
ing very little more to do with any real
aesthetic gratification—as is proved too
clearly by the thoroughly unbeautiful de-
vices to which fashion has recourse. It is
the well-got up woman (to borrow a very

[Column 3]
expressive phrase,) not the really well-
dressed woman, who receives by far the
greatest share of homage.
	And now let us see how all this concerns
the health of women—how much their
"petite sante" is due to their general neg-
lect to make health the first object of dress,
or even an object at all compared to fashion.
	Tight-lacing among habits resembles envy
among the passions. We take pride in all
the rest, even the idlest and worst, but
tight-lacing and an envious heart are
things to which no one ever confesses. A
small waist, I suppose, is understood to be-
long to that order of virtues which Aris-
totle decides ought to be natural and not
acquired, and the most miserable girl who
spends her days in a machine more cruel
(because more slowly murderous) than the
old "Maiden" of Seville, yet always a[-]sures
us, smiling through her martyrdom, that
her clothes are "really hanging about her!"
It would be waste of time to dwell on this
supreme folly. Mrs. Haweis, in her very
noteworthy new book, "The Art of Beauty,"
has given some exceedingly useful dia-
grams, showing the effects of the practice
on the internal organs and skeleton—dia-
grams which I earnestly recommend to the
study of ladies who may feel a "call." to
perform this sort of English suttee for a 
living husband. Mrs. Haweis says that
sensible men do not love wasps, and have
expressed to her their "overallishness"
when they behold them. Considering how
effectively they have hitherto managed to
display their disapproval whenever women
have attempted to introduce rational at-
tire, it is a pity, I think, that they do not 
"pronounce" a little more distinctly against
this literally moral folly.
	I have already alluded to the brain-heat-
ing chignons, just gone out of fashion after
a long reign of mischief; and along with
them should be classed the bonnets which
expose the forehead to the cold, while the
back of the head is stewed under its cush-
ion of false hair, and which have the still
more serious disadvantage of affording no
shelter to the eyes. To women to whom
the glare of the sun is permanently hurt-
ful to the sight, the necessity of wearing
these bonnets on pain of appearing singu-
lar, or affectedly youthful, constitutes al-
most a valid reason against living in Lon-
don. And the remedy, forsooth, is to hold
up perpetually a parasol!—a yet further in-
cumbrance to add to the care of the drag-
gling train, so that both arms may be occu-
pied during a whole walk, and of course
all natural case of motion rendered impos-
sible. In this, as in a dozen other silly
fashions, the women who have serious con-
cerns in life are hampered by the practice
of those who think of nothing but exhibit-
ing their persons. And ladies of limited
fortune, who live in small rooms and go
about the streets on foot or in cabs, are com-
pelled (if they wish to avoid being pointed
at) to adopt modes of dress whose sole
"raison d'etre" is that they suit wealthy
"grandes dame" who lounge in their ba-
rouches or display their trains over the
carpets of forty-feet-long drawing-rooms.
What snobbery all this implies in our whole
social structure! Some ten millions of wo-
men dress, as nearly as they can afford, in
the style fit at the most for five thousand!
	The practice of wearing "decollettee"
dresses, sinning equally as it does against
health and decency, seems to be gradually
receding—from ordinary dinners, where it
was universal twenty years ago, to special
occasions, balls, and court drawing-rooms.
But it dies hard, and may kill a good many
poor creatures yet, and entail on others the


life long bad health so naturally resulting
from the exposure of a large surface of the
skin to sudden chills.
	TO BE CONTINUED.
—Cotemporary Review.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


', '[WomansExponent | Vol5 | 1876 | August | Issue: 5 | Title: LADIES UPON DRESS. | Author: Unknown]

LADIES UPON DRESS.

	The rich dress and costly ornaments that
become maturer life, and ceremonious par-
ties in large cities, are unsuited to the very
young, who need no such "foreign aid."
Some persons toil early and late and strain
every nerve, to procure an expensive gar-
ment, and think, that once arrayed in it,
they will look as well as some richer neigh-
bor, whose style of dress they wish to im-
itate; but they forget, that if it does not ac-
cord with their general style of living, if it
is out of harmony with other things, it will
so strike everybody, and this want of fit-
ness will prevent it looking well on them.
Let a true sense of propriety of the fitness
of things regulate all your habits of living
and dressing; and it will produce such a
beautiful harmony and consistency of char-
acter, as will throw a charm around you
that all will feel, though few may compre-
hend. Always consider well whether the
articles of dress which you wish to pur-
chase are suited to your age, your condi-
tion, your means; the climate, to the par-
ticular use to which you mean to put them;
and then let the principles of good taste
keep you from the extremes of the fashion,
and regulate the form, so as to combine util-
ity and beauty, while the known rules of
harmony in colors save you from shocking
the eye of the artist with incongruous mix-
tures.
	The agreeable effect that all wish to pro-
duce in the eyes of others, depends much
more on just, proportions in the parts, thus
forming a pleasing whole, than on little or-
namental additions, which, though pretty
in themselves, add nothing to the general
effect. Thus in making a pelisse, it is all-
important that the cape should be of the
right size and shape, to agree well with the
sleeves and back, and that the collar should
be well proportioned to the cape; but it mat-
ters very little whether it have one or two
rouleaus of satin or velvet around it, or
whether it have none; and so it is with a
thousand other little particulars, which take
much time in the making and add much to
the expense, without really adding any-
thing to the general good appearance. It is
a happy thing for the diffusion of good
taste, that it may, in many things, be cul-
tivated without any pecuniary expense. The


[COLUMN 1]

price of colored muslin or printed calico, is
the same whether the figure be pretty or
ugly; the ribbon that is tastefully disposed
upon the bonnet, costs no more than the
one that is ill arranged. The shawl is the
same in value whether it is dragged round
the shoulders like an Indian's blanket, or
worn in graceful folds. It costs no more to
buy colors that harmonize than those that
do not. Indeed, true taste will generally
be found on the side of economy, because
simplicity is;—the first cost is thus lessen-
ed, and garments that are really well shap-
ed, are longer in favor. The exercise of
good taste, therefore, need not be consider-
ed the privilege of the few; it may regulate
the toilet of her who earns what she ex-
pends upon it, as well as for her whose bills
are paid by a rich father; the more it is
studies the more good sense and simplicity
will be consulted, because these are includ-
ed in the principles of true taste.
	Whilst taste may be made to regulate the
dress of all, even of the working classes,
wealth, unaccompanied by it, cannot com-
mand its beautiful results. Your clothes
may be ordered of the best dressmaker, and
everything you wear may be of the most
costly materials, and in the latest Paris
fashion; and yet you may spoil your appear-
ance by your manner of putting them on,
and by wearing those articles together
which do not accord. The most expensive
dresses at a ball are seldom those that pro-
duce the best effect; and nothing so effect-
ually defeats its object, as an excess of or-
nament. Some persons seem to have an
inherent love of finery, and seem to cling
to it pertinaciously, even when their under-
standings are convinced that it is repug-
nant to the feelings of refined minds, and
that it is a trait common to all barbarous
tribes: they cannot reason upon their pref-
erences—they can only say, that what oth-
ers condemn as tawdry, looks pretty to
them. This persuasion generally takes
place very early, and is much to be regret-
ed, as it prevents the growth of purer prin-
ciples. We have often thought that the
very bad taste in which dolls are usually
dressed, may have something to do with
this early love of finery. Children have of-
ten a real affection for their puppets; and
when they are bedizened in all the colours
of the rainbow, and decked in all the odds
and ends of finery that can be stuck upon
them, the little dears learn, by this associa-
tion, to love this tawdry ornament: whereas
a well-dressed doll would have an import-
ant influence in establishing a correct taste
in the mind of the child. We once knew a
family where the dolls were all very neatly
dressed, like babies and little children, and
not as fine ladies, on purpose to make them
a more rational and useful source of amuse-
ment; and we would beg all young ladies
who dress dolls for little girls, to do it in
such a manner as not to foster a love of fin-
ery.
	We have heard a mother who guarded
her daughters against this bad taste by mak-
ing it one of their childish punishments to
weak a very tawdry cap full of feathers, and
flowers, and bows of ribbons of all colours.
Judging by what we sometimes see worn
by grown up people, we might suppose that
such a cap had been their reward in child-
hood rather than their punishment, and was
thus recommended to their best affections.
The love of finery is rarely cured, and forms
an insurmountable obstacle to the cultiva-
tion of pure taste. Whosoever is conscious
of possessing it, ought to mistrust her own
judgment in matters of taste, and be will-
ing to take the advice of others. No plain-

[COLUMN 2]

ness of dress can ever be construed to your
disadvantage; but ornamental additions,
which in their best state are a very doubt-
ful good, become a positive evil when de-
faced, or soiled, or tumbled. Shabby feath-
ers, and crushed or faded artificial flowers,
are an absolute disgrace to a lady's appear-
ance; while their total absence would never
be remarked. Next to soiled stockings,
soiled bonnet-caps are the most offensive
things in a young lady's dress; and the lat-
ter are but too common. Whatever ap-
proaches the face should be particularly
clean; and it is better always to wear those
materials which will wash than to use silk
net after it is at all sullied by wear. Color-
ed gauze hankerchiefs, that are worn till
they are faded, or sullied, or the color
changed by perspiration, are a disagreeable
sight, and will spoil the neat appearance of
a person, however well dressed otherwise;
cleanliness being the first requisite in a
lady's dress.
	Such qualities of mind are brought into
action in connection with dress that we
cannot wonder at its having great influence
on the opinions that are formed of us. Who,
that sees a young lady very carefully array-
ed at a ball, and finds her when at home,
and not expecting company, in a torn or
soiled dress, can fail to draw conclusions un-
favorable to the character of the individual?
If you have a friend who has lately be-
come insolvent, and you see his daughters
appear, soon after, in new and costly gar-
ments, what are your feelings? Is not all
admiration of their costume lost, in the
sense of its betraying a want of common
honesty, thus to spend the money that be-
longs to their father's creditors? And does
not this exhibition of lax principles do
them more harm than any personal decora-
tion can do them good? Dress is a very fair
index of a young woman's neatness, indus-
try, economy, good sense, modesty, and
good taste; and she, who is at all times, in
her private as well as in her public hours,
perfectly well dressed, according to all that
we include in that term, must have many
of the substantial qualities that constitute a
good character and are essential to domes-
tic comfort. The character is much more
shown in the style of dress that is worn ev-
ery day, than in that which is designed for
great occasions; and when we see a young
girl come down to the family breakfast
in an untidy wrapper, with her hair in pa-
pers and her feet slip-shod, and an old silk
handkerchief round her neck, we know
that she cannot be the neat, industrious,
and refined person whom we would like for
an inmate. We feel equally certain too,
that her chamber is not kept in good order,
and that she does not set a proper value up-
on time. However well a lady has appear-
ed at a party, we would recommend to a
young gentleman, before he makes up his
mind as to her domestic qualities, to ob-
serve her appearance at the breakfast-table,
when she expects to see only her own fam-
ily; and, if it be such as we have just de-
scribed, to beware how he prosecutes the
acquaintance.
	To begin the day well it is necessary to
rise early enough to perform all the ablu-
tions necessary to health and cleanliness,
and to be neatly and completely dressed be-
fore breakfast. Your morning dress should
always be such that you would not be
ashamed to be seen in, by any accidental
visitor; and a clean muslin collar, or ruffie,
of the plainest materials, give an impress-
ion of far greater neatness than a colored
handkerchief. If you are suitably dressed
or the business of the morning, you are

[COLUMN 3]

ready to enter upon it at once and much
time is saved; you are not discomposed by
unexpected guests; and, if particularly en-
gaged, you can wear the same dress all day.
Very yonng girls are apt to think that the
most important item in their dress is the
material of which their frocks are made,
whereas that is really of the least conse-
quence. Clean stockings, neat shoes and
strings, smooth, well-brushed hair, and
delicately clean hands, nails, and teeth,
would make them look more lady-like, and
better dressed, in a cheap calico, than they
would be in the finest merino, or most costly
French silk without these accompaniments.
Those things which are most essential to a
neat appearance, are most within the reach
of everybody, and therefore the neglect of
them is not to be excused. Everybody can
mend stockings and gloves, however old
they may be; everybody can avoid break-
ing shoe-strings by wearing them too tight,
or having them become untied, by tying
them in false knots. Clean hands and nails,
and well-brushed hair and teeth, it is with-
in everybody's power to possess; and with-
out constant attention to these particulars,
the most expensive garments will fail to
produce the effect you desire. The same
honesty and self-respect which would keep
you from making a saving that you are
ashamed of, should prevent you wearing
anything, even out of sight, that you would
be ashamed to have seen, if sudden indisposi-
tion caused it to be exposed before stran-
gers. We should gladly adopt every con-
trivance for saving time and abridging the
labour necessarily bestowed upon dress.
Those who know how to use the needle af-
ter the best fashion of our grandmothers'
days, may next acquire the art of slighting
certain parts of their work, where it will
not interfere with durability, and so save
some thousand superfluous stitches in a
week. If it has been the misfortune of
any of my readers to have grown up with-
out being made good needlewomen, the
sooner they undertake to supply this defi-
ciency the better. A woman who does not
know how to sew is as deficient in her ed-
ucation as a man who cannot write. Let
her condition in life be what it may, she
cannot be ignorant of the use of her needle,
without incommoding herself and others,
and without neglecting some important du-
ties. Besides this, there is, in this truly
feminine employment, a moral power which
is useful to the sex. There is a soothing
and sedative effect in needle-work; it com-
poses the nerves, and furnishes a corrective
for many of the little irritations of domes-
tic life. Let no woman think herself ex-
empt from the duty of "plying the polish-
ed shaft." In every situation of life, she
will find herself the better for an early and
thorough acquaintance with plain sewing.
	SELECTED.


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		39


']

		WOMAN'S EXPONENT.
		A UTAH LADIES' JOURNAL.
	The women of Utah to-day occupy a position which
attracts the attention of intelligent thinking men and
women everywhere. They are engaged in the prac-
tical solution of some of the greatest social and moral
problems of the age, and a powerful interest is mani-
fested throughout the United States, and, it may be
said, the entire civilized world, to learn from reliable
sources the views honestly entertained by them on
these questions.
	They have been grossly misrepresented through
the press, by active enemies who permit no opportu-
nity to pass of maligning and slandering them; and
with but limited opportunity of appealing to the in-
telligence and candor of their fellow countrymen and
countrywomen in reply.
	Who are so well able to speak for the women of
Utah as the women of Utah themselves? "It is bet-
ter to represent ourselves than to be misrepresented
by others!"
	For these reasons, and that women may help each
other by the diffusion of knowledge and information
possessed by many and suitable to all, the publica-
tion of Woman's Exponent, a journal owned by,
controlled by and edited by Utah ladies, has been
commenced.
	The aim of this journal will be to discuss every
subject interesting and valuable to women. It will
contain a brief and graphic summary of current news
local and general household hints, educational mat-
ters, articles on health and dress, correspondence,
editorials on leading topics of interest suitable to its
columns and miscellaneous reading.
	It will aim to defend the right, inculcate sound
principles, and disseminate useful knowledge.
	Utah, in its Female Relief Societies, has the best
organized benevolent institution of the age; yet, but
little is known of the self-sacrificing labors of these
Societies. In Woman's Exponent a department
will be devoted to reports of their meetings and other
matters of interest connected with their workings;
and to this end the Presidents and Secretaries of the
various Societies throughout the Territory are re-
quested to furnish communications which will receive
due attention.
	Miss Eliza R. Snow, President of the entire Female
Relief Societies, cordially approves of the Journal
and will be a contributor to it as she has leisure from
her numerous duties.
	Woman's Exponent will be published semi-
monthly, each number containing eight pages, quarto.
	The following low rates will place it within the
reach of all, and the hope is that it may be made so
valuable that it will be found in every family in
Utah.

Representative_Docs:
['[WomansExponent | Vol2 | 1873 | October | Issue: 9 | Title: EDITORIAL NOTES. | Author: Unknown]

EDITORIAL NOTES.
	Stokes' trial is set for the 6th inst. Will
he be acquitted?
	A new witness, a woman appears in the
Stokes-Fisk case, through whose testimony
it is said he will be released. Let her tell 
her story.
	Her Majesty, Queen Victoria, receives
as her pension about $5,000 per day. The
Prince of Wales has about $109 per day.
No wonder English Royalty is considered
expensive.
	A man in Kansas offers to bet $500 that his
wife can walk 500 miles in three weeks. Like
a sensible woman she prefers that he should
do the "walking" himself; the design of
the proposed wager is too apparent.
	The big Graphic balloon which was to
start from New York on an upper air voyage
across the ocean to Europe, on the 12th of
September, had the good fortune to burst
before it was pumped full enough of gas to
"go up."
	Hon. Roscoe Conkling has been appointed
Chief Justice of the Supreme Court of the
United States. He is reported to be a very
judicious and good man, as any man should
be holding so high and sacred an office in
the nation.
	An infant winter presented itself in this
position of the globe, about the 20th ult.
It bore no signs of violence, but merely
gave the atmosphere a sharp, chilly touch as if
to stir people up to a remembrance that cold
weather was not far away.
Miss Susan B. Anthony has lately published
a pamphlet containing a complete account
of the prosecution and trial of the women
who voted in connection with herself. No
one could have a better right to state the
facts in the case—not even Judge Hunt.
	The diabolical practice of drowning
children, is to be henceforth considered a
punishable offence among the Chinese. Sure
enough—not only in England, Scotland and
America are the rights of the feminine
portion of humanity being taken into con-
sideration, but even in heathen China also.
	Believing that his wife had the in-
terest of all their children equally at heart,
the late Rev. Dr. Todd bequeathed his whole
estate, worth $15,000 to her. So sensible a
man should have had a wife worthy of such
confidence, and he, doubtless, knew that what
he did was the fair way of doing business.
	Uneasiness and depression in the Lon-
don market, was caused by the sudden
failures in New York, although its own
financial condition is said to be firm and
sound. London has no need to get excited
over little financial crashes this side the
waters—it will have some of its own, per-
haps, before long.
	A Young lady in Pennsylvania left home
recently for the purpose of spending the af-
ternoon in gathering blackberries. The next
news her anxious mother had from her was
that she was married and settled down to
house-keeping in Georgia. She evidently
has an eye to business and understands the
rights of woman in relation to matrimony.
	On the nineteenth, ult., nineteen firms
failed in Wall Street, New York. Wild
[new column]
excitement among the people prevailed for
several hours, as a natural result. Reports
state that in the Gold Exchange, the scene
defied description, members acting like
demons as each new failure was announced.
Thus it is, the face of fortune varies, and
rich and poor are subject to its frowns
as well as its smiles.
	Chicago courts are said to turn out an
average of two divorces a day. The practice
tolerated is represented as having become so
common and familiar, that little attention is
paid to a divorce case by the judges, more
than to report in favor of the complainant's
petition. If, in that city, wives in general
should sue for about $20,000 alimony, their
divorce suits would be likely to create a
great stir.
	"X," in "Woman's Journal," takes a
very intelligent view of the woman suf-
frage question, demands a practical test and
says, why not give impartial suffrage a
trial? accept it as we would one of Dr.
Hall's cheerful prescriptions, which are
warranted to do no harm, even if they do no
good. Let men take the only sensible
course left for them; and give woman the
ballot.
	The "New North West" has a pleasant
way of expressing its views on certain
points. It says: Miss Eliza R. Snow, the
talented maiden polygamist, whose life and
religion constitute a remarkable matri-
monial paradox, has recently returned from
an extended Oriental tour. Her communi-
cations to the Woman's Exponent during
her absence have been characterized by strik-
ing versatility of genius and fine scholarly
ability, We regret that a fine poem from
her pen in blank verse, written we think, in
Palestine, clipped from the Exponent for
publication in this paper, was mislaid
during our vacation. Will some of our Salt
Lake friends be kind enough to forward us
another copy?"
	We are asked by the editor of the
"New North West," why it is that religious
revelations always come to men." It is a
mistaken idea that woman has not the same
right to know for herself what the Lord
requires at her hands, that a manhas The
Latter-day Saints acknowledge no such
theory. In numbers 16 and 17, volume 1,
of this journal, "W.E." writing on woman's
agency, very clearly illustrates that it is a 
divine prerogative belonging to woman to
call upon the Lord, and receive answers to
her prayers; and states that but for having
acted upon this principle, she should not
occupy the position she now holds as the
wife of a polygamist in Salt Lake City.
Thousands of "Mormon" women can truth-
fully bear the same testimony. Number
18, volume 1 of the Exponent, contains
"A Chapter in the Life of Naoma"—Mrs,
Vilate Kimball, wife of the late President
Heber C. Kimball—written by "B"—Mrs.
Sarah M. Kimball—(we give the names now
by permission) in which it is shown that
direct communication from God was given
to the woman in answer to her enquiries,
and that through this means only, she learned
the law of Celestial marriage, or plurality of
wives, as her husband, through sympathy
had refused to teach it to her.
	A convention by telegraph was recently
[new column]
held by the employes of the Atlantic and
Pacific Telegraph Campany, in reference to
the death of the late Superintendent of that
division, Mr. C. L. Goodwin, of which the
Troy, New York, papers give accounts. The
offices in that division were all connected
into one circuit, extending from New York
to Albany, thence via Troy to Saratoga
and return to Albany, westward to Syracuse,
and via Oswego, Clyde, and Rochester to
Buffalo and Niagara Falls, back from Buf-
falo via Auburn, Sennecca Falls, etc,, to
Auburn again. Each employe remained in
his office and gave by means of the telegraph,
his remarks or motions to the others. New
York called the meeting to order, at the
appointed time. Buffalo moved that Mr.
Hauff, chief operator at New York, be made
permanent chairman. Troy seconded the
motion and it was carried. Speeches, reso-
lutions, etc, followed; and after the
business for which the meeting was called,
had all been harmoniously transacted, an
adjournment took place. The accounts of
such proceedings must be interesting read-
ing to telegraphists.
	The "Missouri Republican" gives the
following account of the miraculous escape
from drowning of an infant "King:" "A
Mrs. King had a surprise at Shawneetown,
Ill., the other day. It was an accident that
Mrs. King happened to be at Shawneetown.
She did not intend to go there, but the
steam-boat "Jennie Howell," on which she
was traveling, struck a snag in the Ohio river.
Several of the passengers were drowned,
and among them some children. Mrs. King
had a child on board whom she mourned as
lost, and was taken to Shawneetown with-
out her babe. The "Jennie Howell" sunk
in deep water, and the next morning men
went to the vessel to recover the bodies of
the lost. Soon after daylight a mattress was
seen floating in the cabin, which was filled
with water nearly to the ceiling. Upon 
examination a child, a little boy, was discov-
ered on the mattress sleeping as peacefully 
as if nothing had happened. His bed was
not very dry, for it sunk a good deal from
soaking, but still sustained its living freight.
In due time the child was sent to Shawnee-
town, where the other passengers had been
landed the evening before. Its coming made
an excitement among the wrecked travelers,
for more than one mother had lost children
by the disaster. It was a touching scene
when Mrs. King recognized the child as her
boy whom she had mourned as lost nearly
twenty-four hours. The preservation of
Moses in the bulrushes was not so miracu-
lous as the safety of Mrs. Kings boy. He 
had been tossing about on the waste waters 
in the cabin all night, and was brought to his
mother alive in the hour of her deepest
affliction." This story is worthy of being
repeated by mothers, in teaching the little
ones concerning the goodness and alwise
providences of the great Creator; that He
lives and acts in our day as he did formerly,
when little Moses was born and saved from
the death which threatened him. Had the
child of Mrs. King awkened during the 
night, or had he chanced to roll off the
mattress in his sleep, we could not then
have exclaimed as we now do—happy baby,
thrice happy mother!

EDITORIAL NOTES.

	POPE PIUS Nono has been very ill the
past week. He is old, and his death may
occur at any time.
	THE LATEST agony in New York journal-
ism is a daily illustrated newspaper; and the
wood cuts show all the evidences of being
hurriedly got up.
	THE SECOND Adventists of Groton, Ver-
mout, fixed upon April 12, of this year, as
“the last great day;” but as it wasn’t, they
will have to refix the date.
	INDIANA proposes another “new de-
parture,” by allowing women the suffrage.
Should the proposition succeed where will
Massachusetts progress be?
	A DAUGHTER of President Thiers of the
French Republic, died at San Francisco
during the last week of March, and the
remains were taken to France for inter-
ment.
	AN INDIANA parson determined that his
discourses shall not be wanting in both
rhyme and reason, delivers them in rhyme.
His congregation should escape future pun-
ishment after having to endure such present
torture.
	MICHIGAN UNIVERSITY has graduated
twelve lady physicians and two lady law-
yers this year; and yet some of the New
England States querulously question wo-
man’s ability to practice either medicine or
law. The West keeps in the front!
	WILL ANYTHING frighten fashionable
ladies out of purchasing and wearing false
hair? Here is another “ghost” from Cali-
fornia:	“Somebody sold a dealer in human
hair some hair that had been cut from the
head of a small-pox patient, and the dealer
took the disesse and died.”	
	KENTUCKY HAS a town named Elizabeth,
with a population of 2,500, which spends
$200 a day in whisky; $73,000 a year for
that twenty-five hundred souls, male and
female, infant, youth, adult and aged, to
swallow some ninety thousand gallons of
liquid poison! Very probably they think
themselves temperate people, too! 
	AND NOW word comes from Minnesota
that the “foreman” of one of the leading
newspaper offices in that State is a woman.
There are people still who say women are
not competent to become printers; but the
world has never been rid of that kind of
people from whom even practical elucida-
tion will not remove such unbelief.
	NEW JERSEY legislative corruption bids
fair to outrank oven Congressional Credit
Mobilierism and New York legislative
venality. The question is not, did the leg-
islators sell out to railway magnates; but
how much was paid for them. As the
people elect their representatives they may
thank themselves if they are twice so
afflicted with the same men.
	THERE HAS been a very general outcry
through the press against the action of the
late Congress in passing the increased salary
bill, and making it retroactive so that each
member could draw the advance for the two
back years; and in some instances members
of Congress that voted for it have been
asked to resign. The feeling appears to be,
not that the country should pay more for its
legislation, but that it could do with a great
deal less of the kind of legislation it has
been favored with for some time. It is held,
too, that no act of the last Congress became
it half so well as that of dying.
	THE WOMAN Suffragists of California
were in convention in San Francisco last
week, but the brief telegraphic news re-
ceived concerning it by the daily papers,
contained so little information on the subject
that we must wait for due course of mail to
bring a fuller report.
	IT MAY be considered sad to record that
a number of women assisted, lately, at a
case of “lynching” at Fort Erie, Canada;
yet the foremost in deeds of daring among
the Paris Communists were women. It only
shows how the gentleness of the sex can be
turned to fierceness by association with
rough and bloodthirsty men, or by famili-
arity with scenes of violence.
	DIVORCE, WHICH was at one time deemed
cause for painful reproach has now become
fashionable, and the lady of the period can
meet with the utmost nonchalance the former
husband, while she hangs on the arm of
the present one. Our grand-parents looked
at things differently; but they had a kind
of old-fashioned faith in honor, honesty,
esteem and love, that is little admired now.
	OHIO HAS a Farmer’s Club for Women,”
the members of which meet regularly to
discuss practical questions applicable to farm
life; such as making bread, best methods of
curing fruit, how to make and keep pickles,
both sour and sweet, how to clean house
with the least confusion, least destruction
to furniture and most expedition, and the
like. This, certainly, is more becoming and
more profitable than the idle gossiping
which is fashionable.
	MISS PHOEBE Cousins, who was admitted
to the bar as a practicing lawyer in Salt
Lake City, as she could not secure such
recognition in St. Louis, is an applicant for
postmistress of the latter city, a Washing-
ton paper says. Why should she not be
successful? Unless, indeed, it may be
shown that her services in electioneering
last Fall were not so valuable as that of
some men whose persuasive powers, or
whisky expenditures, were more potent
with the “sovereigns” on election day.
	DR. NEWMAN, Chaplain of the United
States Senate and noted as coming to Salt
Lake to shear but going away with but little
fleece, having been appointed Inspector of
Consulates at a handsome salary and ex-
penses paid, was authorized to take a clerk
with him at $3,500 a year; and knowing
that Mrs. Newman would also like to travel,
sent her name in as clerk. This was
keeping the place in the family, which
might as well draw the money as there will
be little, if any, work to do. Dr. Newman,
however, is not on general principles, an
advocate of woman’s rights; only as far as
Mrs. Newman’s drawing her salary may go
he possibly is.
	PRESIDENT GRANT has appointed Mrs.
Cunningham postmistress of Provo, Utah,
and we understand the choice is acceptable
to the people, as the lady performed the duties
of the oflice very efficiently for some time
prior to the death of her late husband, who
was postmaster. In connection with post-
offices, we might say, while applauding the
commissioning of Mrs. Cunningham, that it
would seem a little singular anywhere else
than in Utah that all or nearly all the im-
portant post offices, where any pecuniary
benefit is derivable, are placed in the hands
of comparatively recent settlers in the Ter-
ritory; while the old settlers have the
chance to run post offices where the pay
does not cover the expenses, and the work,
consequently, has to be done “free, gratis,
for nothing.” We say this might seem
strange anywhere else than in Utah, but
here, impartiality, or old settlers receiving
paying offices, would be matter for surprise.
	HORACE GREELEY soon followed to the
tomb the woman who had been his partner
in life so long; and now Mrs. James Gordon
Bennett, wife of the other great New York
editor, follows her husband. She died
recently in Germany, and of her an eastern
paper say:	“She	 was formerly Henrietta
Agnes Crean, who kept a small hosiery and
fancy goods store in William street, New
York, when lower Broadway was still a
place of residence. She was a bright, viva-
cious woman, of large frame and great
vitality. At the time of her marriage, which
occurred at St. Peter’s church, in Barclay
street, on the 6th of June, 1840, Mr. Ben-
nett made the occasion conspicuous in the
columns of his newspaper, and afterward
faithfully recorded the birth of his children
in the same manner. Four children were
born to them, two of whom, a son and a
daughter, survive. Mrs. Bennett at one
time wrote a number of letters from Europe
for the ‘Herald.’ and they were distin-
guished by much vivacity and dash.”
	THERE HAS been a surfeit of horrors
during the last two weeks. The Atlantic
steamship, from Liverpool for New York,
with over a thousand souls on board, went
ashore on the coast of Nova Scotia, some
twenty miles from Halifax, and nearly six
hundred of the number were drowned or
dashed to pieces. About two hundred
were instantaneously killed, crushed or
mangled, by a boom breaking loose and
sweeping over the decks; hundreds were
drowned in their beds; many more in their
efforts to escape; while numbers died of cold,
exhaustion, and exposure in the rigging.
One lady, after hanging to the rigging for
six hours, partly sustained by one of the
officers, died still holding on with a death
grip, her clothing nearly torn and washed
from her person, and the jeweled rings that
glittered on her tender fingers, shining with
mocking glare on the hand of death. What
a fearful sight must have been presented!
And not a woman or child escaped; they all
perished; men, only, finding their way to
shore. A mere contemplation in fancy of
the awful scene is sickening; but what must
it have been for the fated participants to
pass through, before death kindly came to
their relief! Since then heavy floods in the
east have done great damage; destructive
conflagrations have devoured property and
life; and disasters have been of almost daily
occurrence. These are signs of the times,
if the world would but open its eyes to note
their intent.

﻿NEWS AND VIEWS.
		  —
  NEW YORK blacksmiths are striking—for
wages. 
  THE Democratic cauldron is bubbling ; it
will boil at Baltimore on the 9th proximo.
  PRESIDENT Grant was nominated for re-
election by the Philadelphia Convention.
Will he be elected ?
   ADMIRERS of John Brown are anxious to
erect a monument to his memory at Har-
per’s Ferry or Charleston. Is he likely to
be soon forgotten in those places ?
  MISS Alice Ingham, of Boston, is making
a fortune at Buenos Ayres, having estab-
lished a wholesale millinery store. When
all the milliners have made their fortunes,
it is to be hoped they will begin making
head-dresses of a more common-sense style
than many that are now made and worn.
OVER a thousand American newspapers
collapsed last year, a single fact that shows
how prone our “independent sovereigns”
are to rush into the using of printer’s ink
without a secure footing on which to stand.
More than two millions of money, it is cal-
culated, was lost by the experimental enter-
prises.
 SIX Presidential tickets are already in the
field for the coming election, with several
conventions in prospective ; and Utah has
not a single effective voice in the exciting
contest. Six Presidential elections have
been held since Utah was settled, and yet
she has never cast a vote for President.
 TO BANDY the lie in the halls of Congress,
like street brawlers in a quarrel, as was done
in the House of Representatives on last
Monday night, is a poor way to manifest the
dignity which should characterize the leg-
islators of a great country. If the men who
lead in politics and aspire to govern the
people, would first learn to govern them-
selves, they would be better fitted for the
high duties which they are so anxious to as-
sume.
  AN ILLINOIS clerk refuses to marry be-
cause the women have so many faults. His
objections to one are :	“She walks per-
fectly natural, just as women used to do
years ago, and wears her own hair, like
some old grandmother, which would make
her appear so awkward among his fashiona-
ble friends!” Poor young man ! He’s too
wise for this mad world ! But, could he not
make a choice from some modern bundle of
fashionable millinery ?
  CONGRESS adjourned on Monday last, the
10th inst. The women of Utah are under
obligations to it, that it declined to pass
Representative Wheeler’s bill to deprive
them of the ballot, which the act of the Ter-
ritorial Legislature and the signature of Hon.
S. A. Mann conferred upon them. Had the
women of Utah done violence to their feel-
ings and intelligence, and at the polls voted
against the men they have proved and know
to be worthy of confidence, there would
have been no bill presented to Congress to
deprive them of the elective franchise.
AT THE International Typographical
Union, which met in convention, in Rich-
mond, Virginia, last week, an amendment
to the constitution of the organization was
[end of column; start of new column]
offered, discountenencing the chartering of
female unions, and striking out the word
“male.” Under the rules this will lay over
for a year. It is discreditable to the intelli-
gence of the typographical profession that
such distinctions should exist. Where wo-
men make as good compositors as men they
should enjoy all the rights and privileges
possessed by male compositors, and the ef-
fort to continue a class distinction because
of sex is as unjust as it is ungallant.
  IT IS again announced that Dr. Living-
stone, the indomitable Scotchman who has
made his memory famous through coming
time by his African explorations, has been
again heard from and is safe. He has fur-
nished a large fund of material for small
wits that can neither appreciate his daring,
enterprise, energy, nor the love for science
and the advancement of knowledge which
has sustained him in his many perilous un-
dertakings. Yet he has done more to pene-
trate the mysteries of that hitherto unknown
world, which lies buried in the vast African
continent, than any other man, living or
dead, of whom past or contemporaneous his-
tory furnishes any account.
 THE platform adopted by the National
Rebublican Convention which met at Phila-
delphia on the 5th inst., acknowledges that
the claims of those who advocate woman
suffrage are entitled to respect. This has
the appearance of a bid for the services of
Mrs. Hooker and other women lecturers, to
take the stump in support of the party nom-
inations. But, inasmuch as the Republicans
have had a large majority in both houses of
Congress for some years, why is it that the
sought-for sixteenth amendment to the Con-
stitution, to confer upon women the suffrage,
has never been able to command a respecta-
ble vote in Congress that weuld send it to
the country for ratification.  Plausable plat-
forms sound well, but consistent acts are
better.
  Two more Polar expeditions have started.
  Theodore Tilton is for Greeloy “first, last,
and all the time.”	
  Florida will soon have her first street railway,'
at Cedar Keys. Utah will beat her.
The Boston “Peace Jubilee” will be a great
crash. It opens on Monday, the 17th inst.
  “Scissors editors” is the latest and most appro-
priate name for a numerous class of the editorial
fraternity.
  ILLUSTRATED papers arc growing. They teach
through the eye, which is a pleasant mode of acquir-
ing knowledge when the object is agreeable.
CHINA’s religious institutions, it is said, cost her
four hundred.millions of dollars annually; less than
a dollar a head. Why, Christendom pays nearly
as much per caput for religion, and’ yet see how
prisons, madhouses, dens of infamy and general in-
iquity thrive !
TO BECOME acquainted with the “ strong points”
of a dishonorable or disreputable character, which
the principal men of the country have made during
the past few years, a stranger should take in for
the next three months about half a dozen political
papers sustaining different parties.
CHARLES JAMES LEVER, the author, is dead.
With a rare fund of humor, a wonderful talent for
delineating the rollicking character of the Irish in
all its exuberance, and a wealth of plot and incident
[end of column; start of new column]
rarely excelled, he furnished much pleasant read-
ing for all English-speaking, countries,'which will
long keep his memory green.
  TWENTY thousand singers and eight thousand in-
strumental performers will participate in the “Bos-
ton International Musical Peace Jubilee;” and
provision has been made for seating a hundred
thousand people. One clever Irishman has suc-
ceeded in adding a new feature of notoriety to the
“ Hub of the Universe,” and Boston rejoices.
Fearful storms have swept over different places
in the Eastern and Middle States within the past
two weeks, washing away railroad bridges, houses,
fences, farm-produce, and cattle, leaving buildings
shattered by lightning, and killing a number of
people. It seemed as if the Storm King had gone
forth in his fury, spreading devastation in his track.
  STRIKES have become plenty of late, especially
in New York, and the demand is that eight hours’
work and ten hours’ pay shall constitute the labor
and compensation for a day’s toil. We propose
two other strikes, to include two classes of “ white
slaves.” Let seamstresses and editors demand a
reduction of labor and an increase of pay. Both
classes need them, and both, as classes, are harder
worked than are the men of any trade engaged in
the New York strikes.
  JUST now there is only one subject of surpassing
interest in America:	Who	shall	distribute	the offi-
cial patronage during the four years commencing
March 4, 1873. When men were honest and lost
money by holding office, the offices had often to seek
the men; but now they have found a way to realize
a fortune in a year or two out of a starvation salary,
and the number of people seeking offices is so great,
that they could scarcely be suitably supplied if ev-
ery cell in every penitentiary in the country was
prepared for their especial use.
 ANOTHER appalling domestic tragedy occurred in
New York. The circumstances are one Emil
Andrea, a Frenchman, whose wife had left him on
account of disfiguration by small-pox, was living
with another man. The husband went to her resi-
dence to obtain her signature to a paper for separa-
tion. She refused and fled; he caught her on the
comer of First avenue. and Fifteenth street, took
her by the hair, put a pistol to her head, shot her
dead and waited for the police to arrest him. Sad,
indeed !
REV. HUSTON, of Baltimore, was tried by an eccle-
siastical court, with closed doors, for alleged immor-
ality with young women of his acquaintance. lie
was acquitted, and proposes to bring suit for libel
against the “Baltimore American” newspaper.
Meantime he has been indicted by a grand jury on
the same charge; so that between the libel prose-
cution in which he will be plaintiff, and the crim-
inal prosecution in which he will be defendant,
there is a chance that the public will get at facts
which it is generally believed the ecclesiastical court
concealed.
 FOR jealous and infatuated “ lovers” to shoot
and stab the objects of their insane desires, if the
latter refuse to accept their matrimonial offers, is
become somewhat fashionable. One of the latest
cases is that of a painter in San Francisco named.
Mansfield, who twico shot a Miss Hein, daughter
of an importing merchant, and then put a bullet
through his own body, but without killing either.
William the Conqueror, of England, secured his
wife by seizing her on the street, tearing her hair
—women wore their own hair then—pounding her,
and rolling her in the mud. The modern style is
not so rough, but it is far more fatal. How we im-
prove !

S. A. Mann conferred upon them. Had the
women of Utah done violence to their feel-
ings and intelligence, and at the polls voted
against the men they have proved and know
to be worthy of confidence, there would
have been no bill presented to Congress to
deprive them of the elective franchise.
At the International Typographical
Union, which met in convention, in Rich-
mond, Virginia, last week, an amendment
to the constitution of the organization was
offered, discountenancing the chartering of
female unions, and striking out the word
“male.” Under the rules this will lay over
for a year. It is discreditable to the intelli-
gence of the typographical profession that
such distinctions should exist. Where wo-
men make as good compositors as men they
should enjoy all the rights and privileges
possessed by male compositors, and the ef-
fort to continue a class distinction because
of sex is as unjust as it is ungallant.
It is again announced that Dr. Living-
stone, the indomitable Scotchman who has
made his memory famous through coming
time by his African explorations, has been
again heard from and is safe. He has fur-
nished a large fund of material for small
wits that can neither appreciate his daring,
enterprise, energy, nor the love for science
and the advancement of knowledge which
has sustained him in his many perilous un-
dertakings. Yet he has done more to pene-
trate the mysteries of that hitherto unknown
world, which lies buried in the vast African
continent, than any other man, living or
dead, of whom past or contemporaneous his-
tory furnishes any account.

Rebublican Convention which met at Phila-
delphia on the 5th inst, acknowledges that
the claims of those who advocate woman
suffrage are entitled to respect. This has
the appearance of a bid for the services of
Mrs. Hooker and other women lecturers, to
take the stump in support of the party nom-
inations. But, inasmuch as the Republicans
have had a large majority in both houses of
Congress for some years, why is it that the
sought-for sixteenth amendment to the Con-
stitution, to confer upon women the suffrage,
has never been able to command a respecta-
ble vote in Congress that weuld send it to
the country for ratification. . Plausable plat-
forms sound well, but consistent acts are
better.
Two more Polar expeditions have started.
Theodore Tilton is for Greeley “first, last,
and all the time.”	
Florida will soon have her first street railway,
at Cedar Keys. Utah will beat her.
The Boston “Peace Jubilee” will be a great
crash. It opens on Monday, the 17th inst.
“Scissors editors” is the latest and most appro-
priate name for a numerous class of the editorial
fraternity.

through the eye, which is a pleasant mode of acquir-
ing knowledge when the object, is agreeable.
CHINA’s religious institutions, it is said, cost her
four hundred millions of dollars annnally; less than
a dollar a head. Why, Christendom pays nearly
as much per caput for religion, and yet see how
prisons, madhouses, dens of infamy and general in-
iquity thrive!
To become acquainted with the “ strong points”
of a dishonorable or disreputable character, which
the principal men of the country have made during
the past few years, a stranger should take in for
the next three months about half a dozen political
papers sustaining different parties.
Charles James Lever, the author, is dead.
With a rare fund of humor, a wonderful talent for
delineating the rollicking character of the Irish in;
all its exuberance, and a wealth of plot and incident
rarely excelled, he furnished much pleasant read-
ing for all English-speaking, countries, which will
long keep his memory green.

strumental performers will participate in the “Bos-
ton International Musical Peace Jubilee;” and
provision has been made for seating a hundred
thousand people. One clever Irishman has suc-
ceeded in adding a new feature of notoriety to the
“Hub of the Universe,” and Boston rejoices.
Fearful storms have swept over different places
in the Eastern and Middle States within the past
two weeks, washing away railroad bridges, houses,
fences, farm-produce, and cattle, leaving buildings
shattered by lightning, and killing a number of
people. It seemed as if the Storm King had gone
forth in his fury, spreading devastation in his track.
Strikes have become plenty of late, especially
in New York, and the demand is that eight hours’
work and ten hours’ pay shall constitute the labor
and compensation for a day’s toil. We propose
two other strikes, to include two classes of “ white
slaves.” Let seamstresses and editors demand a
reduction of labor and an increase of pay. Both
classes need them, and both, as classes, are harder
worked than arc the men of any trade engaged in
the New York strikes.
Just now there is only one subject of surpassing
interest in America:	Who	shall	distribute	the offi-
cial patronage during the four years commencing
March 4, 1873. When men were honest and lost
money by holding office, the offices had often to seek
the men; but now they have found a way to realize
a fortune in a year or two out of a starvation salary,
and the number of people seeking offices is so great,
that they could scarcely be suitably supplied if ev-
ery cell in every penitentiary in the country was
prepared for their especial use.
Another appalling domestic tragedy occurred in
New York. The circumstances are one Emil
Andrea, a Frenchman, whose wife had left liim on
account of disfiguration by small-pox, was living
with another man. The husband went to her resi-
dence to obtain her signature to a paper for separa-
tion. She refused and fled; he caught her on the
comer of First avenue. and Fifteenth street, took
her by the hair, put a pistol to her head, shot her
dead and waited for the police to arrest him. Sad,
indeed !
Rev. Huston, of Baltimore, was tried by an eccle-
siastical court, with closed doors, for alleged immor-
ality with young women of his acquaintance. He
was acquitted, and proposes to bring suit for libel
against the “Baltimore American” newspaper.
Meantime he has been indicted by a grand jury on
the same charge; so that between the libel prose-
cution in which he will be plaintiff, and the crim-
inal prosecution in which he will he defendant,

which it is generally believed the ecclesiastical court
concealed.

and stab, the objects of their insane desires, if the
latter refuse to accept their matrimonial offers, is
become somewhat fashionable. One of the latest
cases is that of a painter in San Francisco , named.

of an importing merchant, and then put a bullet
through his own body, but without killing either.
William the Conqueror, of England, secured his
wife by seizing her on the street, tearing her hair
—women wore their own hair then—pounding her,
and rolling her in the mud. The modern style is
not so rough, but it is far more fatal. How we im-
prove !

['[WomansExponent | Vol20 | 1891 | March | Issue: 17 | Title: IN RURAL ENGLAND. | Author: Unknown]

IN RURAL ENGLAND.

X.
	WEARY with the journey and dispirited
because of the continued strain upon the ner-
vous system, the young ladies at the Castle
longed for rest and quiet. Under the circum-
stances this was next to impossible. It was
positively necessary that some action be taken
immediately, and so Mr. Burke informed Miss
Conway. "There is no time to delay this
matter," he said to her. "The will must be
acted upon, the time is now advanced because
of the long journey, and my duty as the family
solicitor compels me to notify all persons
(which I have done) interested to assemble 
here on Friday night." This was the ultima-
tum, then. It was in the evening of Saturday
he had come up from London, and he lost no
time in stating his errand, and he spoke with
authority. "Miss Conway," he continued,
"you will please make this matter plain to her
ladyship, and meantime see that preparations
are made for the entertainment of Sir Edward's
relatives, who will arrive probably a day or
two prior to the date mentioned.
	Miss Conway was mentally glad that there
was something really necessary to be done, other
wise she would brood over the declaration of
Dr. Bland. Nothing further had been said, and 
the self-contained young girl did not know
what was wisest to say or do were he to speak
out again. She had thought over all the ladies
she knew, and wondered if there were one
among them, who could give her safe advice,
and would she take it if it were given? She
was even more helpless than her cousin, for she
could lean upon her, and knew too that she
wanted, while poor Jane was not at all confi-
dent that it would be best for her to accept Dr.
Bland's offer of marriage. She had looked
forward to a life of single blessedness and toil
in the house of some relative, latterly her
uncle Edward's, or as governess, or companion
to some gentlewoman. This had been the extent
of her ambition, except the aspiration to do
right for the love of principle. This seemed
interwoven in her very nature, and an inherit-
ance she clung to tenaciously. And this very
idea made her shrink from  marriage with a
man whom she felt no special preference or
attraction for, that it would be wronging him
and above all wronging her own acute sense of
honor and uprightness. "If I only knew what
was right," she often said when alone. She had
not yet learned that the Lord would guide and
direct her if she sought aright.
	Faithful and true she knew she could be,
even if her marriage should be an unhappy
one. That did not trouble her. She was not
thinking of happiness as the highest good. She
fretted less about her cousin than she would
have done for her own affairs was more puzzl-
ing. When Anna talked to her she gave her
sound advice, and reasoned like one learned in
the art of love-making. Maitland Walton had
kept away from the castle and behaved like a
hero, and she admired his manliness, though
his sweetheart thought it cruel and unkind.
He was waiting patiently to see what turn
affairs would take, or in other words to know
the purport of Sir Edward's last will and tes-
tament. His mother spared no pains in inter-
esting him; she had friends visiting at the Hall
and there was always some amusement—
music, dancing, riding, and the hours flew
rapidly by. Lady Walton had called soon
after their return, and for the first time then
met Lady Anna. Her pale complexion, fair
hair, and delicate figure, with the deep mourn-
ing in which she was dressed, impressed Lady
Walton deeply. "How beautiful she is," was
her first exclamation to her son on his return
home after the interview, and the young man
added, "How could old Sir Edward have

[Column 2]

such a daughter?" and Lady Walton drew
herself up to her full height and looking him
steadily in the eye said, "My son, never let
such an expression pass your lips again con-
cerning her father," and she turned away, that
he might not see her emotion; for though
years had come and gone she had never quite
conquered her own heart, and now that he was
dead, he seemed nearer; that is, she felt it
would not be so very wrong if she mourned for
him as a dead friend. While he was living
she would not dare allow her feelings to con-
trol her in the least. The son was entirely
ignorant of the cause of the estrangement be-
tween the two families. He wondered why his
mother had any interest in such a disagree-
able old fellow as the Baronet had been re-
puted to be; he could not realize, not knowing
his heart history, that he had once been a fine
specimen of one of the old aristocratic, titled
families, whose forefathers had been famous in
English chronicles. But his mother knew
these facts, and what had brought about the
change and made him old before his time. If
she had not visited him that night on her son's
account, she too would have been ignorant of
many things that now seemed plain, things
over which she had brooded in the far distant
past, that had been unfolded in the conversa-
tion with her former suitor, and she could not
help feeling that fate had brought about that
meeting, that some explanation might be made
before he passed away.
	So it was all right, notwithstanding the
embarrassment she had felt, and a sort of
consciousness that she ought not to have sought
him even to intercede for her son. And yet
she had often said in a dreamy way, "It must
be right to know at last that I wronged him
unknowlingly." Lady Walton, like Miss Con-
way, had no one to counsel with; she could
never explain to her son that the father of the
girl he loved had been her own sweetheart and
admirer, and that through all the years he had
lived since then he had carried her image in
his heart of hearts, and so, like Jane Conway,
she kept silence. But her cheek grew paler,
and she cared less for the gay parties where she
had been the gayest of the gay. The death of 
the Baronet could not outwardly affect her;
that is, she was not permitted, according to
conventionalities, to mourn for him; but her
husband noticed a change in her manner, and
frequently found her in tears, and would ten-
derly inquire the cause. She always answered
him in monosyllables, and he grew to think at
last that she was falling into a state of melan-
choly, and proposed more pleasure and gay
society; it was the only remedy he knew. How
could he think she could look back thirty years
and mourn inordinately a summer pastime; so
little do men understand the hearts of women.
His business occupations, looking after a large
estate, left him little time to reflect seriously,
and he had never troubled much about Helen,
except to give her everything a lady should
have to move in the best society. He had
allowed her unbounded liberty, and loved her
in his own fashion, as men do the one to whom
their name is given and their honor plighted.
He looked upon himself as a model husband;
and in some respects he was; still Lady Wal-
ton had longings unsatisfied, and now that she
had come to know the deception practised
upon her that had changed her whole life; she
reviewed the past with some degree of bitter-
ness. "For Maitland's sake I must go into
society," she said one day to a friend. "And
why for his sake? Are you growing old; you
do not look it; you are far more beautiful now
in your full matronly maturity than the young
misses who throng your parlors, or the balls
and parties given in London society?" I can-
not comprehend you of late; you are changed.
Is it that Italian whose fancy for you and your
music is so shockingly absurd, makes you want

[Column 3]

to withdraw from the circle in which you are
the brightest ornament?"
	"No! no, indeed; I am out of spirits, and at
my time of life one should turn her attention to
something more enduring."
	"Lady Walton," said her friend, "if I pos-
sessed your magnetism and power of fascina-
tion I do not think I should ever tire of the
world." You may think differently some day,
my dear Mrs. Emery. I am weary of attention
and of the small talk one hears. I long for ex-
pansion of soul and intellect.
	"I am surprised at your ambition. Fancy,
for instance, Lady Walton, the belle of London
society, termed 'blue stocking.'"
	"Not yet; I have not the talent; but if I had
I would go into seclusion, take a villa away
from here, avoid society, and write a book. I
have the taste for it but not the ability. It
must require originality, feeling, experience
and power of expression."
	"And do you  mean to say you have none of
these?"
	"Oh, perhaps some depth of feeling and a
little experience, but not originality, nor any
practical power to put thoughts to use."
	"I imagine you have seen more than one
love affair that would do to write up in a ro-
mance." "One cannot be in society without
seeing these things; but I have shut my eyes
when I was likely to see too much." "That
might be a good plan; everyone is not so dis-
creet, but seriously, Lady Walton, I cannot
comprehend the change in you."
	"Indeed, my friend, you surprise me; I must
interest myself in something; I am determined
not to live such a useless life; it only brings
bitterness and 'Dead Sea Apples' as its recom-
pense."
	"Bravely spoken, Lady Walton, and I will
join you when you have found a cure for
ennui."
	It was true that Lady Walton was changed.
She longed to go to the Castle and see the two
young women, and she had an idea that if Jane
Conway were like her mother she would be in-
valuable as a friend. If she had but known
that on that fateful night, the Baronet had
confided in his neice and told her of his dis-
appointment and the treachery that had
separated him from her, she might have
gathered courage to go and confide in her, now
when she felt such great need of solace. But
she did not know, and time went on. Miss
Conway had superintended all necessary
arrangements for the coming guests, and thus
time passed quickly. There was much to be
done; many of the rooms had not been used,
only occassionally for some sportsmen who had
come down from London for the shooting, and
no lady's touch had given the apartments the
appearance of home. Now the cleaning had
been thorough, and dilapidated as the old
place was inside it was sweet and wholesome
with a genuine air of home.
	A day or two beforehand (of the appointed
Friday) a lumbering old coach was driven into
the yard by a queer coachman, who seemed to
make himself quite at ease, and out came such
bundles and wraps, and finally at last a very
large old lady, with a stout cane, and more
reticules than she could manage. Miss Conway
was sent for and met her in the hall, when she
addressed her sharply: "You are Lady Anna,
I presume, Edward's daughter; you're not a
beauty though, by any means; your mother was
a rare one, golden hair and such eyes;" and
Jane could not get a chance to tell her she
was mistaken, she rattled on at such speed.
She finally succeeded in getting her up stairs,
her maid following with wraps, etc. When
she had taken off some of her outside wraps
Jane ventured to ask her if she were Sir Ed-
ward's aunt, to which she proudly replied she
was Mrs. Judith Baymore, and his father's
own sister. "Are my daughters not here yet?"


she asked. "Well, they'll be in time. Are
their rooms ready?" and as she put this ques-
tion she looked around the room into which
she had been ushered, which was scrupulously
neat, and a good hardwood fire burning on the
andirons. Seeming to recognize that the room
was comfortable, she sat down in the great arm
chair by the fire with a certain air of satisfac-
tion. "Aunt Judith," said Jane, "you mistook
me for my cousin; I am not Lady Anna; shall
send up your supper?" "Who are you, then,
to call me aunt?" "I am Sir Edward's niece,
Jane Conway." "And you are installed here
as one of the family; I was not aware of this;
how long have you been at the Castle?" "Ever
since my mother's death and burial." "Strange
he never let me know." "Did you not know of
my mother's illness and death?" "I think I
heard of her death, but never knew the par-
ticulars; she kept herself aloof from all her
family, and we never troubled about it."
	Jane turned away; Aunt Judith had opened
a wound; she could not let her go on, and in-
quired again should she send up supper?
	"Yes, Jane, and tell your cousin Anna to
come and pay her respects to her father’s aunt,
if she's not too haughty." "My cousin is not
proud, and has no such nonsense about her; she
is too innocent to have such a thought," and
away flew Jane with her own ideas distinctly
formed concerning the new comer. Before
midnight all those expected had arrived and
were generously provided for, and Jane and
Anna had talked over their relatives and passed
upon them, before retiring for the night.
