﻿IN RURAL ENGLAND.
VII.
	The morning dawned dull and heavy, after
that eventful evening when Lady Walton, the
proud aristocrat had stolen into the grounds
of her former lover, and for many years bitter
enemy, to intercede for her darling son at his
particular request. The young man believed
that had it not been for the interposition of
his sweetheart’s prudish cousin all would have
gone on well with him but she had raised an
[Column 2]
insurmountable barrier, had watched her
cousin, followed her wanderings in the green
fields and shady groves, and had intercepted
the letters, and closed the doors of the old
castle against him. Something must be done
he was in despair, he appealed to his mother
for he knew full well her intense love and de-
votion to him would give her the necessary
courage "to beard the lion in his den;" but he
knew not what he was asking, he was not
aware when he entreated her to go to Sir Ed-
ward and use her persuasive eloquence, of the
hatred between the two families;he had supposed
that the Baronet's idiosyncracies were the cause
of the estrangement between Sir Edward and
his neighbors, as none of the gentle folks round
about were on intimate terms at the Castle.
Sir Edward and his daughter lived in such ex-
clusive retirement that little was known of his
home life.
	When Lady Walton decided to go and ask
Sir Edward's permission for her son
to visit there as Lady Anna's suitor, she felt
no apprehension of her clandestine visit being
discovered,she knew her own purity of motive,
and her husband was away on business, and
was not expected to return for some days, and
she had no forebodings of evil on his account.
In fact though he had been very jealous the
first few years of their married life. he had
never had the slightest occasion to mistrust
her fidelity in thought, word or deed.
	Lady Walton gave her orders to the coach-
man and he drove rapidly across the bleak
strip of country that lay between the estate of
Sir Edward and her own, on that night after
the painful and embarrassing interview, and
reached the Hall almost immediately after her
husband's unexpected return; in fact she had
noticed a carriage a short distance from their
gateway, but had not observed it closely, so
much was she absorbed in her own thoughts;
she was therefore much disconcerted when on
entering the hallway she found a gentle-
man's traveling bag and wraps, and was met
by her maid, who informed her that his lord-
ship had just arrived and finding her from
home, had inquired whither she had gone, and
was met by the reply, that they did not know;
he had found the young master and questioned
him. Lady Walton was in a quandary,
'what had Maitland told his father how could
she ascertain?" but she did not have long to
consider, for the impatient husband having
heard the carriage wheels sent word for her to
come up to his room, she threw off the cloak
and vail she had worn as a sort of disguise to
conceal her features,and hurriedly entered her
husband's presence, not like one who feared
him, but with a face beaming with smiles and
greeting him most affectionately said, "Dear
Henry how delighted I am to see you, I was
thinking how lonely I should be here tonight,
and now all my fears are dispelled, but are
you not well, that you have come so soon?"
Lord Walton had been uncomfortable and in
fact annoyed when he found his wife out on
his coming home, as most men are, for how-
ever uncertain the coming may be, men always
anticipate the wife waiting for them, and she
had entirely disarmed him of any complaining
by her sweet manner and self-possession. All
his misapprehensions vanished and he
answered her question as he would have done
had he found her sitting alone with her needle-
work.
	Later on in the evening he noticed how


[Column 1]

quiet she seemed and taking her in his arms
he looked into her face and said "Helen have
you been crying, your eyes are heavy and
swollen, is there anything the matter? Are
you not glad I am safe at home once more?,'
She answered him confidently, "Yes Henry I
am truly glad you are here again in our own
happy home-nest, I think I am not very well,
your coming is indeed opportune, for I am in
some anxiety about our boy, he is restless and
dissatisfied and needs a change." The father
loved his son with a pride as great, and as deep
as such a man could, but it was the pride of
name, of rank of honors, not the tender love
that seeks the true happiness of the beloved
object.
	He knew that in him the hope of succession
to title, wealth and estates centered when he
should pass away. Like Sir Edward with
Anna he thought him still a child, and now
when his mother had spoken of him; he laughed,
"foolish, fond, over fond mother," he said, "why
the boy is well I have seen him tonight, and
strange enough now I remember he spoke
similarly of you, said you needed a change,
was restless, had gone for a drive across the
country, and when I asked why he had al
lowed you to go alone, replied it was your
own wish to go unattended. It struck me
curiously at the moment I confess, but when
I heard you come in directly after I forgot
everything in the joy of meeting." Lady
Walton was relieved, she knew now the excuse
that had been made for her absence, and she
realized that she must make some further ex-
planation. The servant announced supper and
Lady Walton ordered it brought up here,
for she was weary and troubled, strive to dis-
guise it as she might. She was glad even of
this little break in the conversation, it gave
her time, and her head ached and her temples
throbbed painfully, she wondered at herself
that she could bear up at all.
	Finally she asked "Henry how did you
find your business in London, was everything
satisfactory? Did you see any of our friends
there or were you too much occupied?" "I
saw very little, did not make any calls, but
heard a piece of news that surprised me greatly
in which you may possibly take some interest,
it was about Sir Edward Hilliard, our old
neighbor over there, I saw his lawyer, Burke
of the firm of Burke and Hemenway, he told
me the story, and all about the family; we who
live almost within a stones's throw know less
about our neighbor's affairs than those at a
distance, you know the old saying. Helen 'go
from home to hear the news,' well it proved
true in this case, for I was not aware the
Baronet kept up such an elaborate establish-
ment."
	At the name of Sir Edward, Lady Walton
had trembled, but as her husbaud rattled on she
gradually recovered herself, so much so as to
inquire with self-complacency, what this
strange story was about, and her husband who
was now busy with his supper, stopped to tell
her what he had heard; the substance of which
was, that as the old gentleman had been so
penurious, he had managed to save quite a sum
of money, but had been seriously ill recently
and had made his will, for he had no son and
was fearful lest his daughter should on coming
into possession of the estate, be induced to ac-
cept the hand of some fortune-hunter, etc., be-
sides added the speaker he never has cared for
the daughter and he wanted some other dis-
posal made of a portion of his means while he
was of sound mind, for it is said, he might die
at any moment or lose his mind, he goes into
such violent paroxysms of passion whenever
he is crossed in any thing; his sister died not
long ago, did you know her Helen?" but with-
out waiting for his wife to reply to his question
continued "she married a poor man, a clergy-
man, I think named Conway, they lived in

[Column 2]

Hampton somewhere, and he brought his
niece back with him after the funeral, a deuced
clever young woman, Burke tells me, a fine
manager, and a capital nurse, and the cranky
old man is quite taken up with her, thinks he
can't do without her at all, in fact he is going
abroad, and intends to take her with him."
	"And his daughter too?" inquired Lady
Walton as unconcerned as possible. "O, I sup-
pose so, but his friends say he has no affection
for her never has had, he was so disappointed
when she was born because she was not a boy.
I can't blame the old fellow though I think
I should have been wofully disappointed if our
first had been a girl, and especially now as
there seems no prospect of any more."
	A lull in the conversation followed and the
supper table was cleared, when the door was
closed after the servants, Lady Walton and
her husband talked a little of trifles, home
matters of no particular importance and
the lady began to feel as if she must speak of
Maitland to his father knowing that on the
following day he was to go to the Baronet, and
that as his father had just returned, he would
wonder at his leaving home. Very little more
was said that evening and Lady Walton had
no opportunity of mentioning Maitland except
in a casual way, but she expressed herself
quite enthuiastically on the subject alluded to
by her husband concerning male heirs; for
although she lived long ago she held the
opinion that girls should inherit equally with
boys; in this she was in advance of her time,
and yet she knew that no such advantages of
education were given to girls as the other sex
enjoyed. She was somewhat relieved when
the hour for retiring came, she excused herself
temporarily to get away for a few minutes to
to speak with her boy, knowing how anxious
he would be to know the result of her inter-
view at the Castle. She found him in his
room pacing up and down as men are wont to
do when perplexed or vexed, and related as
briefly as possible what had taken place and
that he was to call on Sir Edward on the
morrow. The young man was elated with
what seemed to him good tidings, but his ardor
cooled when his mother spoke of the probable
difficulty he would have on account of his
father's being at home.
	"I'll be off early and look after some busi-
ness in town and return that way and it will
not take so very long, I hope that the Baronet
will look favorably upon me, and, there is no
reason why he should refuse me is there?"
"Only you are both so young only children,
but good night, and success to you my boy,"
and affectionately embracing each other,
mother and son parted for the night. Lady
Walton's love for her boy amounted almost
to adoration. He was a fine looking specimen
and his gentle blood was easily distinguished
in his manly bearing and magnificent presence.
She knew he could marry the most beautiful
and accomplished lady in the society of which
she herself was a central figure; but this young
creature who had not even made her debut had
so fascinated him, that he had no eyes or ears
for the many dazzling beauties that thronged
the fashionable places where Maitlaud was al-
ways a welcome favorite.
	Lady Walton was a conscientious woman
notwithstanding her love of pleasure and of
popularity, and she queried in her own mind
whether it was right after all to encourage
Maitland in going farther with this affair, now
that his father was at home; she had taken the
initiative step on the impulse of the moment
as it were because of his intercession, but look-
ing at it now in a calmer way, it seemed wrong;
and she slept little that night in consequence.
	The morning found her still undecided as to
what her duty in the matter was, and she was
ill at ease. She wished her husband would
tell her something further of Sir Edward's

[Column 3]

plans, it might open the way, but she had not
the courage to venture upon the matter her-
self, for they had not mentioned his name be-
fore for years, though their lands were not far
apart. Her husband had asked if she knew
Sir Edward's sister, how well she remembered
her, what a lovely character she was, and un-
til now she had not known of her death; it
brought back many tender memories. And
now she knew who this independent young wo-
man was who had obtained the interview for
her, and that had refused to allow Maitland
entrance to the house. "What will she think
of me, this scrupulously exact young woman,"
she asked herself; but it was all in vain she
had no advisor, she was afraid to go to her
husbaud and tried to justify the part she had
taken in the affair. Looking from the window
she saw Maitland ride into the court below
and dismount, she watched him coming toward
the house, his eyes were downward and his
manner thoughtful, she feared he had met with
disappointment; on the way to his mother's room
he encountered his father, she heard their
voices but could not distinguish words, a few
moments later he came up and before Lady
Walton could speak he poured out to her such
a tale, she would fain have silenced him but
her pride forbade her. After listening to his
recital the mother said, you have not told me
Maitland if your visits to Lady Anna are to
be continued or if you even saw her." "No
mother I have not told you, nor do I know
myself, but I saw her for a brief moment,
knowing I was there she waited for me out-
side the gate. Her father will take her to Italy
soon, and mother I must contrive to go there
too." "Poor boy," said his mother despond-
ingly "you must open your heart to your father
I cannot do more, he must know your inten-
tions."
	Meantime all was bustle and noise at the
Castle preparatory to leaving on a long
journey. Jane was as delighted as a child
with the anticipation of travel in a foreign
land, and Lady Anna would have been, but
for her pining and longing for young Walton.
That morning when he came to the house she
had heard her father's voice in loud tones, and
alarmed lest he was too much excited, had in-
quired of his valet who was with him? on being
told it was a dashing, young man she guessed
the rest, though she was not aware of the
stolen visit of the unknown lady the night be-
fore; determined to see him, as well as to ascer-
tain the result of his coming, she wrapped
herself up, and went out into the grounds un-
observed to watch for him. She was utterly re-
gardless of "the proprieties" her cousin Jane
talked of, and when she reached the park
through which she knew he must pass on his
way home, she flew along under the great trees
that swayed and sighed mournfully, filling her
faint heart with vague apprehensions of com-
ing evil.

The nine lady managers resident in Chicago,
called together by Mrs. Potter Palmer, have
decided to establish a model sanitary kitchen
in the woman's building at the Exposition.
An effort will be made, by a special entertain-
ment or otherwise, to raise the $4,000 neces-
sary.
	"The Woman's Real Estate Association"
was organized in Indianapolis, Ind., a few
weeks ago, and incorporated with $5,000
capital stock. Its purpose is to deal in real
estate, both as rental agents and as speculators,
and it already has considerable property
placed under its control. The members are
ladies of standing. Mrs. Leon Bailey, who
holds a prominent place in the literary and
musical circles of the city is president of the
association.

A CHRISTMAS FLOWER.

	It was Christmas Eve, and the city was all
astir.  The busy throng, with arms heavily
laden, were passing to and fro; gay greetings
were heard from every side, mingled, occasion-
ally, with the piteous cry of the beggar, or the
entreaty of the street wender.  From the win-
dows of shops and dwellings the lights shone
until a late hour.  The bright laugh, the merry
voice, the tramp of feet, the jingling bells, the
rolling wheels, the sounds of music, the occa-
sional whistle from the boats flying along the
river, all mingled in a dreamy, muffled sound,
like the approach of a huge army towards the
city.  It was the heralding of Santa Claus
with his hosts of assistants, about to beseige
the homes all over the Christian world, and for
one day, at least, overturn and upset all the
domestic arrangements.
	This eventful night was to be celebrated, at
least at one fashionable house, by a grand ball,
the day being the anniversary, as well as
Christmas, of the birth of the fair daughter of
this home.  And fair she was; almost without
an equal.  Perfect in form and feature, the
embodiment of grace and womanliness, and
with all, a disposition and nature so spirituelle,
humble and irresistible, that she alike charmed
and delighted all who met her; to see her once
but led to the desire to know her, and to know
was to love, almost adore her.
	She stood, to-night, in front of a table strewn
with flowers, delicately and tastily arranged, in
a quandary which to wear, all were so beautiful.
	“I am sure,” said her fond mother, “as your
dress is so simple and plain, that this bouquet
of various colored roses (lifting the flowers sent
by the suitor she most favored) would become
you most.”
	“I think these gerainums lovelier than any-
thing on white; do wear them, Alice,” said her
sister.
	“It would be better not to offend anyone to-
night and as I cannot wear them all, I prefer
only this white camelia, which my little invalid
friend sent me this morning; surely no one
could take offense at that; and let all these
flowers be arranged on the table near my stand-
ing place for to-night, and to-morrow they shall
be sent to brighten the dreary rooms of the
poor.”
	She did not yield, notwithstanding her
mother’s entreaties, but in her pretty white
costume, with the one camelia for ornament,
all the lovelier for her plainness, entered the
brilliantly decorated ball room.  In the sweetest
manner and gentlest way she received the many
friends who had assembled to greet her, and,
favoring no one especially, but all heartily,
passed pleasantly away the hours.  She loved
society dearly, still could not help but grieve
to see so much display of wealth for the mere
pleasure of an hour.  She announced to her
lady friends her intention of sending her flow-
ers to the sick and poor for Christmas cheering,
and they all were charmed with the idea, and
stripped their robes of the beautiful, costly

[Column 2]
ornaments and laid them on the different tables.
Alice felt very joyful with the thought that
her party had really reverted into a charity
ball, and might be the means of gladdening a
few lonely hearts.  The tables were heaped
with garlands and bouquets of most exquisite
flowers, and Alice was told to distribute them
as she chose.
	In every heart, however proud, is a kindly
chord, which yields to a delicate touch at
Christmas time, if only a player can be found
who understands the instrument, and Alice un-
derstood exactly the human heart; she had
made it her study, and could cause it to yield
tears and laughter, joy and sympathy with the
easiest, lightest words.  She had a faculty for
rendering people happy, and felt to-night that
she had caused her wealthy friends as great
joy by taking from them, as on the morrow she
would cause her poorer ones by giving to
them.
	The morning came, bright and cold.  Alice
started early on her rounds from house to house,
bestowing her gifts, her sweet smiles and
pleasant words on her needy friends.  What a
sweet, gracious Santa Claus she was!  No won-
der the people loved her.  At last she had
finished, all but one place, the home of her poor
little boy who had sent the camelia.  As she
reached the door she wondered how he came
by such a beautiful flower, for surely he had
none such among his pretty collection of window
plants.
	She found her little friend (for such she al-
ways called him), bolstered up in a chair, and
looking brighter and better than he ever had
before since she had known him.  Always for
many years he had been compelled to lie on his
bed, or in the large rolling chair, in which,
sometimes on a fair day, his widowed mother
would take him out.  It was on one of these
occasions that Alice first saw them, and was so
moved to pity by the languid appeal of his
large brown eyes, and the tired look of his
mother, that she ventured to address them, and
found out their condition; their name was
Warner, and they lived not far away in a poor,
but tidy row of tenement houses, and had no
means of support but the earnings of the
mother, who went out sewing, and so had to
leave her boy alone the whole day long, while
she toiled for their bread.
	“And how came your boy to be so sick?”
said Alice.
	“He fell over a little stool when only two
years old, and hurt his hip; at first, when his
father was alive, we had a doctor for him, and
believed he might be cured, but his father died,
and I had no means with which to retain the
physician, and he has grown up weak and help-
less, as you see; sometimes better, sometimes
worse, and in such pain that I could almost
pray to have him die; but then my loneliness
comes over me and I feel as though he must
live, and some day, perhaps, the Lord will help
me, so that he may be healed.”
	And this was their story, which so moved
Alice that she then and there pronounced her-
self their friend, and aided them in many ways,
by obtaining work for the mother, and helping
the little invalid to learn to read and write and
cut out pretty papers, for which she found a
sale, and thus he became a help to his mother,
and felt less the weight of his dependence.
     On this Christmas morning a great change
seemed to pervade the house; the boy was so
bright and cheerful, and the mother sang as


she carefully prepared the dinner.  Alice never
remembered seeing her so before, for she was
always repining and recalling the days “when
things went better, and she didn't have to work
so hard.” They seemed more pleased than
ever to see her this morning, not so much for
what she had brought them, but for the won-
derful news they had for her.  After the con-
fused and loud greeting, from which she was
able to comprehend nothing, Alice inquired
from Henry the meaning of it all, and how he
came to be sitting so upright, and what had
really happened; had they had a fortune left
them, or what?
	“No, dear Miss Alice,” replied the boy, “not
a fortune left us, but something brought to us
which we believe to be far greater and better.”
And then he explained how a young man had
knocked at their door yesterday, and asked if
he might come in and warm himself. “We
were much surprised to see a strong, handsome
fellow, quite well dressed, asking for shelter as
though he were poor.”
	“Perhaps,” said Alice, “he was worse than
poor; maybe he is wicked and robs the poor of 
what little savings they possess.”
	“Oh, no!” said Henry. “If only you could
hear him and see what he can do; he says he is
not poor, but has a beautiful home and kind
relatives and friends in a far distant land, but
that he has been sent out in the world, like
many other of his friends, to proclaim glad
tidings, that he brings news that the Gospel of
Christ has been restored, and to entreat the
people to repent, to have faith and believe, to
turn from the world and gather to this beauti-
ful land where he lives—the land of Zion, he
calls it.”
	“He must be an impostor,” said Alice, “oh,
don’t have him here, he will ruin you both, I
am sure.”
	“You would not think like that if you could 
but see how humble and good he is, “besides ”
answered the boy, with a hot blush mounting
his lace, “do you not see that he has almost
made me well? and he tells us that he thinks I
can be healed; that he knows of many instances
where his friends have caused as great afflic-
tions as mine to be instantly cured.  And, then, 
why would he leave a pleasant home and travel
on foot in a strange land, without means, even
as the Savior and His disciples did, if he were
not good and”—but here he stopped speaking,
for, raising his eyes, he saw an offended look on
his fair listener’s face, and thought, perhaps, 
he had spoken too hastily and sharply, and it
grieved him to think he had wounded one who
was always so kind to him; but Alice never
bore resentment, and thought best to turn the
subject, for she had little doubt still in her own 
mind but that her friends had been taken in,
so to speak, and she brightly asked Henry,
“Where did you obtain that beautiful flower
you sent me yesterday?  Do you know it was
the only ornament I wore last night, and that
I have it still in my room looking as fresh as
ever."
	The boy blushed again, for he did not wish
to tell her that the very young man whom she
thought so poorly of had purchased it for him
to send, but his mother, only too eager to tell
anything in his favor, quickly replied:
	“Why, Miss Alice, our new friend, hearing
Henry raving about you, as he always is, and
almost crying because he had no birthday gift
for you, kindly offered to get him something,
and suggested a pure white flower, which he
most beautifully said, if you were the being
Henry declared you to be, would be a fitting
present, and you would, doubtless, have too
many fancy presents to please you anyhow.”
	This was almost too much for Alice, and she
rose and coolly left them, for she was hurt and 
grieved to think she had worn the flower from
a perfect stranger—doubtless some vagabond—
with such perfect ease, despite her mother’s and
sister’s entreaties.  She had not gone far before
her heart relented to the sick child, though
with the mother she had no patience, and she
turned back to say a few kind words and let
her boy see she bore him no ill feeling.  Her
surprise was very great, as she re-entered the
room, to see a tall, handsome young man sitting
holding, quite tenderly, Henry’s poor thin 
hands, and she stood for a moment as though
riveted to the spot.  Surely, thought she, this
is strange; he does not look as though he needed
anything, and that frank, kind look in his face
does not speak deceit.  She apologized for com-
ing back, and was about to leave again, when
the young stranger gently requested that she
remain a moment, and to her own wonder, for
under other circumstances such a request from
a stranger, in her friend’s house, would have
seemed a mark of insolence, she seated herself
and awaited his pleasure.  He soon told her
that her little friend had made him acquainted
with the occurrences of the morning, and he
was greatly pained that he should have been
the means of bringing discord or ill feeling
where such love existed, and she need not be
offended about the flower, as it was Henry’s
gift, and he only performed the part of errand
boy in purchasing it, as Henry was unable to
go out himself; doubtless her other flowers,
pointing to the ones she had left for Henry,
were purchased in a similar way, messengers
taking the orders to the florists.  How gently
and sweetly he spoke, and yet how firmly; no
wonder, thought Alice, that these poor people
are elated; then they speak of restoring Henry’s
health, I forgot to ask about that, perhaps he
is a physician; I will inquire:
	“How is it that my young friend has so far
recovered, are you a doctor?”
	“No,” replied the stranger, “not of medicine,
but I try to carry with me enough faith to effect
some cures, and touch the souls of a few honest
people.” He then explained his mission, as we
have heard Henry tell before, only more ex-
plicitly and described the power of healing by
anointing with oil and prayer. “But I detain
you,” said he at length, “and your Christmas
will be passed from home; my friends here have
kindly offered me a Christmas dinner, after
which I must renew my journey, for I have an
appointment to-morrow many miles from here,
and must get an early start, or shall not be 
able to reach there.”
	“And do you really travel all the time on
foot? and where will you stay to-night”
	“Wherever night overtakes me, and we walk
most of the time, as in that way we can bear a
testimony and give a warning to all we meet,
that is, all who will listen.”
	“And when will you return here again?”
	“Next week. I hope to re-visit my friends
here, and find them prepared to receive further
instructions, perhaps baptism.”
	Alice surprised herself by the way she had
lingered, but now, feeling oh, far happier than
when she left before, again bade them and her
new acquaintance goodbye.
	She did not reveal what had happened to her
parents, for she knew them to be so worldly, and
she dreaded their sneers.  Already they thought
her too philanthropic, and, as they termed it,
sanctimonious; and besides, she remembered her
own incredulity, and how offended she became
with her poor friends for believing in this
stranger, and now she herself was just as firmly
convinced of his sincerity and truth as they
had been; she scarcely dared allow herself to
think of the impression he had made and the
words he had said, but her soul was full of de-
light, and her lovely eyes bespoke the bright
thoughts that possessed her.
	That night she laid, among some other treas-
ures, that beautiful white flower, and smilingly 
repeated to herself, "A pure white flower
would be a most fitting gift for such a one.”
Alice spent most of the time the following week

[Column 3]

with her poor friends, reading the books the
stranger had left them and talking the subject
over with them.  At last the week had ended,
and all three, fully convinced of the teachings
they had read, anxiously awaited the stranger’s
return.  A day, almost another week passed,
and still he did not come, and Alice began first
to wonder, then doubt, and then, as in her
dreams, the kind, gentle face appeared before
her, and she began to fear that some evil had
befallen him.  At length he came, but how
changed; her fears were realized.  The night he
left them he had failed to procure a resting
place, and many doors had been fastened
against him; all that long, cold night he walked,
and the exposure had given him such a cold
that he had not been able to return before.
Perhaps it was just as well, he said, for pity
had opened another door for him, and in another
home had he found honest hearts awaiting the
message he had to bring.
	It is needless here to relate his great joy and
satisfaction when he learned the effect his
teachings and books had had upon them, but
he accepted his good fortune in a grave and 
reverent manner, and the first thing he did
was to offer praise and thanksgiving to the
Lord for his success.  With Alice he was very
grave indeed, and pointed out her great risk
in adopting a faith contrary to her parents’
wishes; she ought, at least, to let them have the
same privilege as she had herself; they could
do no more than refuse to believe, as many
others had done.  Alice knew this was right,
and she liked him all the more for his frank
advice; she had feared to tell her parents, feel-
ing that they would ridicule her new friend
and perhaps deny her the privilege of seeing
him again, but now she would tell them all.
It had caused her considerable pain to think
that she had deceived them in anything.  That
night she waited long for an opportunity to
speak with them alone, but the evening wore
away without an opportunity, and at last, al-
most in tears, Alice retired to her chamber,
followed by her sister, who had noticed her
anxiety, and into her ears Alice poured the
whole story.
	“’Tis not the religion, Alice, that exercises
you so,” her sister replied, “your heart is
touched, you are in love, and are willing to
believe anything that man tells you, even if he
told you black was white. That’s what your 
philanthropy has led you into; I always thought
you were going too far, and told mamma as
much.”
	Poor Alice, what could she say?  She did not
believe she was in love, still she knew her new
friend held a certain influence over her, which
she could not define, and rebel as she might
against her sister’s injustice, she knew she
would have the same to meet from father and
mother.  That night she offered up a piteous
prayer, for she saw that in her happy, comfort-
able home a storm was rising, and she believed
herself to be the fault, yet she also knew that
something more had entered her life, something 
that, even for them, she could not resign.  In
the morning she sent a note, asking Mrs. War-
ner to conduct her new friend to her home and
she would introduce him to her parents.  They
came, and he spoke with them, but, alas! with
what results.  The mother wept and wailed, and
declared the fellow had tried to lead her beauti-
ful child astray, and he and his friends, as he
called them, were breaking up peaceful homes
and scattering distress wherever they went.
The father turned them out, and in a very
fierce manner, threatened to punish him severely
if ever again he heard of him interfering with
his family.
	Alice ceased visiting with her poor friends,
and tried to be reconciled to her home, but in
vain, and when, at the end of a month, her
little boy Henry (he could walk now), brought
her a note, she was overjoyed.  It was very


brief, written only to say that the missionaries
would occasionally visit near, and at certain
periods performed baptisms, and if her con-
science told her to accept the Gospel that she
might confer with them through the Warners,
who were now fully convinced, and had a great
testimony in the healing of Henry, which
would be a testimony unto her as well. Then
Henry told her how he had been anointed and
prayed for, and had improved, but was unable
to walk, and the missionary himself carried
him into the stream of water, where he was
baptized, and that immediately after that or-
dinance he had walked out of the stream and
had had no trouble ever since; “And as soon as
we can we are going to leave this place and
gather to Zion.”
	Her life at home seemed empty now, and she
resolved to explain all to her parents. She
had tried to forget her new faith and be the
same as usual, all to no use, and they must let
her choose for herself. She announced her de-
termination to her father, and strove to explain
how hard the struggle was to give up either
faith or home, and she begged him, as no child
ever plead before, to be lenient, and consider
her position, not to judge harshly, but to let
her love them and have their love the same as
ever, nay, she knew that now she loved them
more. Her pleadings and tears were alike
unavailing, and she had her choice, either to
give up her religion or her home. If she clung
to the first, he would have no more of her, and
she might go to her new found friends, she was
no daughter of his. She bowed to his will,
and left her home, not without great sorrow,
for she recalled her happy childhood and girl-
hood, the fond indulgence of her parents, and
as she placed together the few necessary arti-
cles she was to take with her, her heart ached
and bitter tears poured down her cheeks. Her
sister and mother were most scornful, for they
believed she would relent and return in a few
hours, and could not think her so sincere in
her belief as to leave such a home of luxury
for poverty, perhaps distress, but they little felt
the inducements the Gospel held, and could not
realize that worldly wealth faded into insignifi-
cence compared with the light and life which
had been revealed to her. Her father refused
to see her again, but her mother, tenderly re-
lenting, as she was about to leave, held her in
her arms and told her if ever she desired she
should come back to them; she gave her, also,
what ready money she had, and offered every
inducement if only she would remain and yield
to their wishes. This was a bitter trial to
Alice, but she knew sacrifices had to be made,
and that she could never feel the same again
if she remained at home, for she could not give
up a religion which held so much for her. She
kissed her mother and thanked her, and asked
for her blessing, and “whereever I am, dearest
mother, I shall always think of thee and this
last hour, and may your hearts be softened,
that you will receive me home again.” With
these words they parted, and the poor girl
heard her father’s door close behind her never
to be re-opened. How lonely it seemed, how
dreary in the street; twice she turned, as though
to retrace her steps, and each time something
whispered, “Do not turn back; the way looks
dark, but it will brighten as you proceed.” At
length she reached Mrs. Warner’s humble home
and told them her story. They were only too
glad to be able to do something for one who had
helped and befriended them so long.
	That night, towards midnight, when all was
hushed and still, with only the stars to light
them, and only the angels to guide them, a
small party wended their way to the river banks.
A beautiful prayer was there offered, and then
Alice’s stranger-friend, with head uncovered,
walked into the midst of the stream, a com-
anion of his followed, leading a young man
by the hand; they blessed and immersed him,

[Column 2]
even as it was done in the Savior’s time, and
then Alice herself was led forth and received
the same ordinance. She walked from the
water with hope revived, regrets all banished;
she had entered a new life, and her soul was
filled with most ecstatic joy.
	’Tis many years now since that sweet and
sacred night, and Alice has found her a new
home in a new land. She left old England
shortly after her baptism, and came a stranger
to Zion, but kind hearts were ready to receive
her. She never had many regrets, for, after
informing her friends of her baptism, they re-
fused to see her again; only her sister saw her
the night before she sailed.
	Two years after her arrival she became the
wife of the dear friend who had first brought
her the Gospel, and who declared he loved
her and felt as though she would be his the
first moment his eyes rested upon her.
	“Why, then,” replied she, “did you never tell
me before I came away?”
	“Can you not see, Alice, if I had brought
my own feelings before you, that your parents’
claim would have been verified, and you might
have believed me an impostor, claiming a new
faith to help me to win you? No; it was my
mission to teach the Gospel, and your gentle
yet firm way of receiving it afforded me great
happiness, and proved you to be free from
vanity and worldliness.”
	’Twas Christmas Eve, and all was quiet in
Alice’s home; the members of the household
walked with cautious step and spoke in an un-
dertone, for in one chamber of the house lay
our sweet Alice, with a tiny babe nestling in
her arms. She brightly returned her husband’s
smile. and whispered,
	“We will call our little one Camelia, our
Christmas Flower. It was that flower which
first led me to the Gospel, and to you, and per-
haps this little flower may be the means of
leading my family to us.”
	And she was, not all of them, for; the Christ-
mas which brought the little flower to earth,
took to another world Alice’s mother. After
this bereavement the father's heart softened,
and as the baby fingers first began to write,
and sent for grandpa to come, he obeyed, and,
with his remaining daughter, who always had
believed Alice to be right, but had not the
force to follow her, sought a refuge in the home
of the daughter he had so cruelly driven forth;
but he atoned to the little one for it all, and
“Grandpa’s pet is the purest, fairest little
flower a Christmas ever brought.”
	Annie K. Cannon ANNIE

['[WomansExponent | Vol3 | 1874 | May | Issue: 23 | Title: FLORAL HINTS. | Author: Unknown]

		FLORAL HINTS.

	ST GEORGE, UTAH, April 12th, 1875.
DEAR EXPONENT.
	As spring is likely now to put in a tardy
appearance, it is time the flower growers
were commencing work.  Now it is better
to have a small flower garden with a few
really nice flowers, than much garden and
few flowers.  So I will suggest, that each
take a small area of ground, have it well
prepared and plant with care. Water twice
a day or as often as the surface of the ground
becomes a little dry. Say take one square
rod of ground, make it properly level, get
the boys to bring on to this six wheelbarrow
loads or one wagon load of WELL ROTTED,
fine manure, and about the same quantity
of washed sand.  Spread evenly, then have
the ground spaded well and fine, thoroughly
incorporating sand, manure and soil together,
leaving no lumps. Now lay off the ground
and make shallow drills for the seeds, and
cover not more than a quarter of an inch
with dirt; then sprinkle along, about the
same depth of coarse sand—gently press
down, then with a watering pot keep the
ground moist to the top till the seeds are
up; you may need to give a little water two
or three times a day.  Pinks, sweetwil-
liams, antirrhimnus, canterberry bells and
most other perrennial plants do best to take
them up every spring, and plant them in
well fertilized and prepared ground, divid-
ing them when too large.  Pansies and vio-
lets require a half shaded position with
ground liberally moist.
	The smallest sized flower seeds will do
best sowed on the top of a pot of good earth,
and kept in the house until plants are well
up, and then transplanted.  Seeds of mig-
nonette and many other flowers will come
up much sooner if soaked in warm water
twenty-four hours, and better still when a
few drops of spirits of camphor are added
to the water.  Seeds of cypress should be
soaked in HOT water before planting. Nas-
turtian makes a lovely house plant. More
anon.					     FLORA.
	A DOUBLE flower is unnatural and will
take every chance to get back into its single
or natural state.  It is useless to try and
save seeds of double petunias, balsams, etc.,
for they will surely be single the next year.
—Exchange.
	STARTING DAHLIAS.—Among the many
things to be done as the season advances, is
the starting of dahlias. We start ours in
boxes by the kitchen fire, keeping very
moist; as soon as the danger from frost is
over, put them in open ground in a warm
situation; separate the tubers; do not have
more than one in a hill.  Have a good strong
stake to each plant and begin tying it when
quite young with woolen yarn or strips of
cloth. If your dahlia has more than one
shoot, select the strongest one; pinch off all
the rest; pinch all the side shoots up to two
feet from the ground; then allow one or
two branches to grow; mulch with fine
grass; have an old tub, save all the soap
suds and put it on daily and your flowers
will repay you for all your trouble.—Rural.
PERENNIAL PEAS.—Whatever else you
do in the garden this spring, do not forget
to plant a few seeds of Perennial Peas.
They do not flower the first season, but with
ordinary care in hoeing and manuring, the
plants increase in beauty each year.  They
are perfectly hardy, though a slight cover-
ing of coarse stable manure, put on in the
fall, gives them a good start in the spring.
	One variety in our garden is very pretty,
and its clusters of bright, rose-colored flow-

[Column 2]
ers are borne in great profusion in early
summer.  If no seed is allowed to mature,
you can be sure of finding some flowers for
bouquets throughout the season.
	The plants are easily trained in any de-
sired form, but a cheap and favorite mode of
ours is to surround them with hemlock
brush four feet high and set in the ground
so closely as to form a sort of circular hedge.
This can be trimmed evenly on the top, and
at first, is rather ornamental of itself; but
the leaves soon fall off, leaving a fine net-
work of branches to support the vines,
which are soon covered with a mass of rosy
bloom.—Ib.
	A STRAWBERRY grower states that to
two barrels of rain water he put one quarter
of a pound of ammonia, and one quarter of
a pound of common nitre, and with this so-
lution he sprinkled his strawberry beds
every night when blossoming. The result
was double the amount of large strawber-
ries to that just adjoining not so treated.
Try it.


For the EXPONENT.
			A LECTURE,
READ BEFORE THE YOUNG LADIES RE-
	TRENCHMENT ASSOCIATION, BEAVER, MAR.
	13th—75.
MY DEAR YOUNG FRIENDS:
	Permit one who has had a deep experi-
ence in the ways of life, to offer a few re-
marks on this interesting occasion. You
have assembled here to-day to celebrate
your organization into a band of faithful
laborers in the cause of truth and reform.
What greater step could you have taken in
the march of advancement and progression,
in the more perfect habits of life, and in-
terior powers of intellect and understand-
ing, than to form yourselves into a body poli-
tic, for the mutual regulation of your hab-
its, both social and domestic. Assimilation
in faith and practice creates union. This So-
ciety is denomiated "The Retrenchment
Association." The true definition of the
term signifies, co-operation of effort, to re-
strain any tendency to extravaganve, to re-
frain from what is impracticable in words
and deeds: inter-communion, harmonizing,
and agreeing to establish the most wise, pru-
dent, and refining intercourse with each
other.
	If as a Society you exist only in name,
where will be the influence of your excellen-
cy and beauty, which your friends have so
fondly anticipated? I could wish that your
habits of deportment might be so far supe-
rior to those who do not incline to unite with
you, that you could be individually recog-
nized. Let your dress be a medium be-
tween two extremes: always in good taste,
and of material accessible to all, never try-
ing to outvie each other in fashionable ap-
parel, but in affability and acts of kindness
let each one endeavor to be an example to
others.
	I know it would grieve the faithful sister
who presides over you, as well as others who
yearn over your best interests, were they
to see pride creeping in among you. I will
dare to believe that those whom I now ad-
dress have inherited a little sound common
sense from their mothers. That honesty,
integrity, humility, charity and love of
good order, are inborn principles in your or-
ganizations. Never be intolerant, and harsh
in judging. If some of your associates hap-
pen to fall under a different influence from
yourselves, and have ventured incautiously
on inadmissable ground, be not too ready to
condemn them, try by loving words to win


them to walk in safer paths. Perhaps their
parents and guardians have failed to exer-
cise a proper discipline over them. Never
try to appear affable in the presence of a 
profane man; were you to hear the name of
a kind earthly parent spoken of in a disre-
spectful manner, you would feel aggrieved,
should it not wound you more to hear the 
name of your kind Heavenly Father irrev-
erently repeated.  Shun infidelity as you
would the poison of asps.
	Lastly, cultivate the society of the good
and true, of both sexes. Remember this,
that prayer is as necessary to the soul, as
food is to the body, that you may grow in
grace and knowledge. 
				MRS. L. B. PRATT.

WINTER HOUSE PLANTS.

	Who is there among the lady readers of the
EXPONENT, that does not love house plants,
and their culture? If any, they need not trou-
ble to read farther, as anything I have to say
will scarcely interest them.
	But to those who are fond of these trouble-
some pets, perhaps I can suggest with profit to
them, something learned from actual exper-
ience, and not from books on floriculture.
	What is there more bright or beautiful, in
the dull days of winter, or that makes a room
look cosier, than a glowing grate fire, and win-
dows full of blooming plants. I say blooming
because I suppose there are some like myself
(and for them I write) who have gone through
the seige so trying to amateur florists, i. e. of
tending with great care, plants that stubbornly

[Column 2]

refused to bloom, except when the summer
garden was lavish with blossoms, and we cared
bu little whether they favored us or not.
	Now I am aware I ought to have given my
information at least a month ago, but remem-
bering the old adage "better late than never,"
concluded to give it now, for the benefit of
those like myself, a little behindhand. I take
it for granted you all like free bloomers, and
will suggest some that are very easy to culti-
vate, and that richly repay a little care.
	The different varieties of flowering begonias,
the delicate beauty of whose foliage combined
with their graceful flowers, and free blooming
qualities, are very desirable for winter window
culture. The variety of leaf and blossom is
truly beautiful. I have one with lovely bronze
green, pointed leaves, and red translucent stems,
that is one mass of pale pink blossoms two
thirds of the year. Another dark green
rounded leaves and pure white blossoms; still
another, light green leaves and coral red flowers,
and my list is very incomplete.
	They require a temperature of about 70
degrees. The soil I use is moderately rich and
mixed with sufficient fine sand for good drain-
age. Under these conditions my plants favor
me with masses of bloom most of the time,
and although I called them in the beginning
of this article troublesome pets, their care is
one of my chief pleasures.
	Do not think dear reader, that I have no
other plants to offer, of easy cultivation. O!
no. The dear old Calla is, and well deserves
to be, a favorite of mine, for it sends forth its
lovely waxen flower, with but little attention,
save water in profusion, and warmth. I call
it my 'angel flower,' it is so pure and beauti-
ful. And then there are the old stand bys,
the geraniums in all colors, double and single.
Pot them early in the fall in six or eight
inch pots, cut them down pretty well, give
good soil and a moderate allowance of water,
and they will repay you with numbers of blos-
soms all Winter.
	For hanging baskets, I have German ivy
and kenilworth, maderia and maurandya vines,
and ivy geraniums, with the lobelia plant
which is so pretty because of its mass of blue
flowers. And don't forget to try the begonia
as a basket plant. I assure you, they are a
lovely addition to our little hanging gardens.
	In conclusion, water your plants when they
need it. Don't go round every morning with
your watering pot, giving to each and all im-
partially. With plants you have to be partial;
some need twice as much as others, and I have
learned by experience, that a quantity suffi-
cient for one, is death to another. A good
rule to go by, (though there are exceptions,)
is to give more water to thin leaved plants,
than to thick leaved ones.
	You will soon see when you give too much,
as the foliage will drop.
	I have mentioned only a few flowers, but
they are all easily cultivated, and I hope if
tried, will give as much pleasure to you as they
have to me, I think you cannot fail to have
success, if you love flowers as well as I do.
	MYOSOTIS.


', '[WomansExponent | Vol3 | 1874 | July | Issue: 3 | Title: DEAR EXPONENT:— | Author: Unknown]

DEAR EXPONENT:—
	In our last “talk” we were planting the
seeds of flowers which are now well up, no
doubt; so a few hints as to management will
be quite proper and we hope acceptable. In
the selection of flowers, for either house or
garden culture, preference should, in most
cases, be given to those yielding an agreea-
ble odor, providing the bloom is reasonably
showy, or the plant interesting.
	Among those very fragrant, and also
showy, suited for either out or indoor cul-
ture, the double Ten-weeks Stock is fore-
most, and of easy culture. If planted too
thick, transplant to six inches apart. If you
desire to save seeds, leave the single, bloom-
ing near the double ones until seed is ripe.
These seeds will produce again, both single
and double. If you do not care for seeds,
destroy the single flowering when they 
bloom. Of this flower there are great vari-
eties of color; to-day we counted eight
shades of color in our bed of stocks.
The Verbena, with its thousand shades
and colors, is a very interesting plant for
both garden and pot culture,  is easily raised
—generally fragrant and will bloom all the
year round—grows readily from layers, or
slips and blooms directly. Every lady should
have a bed or border of Vebenas through
the summer and in autumn: select the sorts
most desirable and pot for winter blooming.
	Mignonette, (Reseda Odorata,) though it
gives no beauty of bloom, yields a powerful
fragrance and is considered indispensable to
the bouquet,grows well in pots or outdoors.
For winter use should be sown in autumn.
	Portulacca or moss rose(?)—with its sever-
al shades of color, is a desirable plant, in-
doors or out, or for the hanging basket.
Start seeds in a pot, keep damp and shaded.
It may be safely transplanted, and makes a
fresh, gay appearance.
	Astors are in great variety of color, size
and shape; very showy, and may be trans-
planted successfully with care; are better for
the garden than for house culture as their
period of bloom is comparitively short.
	Phlox, (Drumondii) is a lovely annual, of
easy culture and great variety and profusion
of bloom; should be grown outdoors in beds
or masses, plants a few inches apart, assisted
by sticks to keep upright; blooms a long
time, very showy and fragrant.
	Nasturtian, (Indian Cress) annual, plants
to be placed ten inches a part; handsome fo-
liage, flowers rich and various in color; the
seed or fruit when green and tender makes
the most delicate pickles; keep it up with
sticks or brush.
	Ice, or Dew plant, is one of the richest
for hanging baskets, a rapid grower, annual.
There are many varieties. Some native
sorts grow around Utah Lake. Some vari-
eties have a more icy look than others.
They all grow well as garden or pot plants.
The variety generally known as Dew plant,
will grow a yard long if well cared for.
	China Pinks and Hadwigii are perennials,
blooming the first year; nice garden or house
plants, constant bloomers, in endless variety
and gay enough; plant ten inches apart, cut
off the blossom stems as soon as out of bloom.
They are of the very easiest culture and
perfectly hardy.
	Pansies must have a shaded, moist situa-
tion to thrive in summer. They are indeed
beautiful, propagated from end or division
of the roots. They bloom in every variety
of shade and make lovely pot plants. They
like a humid atmosphere, and pine in a hot,
dry one.
	Geraniums, Pelargoniums and Fuchsias
are more expensive and less common plants,
and especially the last named is more ten-
der and needs greater care. It is generally
cheaper to obtain these from florists than
to raise them unless it be from cuttings.
	The Oleander is a tender house-plant and
the bloom very showy and fragrant. The
red and white, most common, may be easily
rooted from tender cuttings about six inches
long. Cut off the lower leaves, and with a
sharp knife slit the bark at the bottom in
several places; place in the mouth of a bot-
tle with stem half an inch in the water;
stand in the shade till the roots are two in-
ches long, then carefully pot.
	Tie Dahlias to smooth stakes as soon as
they get well up, and continue to tie as they
grow, trimming off all suckers and lower
branches.
	The Maderia vine is one of the sweetest,
most graceful and beautiful flowering plants
of all climbers.
			More anon.		FLORA.
	P. S. First apricots and apples we picked
to-day—Raspberries and gooseberries begin
to ripen. You see, woman like, I had to
add a postscript, but I’ll mind I don’t next
time.							F.


']

['[WomansExponent | Vol17 | 1888 | December | Issue: 14 | Title: THE CHRISTMAS TREE. | Author: Unknown]

THE CHRISTMAS TREE.

	What a busy, noisy day it has been; storm
and sleet outside, so that all had to stay within,
and the sweet little ones have romped and
danced about in the noisiest, prettiest way, and
now night has come on, and the pretty golden
heads are resting on the snowy pillows, and the
house is still; no sound but the gentle, quiet
breathing of the children, the occasional wail
of the wind, as he sweeps around the house
corners and the soft dropping of the filmy
snow. I sit and gaze in the dying fire, and
fancied pictures come and go among the glow-
ing coals. How near to Christmas it is; only a
few more days, and oh, how different from the
Christmas's of other years. I dream, as the
coals gleam and drop, of the long ago, and
sweet visions of loved, lost faces pass before
me; days of joy and days of sorrow blending
bright and sober colors appear, as twilight with
its grey and golden tints. Wandering thoughts
and vaguest dreams circling round among the
embers seem to center now.
	A dozen years or more have passed, and be-
fore me is a vision of the happiest, gayest
Christmas all the years have ever brought.
How joyous we were that night, all the loved
ones there, and we sang and laughed and
talked of merry things, and the little ones
danced around and begged for stories of Christ-
mas cheer, scarcely dreaming how the hours
sped, until the old clock tolled the twelve
ominous strokes, and then the church bells be-
gan to ring their merry Christmas's to the
people all around; the hour, the auspicious
hour, when older folks bid each other good
cheer, when the youth try first to call out
"Christmas Gift," and the little ones see visions
of Santa Claus riding over the house tops
driving those gay reindeers with his sleigh
heaped full of pretty things. Hark, did you
not hear his bells? and now they have stopped,
stopped, right at our door; can it possibly be
that he is coming here, and we all awake to see
him? Certain there is a knock, oh, so loud,
pray do let him in? How they laugh, those
older ones, and the little ones, half in fear, half
admiration, and all expectancy, hover near the
mothers, while this great, white-bearded, snow-
covered man walks proudly in, as though all
belonged to him, and never halts until the cor-
ner of the room is reached, where, for two
long days a mysterious curtain has hung, and
now, to the magic touch of this Northern king, 
it drops, and lo ! the tree, the bright, glimmering
Christmas Tree. Oh, how our hearts leap and
our voices shout! What a happy, lovely sight !
The pretty colored lights, the gaily dressed
dolls, the boys drums and sleds, the banners
and balls and ribbons and trains, and oh, did
ever any one such a sight before behold ! Quiet,
now ye have gazed awhile on that smiling,
glimmering, dazzling tree, now hear what Santa
Claus has to say. He must hasten, there are
so many such to uncover to-night, and he
must speed away, but he tarries awhile to un-
loose a few of the gifts from the tree.
	First, our sweet little invalid, with her beau-
tiful golden curls and thoughtful eyes, yes, she
little precious heart, her pretty gift must have;
then rollicking, noisy Onie, that the rest may
hear themselves think; then dreamy, brown-
eyed Belle, that she may wake long enough to
see what Christmas has brought her; and now
our dear singing boy, whose cunning songs
have so cheered us to-night, and so on until


each little heart is made happy and light.
Then the older ones receive attention, and all
are served, and the wonderful man, with his
snowy coat and funny voice, bids us all "good
morning, and a merry Christmas."
	He is gone, gone! the door is closed, and the
jingle of the bells tells he is on his way to other
homes. A comparing of the pretty things, a
little more joking and laughing, some music,
a Christmas carol from our beautiful Louise,
and the little one's eyes grow dim, and peaceful
sleep embraces them, while some one enters
now, not a great white man, but a beautiful,
laughing girl, calling Christmas gift to all
around, and flitting from one to another with
her bright, cheery words. Who is she, this
lovely girl, who carries light into the room as
the waxen candles are fading. who brings such
joy to our hearts on this cold Christmas morn-
ing? It was our Santa Claus, but now it is
only Our Em. What matter that our brother
had to go out in the cold unwrapped. while she
had purloined his big rough coat and covered
it with cotton and spangles; what matter that
the bells were missing when the sleigh had
to be used; what matter that the lights went
out in the hall just when the children were
romping there; it is all plain enough now, and
the happy hour with Santa Claus atones for
every inconvenience, and the bright hour with
the past to-night, atones for the rainy, dismal
day.
	She is gone now, our Em, and others as
dearly loved have joined her, and it is only at
times like these, when we can draw a curtain
over the present and gaze into the glowing
coals and fancy pictures there, that "the tender
grace of a day that is dead" can ever be re-
called.
	Annie W. [-]annon CAMELIA.


﻿WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


[Column 1]

', '[WomansExponent | Vol10 | 1881 | December | Issue: 14 | Title: CHRISTMAS. | Author: [COLUMN 1]]

CHRISTMAS.

	Christmas is coming! Christmas is coming!
is the cry sounding all around. All the shop
windows are filled with such pretty and won-
derful little things, making the children stare,
and shriek with delight. And many times we
hear some little voice say, "I wonder what
Santa Claus will bring to me." At home the
hands are all so busy, and every one is so very
sly, so as to keep the pretty gift a secret until
the merry day.
	Yes, the gay, joyous holiday is near, the
time of great rejoicings and fun-making; the
time when all hearts are filled with love and
kindness; the time when we truly feel to act
by the Golden Rule—do unto others as we
would be done by. The happy time brings
forth many sweet recollections in the hearts of
all. Children recall with the utmost joy the
bright Christmas tree, all glowing with its little
wax candles and glistening ornaments. With
what delight the toys are received, and what a
noisy day it is, with its trumpets and drums
and bells, and all its bright and pretty things.

[Column 2]
How wide the childish eyes open, and with
what gladness the little voices ring as the
bright, shining spectacle of the Christmas tree
is shown to them.
	Yes, such are the Christmas times now-a-days;
but I recall with sweet pleasure the time when
beautiful things were not quite so plentiful, but
the Christmas was just as gay. We had no
great evergreen trees then, all filled with shin-
ing lights and rare and costly toys, but we had
a line of little stockings all hanging round the
chimney corner, and each little heart had per-
fect faith that Santa Claus would bring him or
her something nice- How we hated to go to
bed Christmas eve, thinking that if we might
sit up the great Christmas king would come
and we could see him; but he never comes
where little ones are naughty, so we had to
mind our dear mother, and saying a sweet
goodnight, we would bend in prayer, and then
hush softly to sleep.
	All was still and quiet, and I fancy now I
see the dear, kind heart moving noiselessly
around, filling the tiny stockings with the little
things that made our hearts so glad. Then in
the cold, frosty mornings—how early we used
to wake in those days—each little one would
pop out of bed, and claim her stocking so full
of goodies now, so empty when she saw it last;
with what unspeakable delight we used to
draw out, one by one, the little toy, the nuts,
the candy, all sweeter and prettier than ever
because it was Christmas. Yes, this was a day
when we laid aside all our troubles and harsh
feelings, when we all tried to make each other
truly happy.
	Let us pause in the midst of our fun and re-
joicings, and spend a few moments in sweet
thanksgiving for this blessed day. We will
think of Him who gave us light, who brought
the pure knowledge of Christianity, and of the
One who was crucified to redeem the world.
What a day should it be—the day when our
Savior was born! Rejoice all the world, and
sing praises for this blessed day, the anniver-
sary of the birth of our dear Savior. Yes,
the memories of this day are sweet; sweeter,
perhaps, by being mellowed with time. The
rejoicings of this day are happy, and happy
may they always be.
	A merry, bright Christmas to all my friends,
and may the future be as beautiful to you all
as the dear past is to me.
[handwritten] Annie Q. Cannon	ANNIE.


[COLUMN 1]

', '[WomansExponent | Vol17 | 1888 | January | Issue: 15 | Title: CHRISTMAS THOUGHTS. | Author: Unknown]

CHRISTMAS THOUGHTS.

	The beautiful story of the birth of Christ,
and of the shepherds keeping watch over their
flocks by night, the earnest looking out for the
star in the East, to welcome Him, all these
historical events, and others therewith con-
nected, cannot be too strongly impressed upon
the minds of our little ones.
	To many of the children nowadays, Christ-
mas is simply “Santa Claus,” who brings them
toys and sweetmeats, and the birth of Christ
never enters into their minds; the cause of the
rejoicing is never told them in a way they can
understand. They do not know why "peace
and good will” prevails, they have not heard
the incidents of the birth of the Messiah,
which all this social festivity commemorates.
	"The hallowed anthem sent to hail
	Bethlehem’s shepherds in the lonely vale,
	When Jordan hushed his waters, and midnight still
	Watched on the holy towers of Zion's hill."
is all Greek to them.
	The gathering together of friends and of
families at this season gives it a prestige and
importance that is real to the children, and
they know and feel that the day and the time
is significant, but they know not why and the
lesson is lost to them, that might otherwise be
fraught with good fruits.
	The legend of Santa Claus is a very pretty
illusion, but it falls far short of the exquisite
tenderness of the story of the true origin of
Christmas. The custom of giving gifts at
Christmas had no doubt its beginning in the
giving of gifts at the birth of Christ.  The
wise men of the East, who had seen the star of
Bethlehem, came and opened their treasures,
and presented unto the newly-born babe “gifts,
gold and frankincense and myrrh;” and this
example has been followed—of giving, and the
practice perpetuated from generation to genera-
tion, and it is right and proper that we should
fulfill the saying of our blessed Lord: "Inas-
much as ye do it unto one of these my little
ones, ye do it unto me.” We have not the
privilege of giving to the royal Prince of the
house of David, but we all have the precept
of our Master to teach us to give our good
gifts in the season thereof, and if we do this in
obedience and reverence, and that we may be
like unto Him, we should not fall short of the
blessing.
	The poor are at the Christmas season the
especial recipients of the bounties of the rich--
and there are many who quiet their consciences
by so doing; but is this all? Does this cover
the ground? Is there not something we owe 
to each other besides the simple act of giving!
Christ’s coming proclaimed “peace and good
will,” which does not always follow the giving
of precious gifts; it is often very easy to give,
when kindness and love would be infinitely
more of a sacrifice, and more acceptable unto
Him, who gave Himself a sacrifice greater than
any we can make. Is it not very often like
the prayer of the Pharisee, to be seen of men?
Let us hope not, but is there not a spiritual
lesson for us all to learn by the observance of


[Column 1]
the Christmas season, as well as to feast upon 
the good things of the earth and laugh and be
merry withal.
	It is a time of sentiment, a season of affec-
tion, when we recall the past, and don the
garment of love, in  which occasionally we find
sad rents have been made. There are changes
in our lives that seem more apparent when the
heart is awakened by the ringing music of the
present, and we find "parts unfilled and voices
wanting," and we find ourselves involuntarily
saying,

"Would they were with us still!"

Such feelings will arise even though we are in
the midst of gaiety, and we unwillingly and
unwittingly contrast the past with the present,
and thoughts of the loved and lost, the distant
and the dead" come something like,

"A pall—
And a gloom o'ershadowing the banquet hall,
And a mark on the floor as of life-drops spilt,"

darkens the joyousness and the revelries of 
the merry Christmas time. So many changes
have the years brought, that the words of the
poet come home with a deep and awful mean-
ing.

"A change he may find there, and many a change!
Faces and footsteps and all things strange!
Gone are the heads of the silvery hair,
And the young that were, have a brow of care,
And the place is hushed where the children played!"

Now to me the children are the ones to be
first considered at Christmas; it is the birth of
a child we celebrate, and we are told that the
kingdom of heaven is likened unto a little
child, and also, that unless we become thus
meek and lowly we cannot enter there; but
these are solemn thoughts, that we cannot al-
ways cast aside, not even for the children's 
sake, and tho' Christmas brings merriment
and good cheer, it has also its sober side, and
no picture is complete without it. If we al-
ways reveled in sunshine we would never know
how lovely the twilight was, and the darkness 
in which we grope at midnight, makes us ap-
preciate more fully the dawn of the new-born
day. Children feast themselves at Christmas
time, and romp, and play, and laugh, and sing,
and dance, and sometimes even grow weary 
with excess of gladness and exuberance of
spirits. It is well, methinks, to mingle a little
soberness with all this festivity and joyousness,
and to relate a story that will touch them in a 
tender place cannot be amiss, if it will teach
them to feel another's woe.
	I promised to write a story for the children,
and I am almost sorry that I did, but I like to 
keep my word, and so I will, even if it is
against my after-thought.

	It was Christmas time, and the snow lay
very deep upon the frozen ground, and the 
bells upon the horses were ringing out joyously,
as the sleighs glided up and down the streets
of the city. Mirth and festivity seemed to
reign triumphant everywhere; but when joy is
at its height, sometimes sadness falls like a 
solemn pall, and so it was this Christmas time
of which I write. The children had hung 
their stockings up on Christmas eve, and Santa
Claus had filled them to overflowing with good
things—it was a happy household—and there
had been much longing and expectancy be-
forehand, and wondering among the children,
of what Santa Claus would bring them, and
especially what choice and precious gift would
be bestowed upon the little one whose cross
had seemed heavier than infancy could bear,
whose life had been one of constant suffering, 
and whose patience and resignation was more
than mortal. The little pale, starry-eyed 
darling, with the thoughtful brow, the silken
hair, that lay so lovingly in rings upon it, and
whose gentleness shone in every feature of her

[Column 2]
beautiful, spirituelle face. She was first in
everything; the big, fat baby brother, with the
curls of burnished gold on his sunny brow,
was content to wait for papa's kiss, when he
came in, until his little pale sister had been
lovingly caressed. O, it was a happy family,
there, how could it be gloomy, for there seems
ever sunshine where the children are.
	But a shadow fell that day upon the happy
household, and yet it was as if the loving
Savior came and took into his own bosom the
little lamb, whose sufferings had been so pitiful.
Just when the dinner was announced, and the 
father and mother and all the little ones were
ready, an unseen angel entered, and the light
went out of that happy home, and the shadow
of death fell across its threshold. It had been
such a happy day, and now all was so changed
—a hush—a stillness brooded over all—the 
children knew not what to think; how could it
be that on this day of gladness for all the
world, such grief should come to them! It is
useless to attempt to reason with an excess of
sorrow; not until grief has spent itself is it
possible to be calm. And for children what
can one do? The little transparent hands
were folded, the soft eyes that had looked up 
so imploringly but a few moments ago, were
closed, and the long, silken lashes lay lovingly
upon the pale cheek, as we laid her down upon
the dainty pillow; the pure spirit had gone to
that better home, where no pain nor sorrow
could ever afflict her again. The children put
their toys away without a word; silence had
fallen upon all. The snow fell thick and fast
without and lay like a shroud all over the
valley. There were no songs that night, but
tears and sobs and piteous moans; but those
who realized what her sufferings had been
knew that she had entered spotless on eternal
years, and, "Oh, how much the best!"
	All day and all the long night, and another
day the snow kept falling, and when we laid
the little white figure down, it was in a valley
of whiteness, emblematic of her purity; and
the snow enveloped her grave with a mantle
of beauty. A white cross of marble now
marks the spot where the little suffering one
was laid to rest, that Christmas time; and all
the summer long the loving ones bereft, carry
flowers and lay upon the sod, in remembrance
of her sweetness and innocence, but when the
snow falls, it seems more fitting still in emblem
of her purity. And every Christmas time we,
who loved her so, remember how the shadow
fell upon our hearthstone, and our hearts go
out to all who suffer; all the more tenderly in
deeper sympathy, because of the great sorrows
that have come to us.
	
Nine years have faded since she went away
Nine years for her to live in heavenly places;
To learn the look of blessed angel faces,
Nine years to grow as only angels may.

"I wonder who of those that went before,
And those that followed in her shining way;
She has met there, in heaven's auroral day,
And if they talk their earth life o'er and o'er,

"I think this very morning they are met,
She and the other dear ones who have gone,
In some dear place in heaven secure and lone,
To talk of things they never can forget.

So we will think of them just as they were,
Their voices sweet and all their pleasant ways;
And thoughts like these shall help us through
the days,
Until we go to meet them where they are."

	I fear this is a sad story to tell children, but
it is true nevertheless, and I have told it you
just as the old year is dying, and when we all
grow more or less sober and thoughtful. And
whatever may be said of stories grave or
gay, it is fitting that amid the mirth and jollity

[Column 3]
of holiday time, one's thoughts should be occa-
sionally stirred by the experiences of the past,
"birth and death, marriage and separation, joy
and grief in all their familiar forms," knock
at every door and cross every threshold at one
time or another, but we feel heavy sorrows, keen-
est perhaps, in times of general rejoicing. The
lyre of human passion has been struck on all
its chords," and no life is complete that has
not tasted the bitter as well as the sweet. The
true poetry of life is not all like a summer
dream, but abounds in shadows, mysteries and,
perchance, occasional midnight darkness.
	And now on this the last night of the old
year with which we are about to part, many
tender recollections arise, and we are fanciful
enough to feel the influence of the season and
the time, and while we bid the old year good-
by and stand, as it were, waiting to welcome
in the new, we recall the words of Tennyson,
and repeat them here:

"Full knee deep lies the winter snow,
And the winter winds are wearily sighing;
Toll ye the church bell sad and low,
And tread softly and speak low,
For the old year lies a-dying."
	EMILE.
[Signed]  E. B. Wells


WOMAN'S EXPONENT.


']
