["[WomansExponent | Vol3 | 1874 | April | Issue: 22 | Title: FACTS AND FIGURES. | Author: Unknown]

FACTS AND FIGURES.

	DR. Glenn, said to be the largest farmer
in California sold his last year's crop of
grain for nearly $500,000, gold coin.
	THE consumption of ice has greatly in-
creased in this country during the past five
years. As illustrative of this fact, it is sta-
ted that in 1854, 300,000 tons of ice met the
demands of the people, while now one firm
has put up 880,000, and other parties have
packed as much more.
	BOSTON has been panic-stricken on ac-
count of the scarcity of water, and not with-
out reason, for the water in Lake Cochitu-
ate has been 14 feet below the highest wa-
ter mark, and several inches below the out-
let. The city got water only through the 
agency of two powerful steam pumps run at
the expense of $800 per day.


", '[WomansExponent | Vol1 | 1872 | July | Issue: 3 | Title: FACTS AND FIGURES. | Author: Unknown]

﻿FACTS AND FIGURES.
			—
  THE Wisconsin tobacco crop for 1872 is
estimated at 10,000,000 pounds.
  THE Odd Fellows of Indiana, in even
numbers, are 20,000 strong.
  THE biggest sale of real estate ever made
in Minnesota was that of 31,500 acres in
Todd and Otter Tail counties sold the other
day to a London firm for $80,000.
   KOOPMANSCHAP has gone to wreck at
last, on the Mongolian wave, with liabilities
of $260,000 and any quantity of assets in
Chinamen’s notes, quotable at a cent a pound
for old paper.
  IN 1871 the iron mills of Illinois rolled
40,026 tons of rails, iucluding 2,800 of sheet
rails, and rounded 51,152 tons, making a to-
tal production of 91,178 tons of railroad bars,
which places Illinois next to Pennsylvania
in the manufacture of rails.
   FRANZ ABT works a whole year in Ger-
many for about $1,500, while in this coun-
try twice that sum is lavished in his enter-
tainment almost nightly.  America must be
to him the Eldorado of his dream days.
  THE total membership of the Orthodox
Quakers at present in the United States is
57,105, being an increase of 715 in 20 years.
   AT THE Methodist General Conference it
was stated the other day that the church
has $70,000,000 of property, and that before
long it will amount to $100,000,000.
  THERE are 215 cadets at the Naval Acad-
emy, 39 of whom were appointed by the
President, four are naval apprentices, three
Japanese students, one is from the District
of Columbia, and 169 from the various
States.
  THE American Printworks at Fall River,
Mass., are arranging to suspend operations
for a season on account of the price of goods.
The amount of business done at the works,
requires $2,100 a day to carry it on, and
nearly $3,000,000 worth of cloth is in process
of printing from the time it goes into the
mill before it is finished.
   THERE are over 650,000 scholars in atten-
dance at the public schools in Illinois.
Teachers number over 20,000. School
houses, log, frame, brick and stone, 10,773.
Expenditures for school purposes for the
past year, $7,000,000.
   LEXINGTON, Ky., has nine hemp facto-
ries, employing an average of about sixty
hands each. The average amount of hemp
consumed per day the year round by these
firms, is from twenty to thirty tons, necessi-
tating the planting by the farmers to supply
this demand of about 30,000 acres of the
best land.
   THE following are the dimensions of the
great bridge now being erected between
New York and Brooklyn: Total length,
5,682 feet; length of central span, 1,600 ;
elevation above high water, 130 ; Width of
bridge floor, 80 feet; height of towers above
high water, 268 feet; base of towers at wa-
ter line, 121 by 56 feet.
  THE Vulcan Iron Works, Carondolet, Mo.,
employs 800 men in relays of five hours
each. The furnaces are turning out from
seventy to eighty tons of pig iron daily.
One of them is making pig iron directly
from pure. Iron Mountain (Mo.) ore, to fill a
contract with the Bessemer Steel Works at
Chicago. The rail mill is making forty tons
per day. The largest vertical, direct-acting,
blowing engine west of Pittsburg is here ;
its steam cylinder has a diameter of five feet
and stroke of nine feet; the blowing cylin-
der, a diameter of ten feet and stroke of nine
feet. Its capacity is 20,000 cubic feet of air
per minute.


WOMAN’S EXPONENT.


﻿The ladies of the Congregational Society
of San Mateo, realized from their “Mother
Goose party” over $300.


﻿Ohio has enacted by law that eighty-
eight cubic inches of bituminous coal shall
weigh just sixty pounds avoirdupois.


A Connecticut company is putting up a
factory for the building of carriages made
entirely of India rubber, excepting the axles
and tires. A decided superiority is claimed
for the material over wood.


﻿In 1787 the Emperor of China issued the
following decree: “All persons of the female
sex are forbidden, upon any pretext what-
ever, to enter a temple or quit their houses,
except in cases of absolute necessity.
Fathers, husbands, sons or relatives are
commanded to keep them at home on pain
of being themselves severely punished.”
What a believer in equal rights! The men,
most likely, had as hard a time of it as the
women.


The Massachusetts Labor Union Conven-
tion met in Boston on the 28th ult. Wendell
Phillips offered a series of resolutions, which
were adopted after a long debate, approving
all labor associations, announcing the belief,
expediency and necessity of strikes, but
denouncing violence; advocating congres-
sional establishment of labor unions; declar-
ing that all dividend paying companies
should be obliged to divide half of the net
profits among employes, and that imported
pauper contract work should be dutyable.

["[WomansExponent | Vol18 | 1889 | June | Issue: 2 | Title: SERICULTURE. | Author: M. A. TILL,]

SERICULTURE.
	Several young girls were taught the reeling of
silk in Provo at the late Sister Beebee’s residence
and we sent quite a number of the skeins East to
be worked up. The Silk Association of Utah
Stake emigrated a woman, a silk weaver from
England to Provo; who wove dress goods, and
100 Handkerchiefs were made by her and another
sister in this settlement, and we donated 30 yards
of silk goods to the Manti Temple as furnishings.
We sent to the Chamber of Commerce a silk
handkerchief and reeled and spun silk which went
East in the Exposition Car.
	We are now having 50 pounds of Cocoons spun
which we intend making into dress goods. There
is a great need felt for the proper machinery for
manufacturing the silk, but the sisters of this
County do not feel like letting the silk industry
die out, though we realize all new enterprises
have to encounter many disadvantages, but we
still have faith in President Brigham Young's as-
surances on the silk question.
				M. A. TILL, Prest.
				JANE P. JONES, Secy.
	Brother Graves wishes made public in the
columns of the Exponent that Congress last year
donated $20,000 to the silk industry but confined
the reeling of silk to the District of Columbia as
experimental.
	He received this spring from the Department at
Washington, a number of Official Envelopes and
Circulars for distribution, also schedules to be
answered; enquiring the number of trees raised
for feeding silk worms, what experience and suc-
cess heretofore in raising cocoons, and the amount
of eggs wanted which would be furnished gra-
tuitously.
	Brother Graves made it known through the
papers and received applications for the same
from as far north as Cache Co., and to St. George
in Washington Co. Several persons in Provo
have taken eggs it being fully understood that the
market price will be given for all cocoons raised,
or if preferred they will be worked up on shares.
						M. A. TILL,
	President Utah Co., Silk Assocation.
Provo, June 5th, 1889.


", "[WomansExponent | Vol5 | 1876 | April | Issue: 22 | Title: HOME AFFAIRS. | Author: A. M. Musser, and O. F. Whitney, in Col-]

HOME AFFAIRS.

	WE HAVE received the pamphlet published
by A. M. Musser, and O. F. Whitney, in Col-
umbia, Pa. on Mormonism and particularly
explaining the principle of Plural Marriage.
We would recommend it as valuable to the
reading public of Utah as it brings many good,
and wholesome arguments in favor of that pe-
culiar institution of Marriage.
	MRS. M. E. Randall had a juvenile enter-
tainment in the 14th Ward Assembly Rooms,
Thursday and Friday evenings called The
Floral and Little Old Woman's Concert, Her
pupils who were all quite young did credit to
themselves and their training. The princi-
pal feature was the Zephyrs, which consisted
of thirty pupils, all at once chanting forth the
Lord's prayer.
	WE ARE pleased to be able to announce to
the public that the Treatise on Silk Raising is
now ready for sale. It gives those who desire
instructions, from the formation of the eggs,
through all the various stages to the
spinning of cocoons. It describes minutely
all the particulars, and the information has
been collected from the most reliable sources,
and practical experience of persons engaged in
sericulture. The Deseret Silk Association have
compiled this pamphlet very carefully for the
benefit of those who are uninformed in these
matters, and every item has been carefully ar-
ranged. We recommend it as invaluable, for
it contains the real and positive facts briefly
stated and adapted for use. All Relief So-
cieties should supply themselves with a suffi-
cient number, as they now have the special
mission of silk-raising by unanimous vote of
the General Conference. The pamphlets on
Silk Culture are for sale at the Woman's Com-
mission House. price ten cents each.
	It is now clearly demonstated that a superior
quality of silk can be made of the elements of
Utah. We were shown specimens of the most
excellent quality and colors of skein and floss


[Column 1]

silk, spun and colored by Miss Rockwood of
this city. Mrs. Robinson of Farmington is
now weaving very elegant dress silk. The
loom for weaving ribbons is nearly completed
at Bountiful and Mrs. Wadoup of that place
understands the weaving of ribbons in the
hand loom. There are many ribbon-weavers
here from Europe but most of them have been
accustomed to the machinery run by steam,
which has not yet been introduced here.
	Mrs. Susan Stringam and Miss M. A. Rock-
wood, 13 Ward are prepared to take pupils and
teach them the art of reeling the silk from the
cocoons, at $l0,00 apiece. Persons who are in-
tending to commence raising silk, would do
well to take advantage of this oppprtunity.
	AMONG THE home manufactures, we are
pleased to announce many useful chemical ar-
ticles for domestic use by R. Matthews & Co.
Every article used by them in manufacturing,
is subjected to chemical examination by Prof.
J. L. Barfoot and prepared under his direction.
They have the Bath Sand Tablet which we can
recommend as cleansing, and can be used with-
out injuring the most sensitive skin. Baby,
Toilet and Shaving soap of superior qualities,
also scouring-soap for woodwork and all house-
hold purposes. They also manufacture the Es-
sence of Jamaca Ginger and other essences,
which have been tested by many as equal
to any manufactured elsewhere. Writing fluid,
and boot-blacking of excellent quality and free
from adulterations, also waterproof paste which
by applying to boots and shoes, wet feet
may be prevented and render the leather more
pliable and endurable.
	It is to the best interest of the Latter-day
Saints to patronize all home manufactures.
	We would recommend the Relief Societies
to form Clubs and send to the Woman's Com-
mission House of this city, and get the above
articles at wholesale rates.
	THE WOMAN'S Commission House, estab-
lished in this city under the auspices of the
Relief Society, and called the Relief Society
Woman's Mercantile Association, is now doing
business in all the departments of dry goods
home productions. This enterprise has requir-
ed a great amount of indomitable energy and
perseverance on the part of those who have
been active and efficient workers in the cause;
and the sisters of the Relief Society through-
out the entire city and its vicinity should not
forget to whom they are indebted for the suc-
cess of the institution thus far. It is still in
its infancy, scarcely six months since it was
established, and it needs the practical suoport
of the people, to ensure its future success. We
sincerely hope the presidents and officers of
Relief Societies will bear in mind that this in-
stitution represents their interests; and do
whatever is in their power to sustain it, and
those who devote their time solely for the pub-
lic good without renumeration. Complaints
have been made by women in times past, that
there was no place where home made work
could be offered for sale, and fairly represented;
now let the sisters be earnest in sustaining a
place where all have equal opportunity to dis-
pose of such articles.
	SILK ASSOCIATION.—The Deseret Silk Asso-
ciation met persuant to adjournment Friday,
Apr. 2, at the Firemen's Hall.
	Mr. P. A. Schettler in the chair.
	Mr. P. A- Schettler said the season was fast
approaching for commencing to raise silk
worms, and by present appearances we would
have to begin much earlier this season than
usual, as the trees were already budding out.
He thought the interest in silk culture was

[Column 2]

progressing, as there were a great many mak-
ing inquiries and planting out trees. He had
had confidence that silk could be raised here
for many years, and now it was demonstrated
beyond a doubt, that it not only could be raised
here, but could be manufactured. He strongly
urged that the people plant out trees as that
was one of the most essential points. He said
our prospects were good for getting machinery
for spinning and twisting, and reported that
the Treatise on silk raising was ready and could
be got by applying at the Woman's Store, Old
Constitution Buildings, or of the Secretary,
second house east of Fireman's Hall. They
are ten cents per copy.
	Mr. Smith, of Bountiful, said he advised all
that could to plant out trees and cuttings. The
time would come when the people would wish
that they had planted out mulberry trees.
Silk raising and manufacture would yet form
one of the greatest sources of wealth to this
community and give many people employ-
ment. We ought to encourage our children
in it and by so doing we would be teaching
them something useful, instead of their being
left to wander about the streets in idleness.
He had planted out from five to six hundred
cuttings.
	Mrs. White of Mill Creek, said that her
daughter was learning to reel silk. She had
planted out a quantity of cuttings and was
going to plant out some trees.
	Mrs. Morgan of Mill Creek, was going to try
and raise some silk worms this season.
	Very interesting and encouraging remarks
were made by Mrs. Smith, Mrs. Higgs, Mrs.
Hardy and others, and all felt encouraged to
do their parts.
	Mr. Schettler said that to those who had
children who wished to try and raise some
worms he would give some eggs in small quan-
tities.
	Meeting adjourned until the first Friday in
May at this place, at 3 p. m.
	R. SIMPSON, Secretary


", "[WomansExponent | Vol5 | 1876 | July | Issue: 4 | Title: SILK ASSOCIATION. | Author: ZINA D. YOUNG,]

			SILK ASSOCIATION.

	One year ago to-day, June 15th, 1876, the
above named association was organized with-
out capital, yet, by the blessing of God upon
the diligence and perseverance of those who
labored, and the means appropriated by dona-
tion from different persons in the community,
the Society has been able to pay for nearly all
the cocoons that were for sale in the Territory.
Seventy lbs of cocoons have been disposed of
for reeling; fourteen lbs have been donated to
the Society.
	Seventeen silk pocket handkerchiefs, (a very
good article) have been woven, and twenty-
five yards more of the same material are now
in the loom; a sample silk stocking has been
knit on the Lamb's knitting machine; three
pounds of silk have been made into sewing
silk, floss and twist; this twist is for sale at the
Z. C. M. I. Some of the silk has been woven
into pillow lace. Some of the cocoons have
spoiled by moths and millers soiling them.
By referring to Mr. Piper's remarks in the
Deseret News, and carefully observing, these
difficulties may be obviated.
	A few large cocooneries will not [--] as benefi-
cial to the community as if farmers in general
would devote a portion of land to raising mul-
berry trees, which would give employment to
aged people and children, and would be a source
of wealth, as well as to promote industry.
	Utah is as good a climate for silk culture as
any in the world; samples of silk were taken
from Utah, by Elder Paul Schettler, to France
and Italy, and by competent judges there were
pronounced of the best quality. It now re-
quires quantity before machinery can be made
practicable, meantime, stockings, sewing-silk,
floss, &c., can be manufactured for home use.
	Although few in numbers, as yet, we hope
to see this enterprise prosper, as also all others


that will benefit the community. Hoping that
it may increase in interest each succeeding
year.
	ZINA D. YOUNG,
		President Deseret Silk Association.
	June 15th, 1876.
	The Deseret Silk Association met per ad-
journment, City Hall, Tuesday evening, July
11th, Mrs. Zina D. Young presiding. After
the meeting had been opened by prayer, and
the minutes of the last meeting read and ac-
cepted, Mrs. Young made a few remarks upon
the business, the success, etc. Bro. A. M.
Musser then gave some of his views in regard
to the course to be pursued; said he had 2,000
mulberry trees that were doing well; he an-
ticipated great results. Bro. Smith from Boun-
tiful, a silk weaver, spoke mostly upon the get-
ting of machinery; said it would be very little
expense to procure sufficient. Sister Smith
also made some excellent remarks. The silk
businesss is progressing slowly but effectually.
Some of the finest cocoons are now ready for
use. Miss M. A. Rockwood spoke of having
commenced to learn the silk reeling; she be-
gan about two months ago, and intended mak-
ing herself proficient in the silk work; was
pleased with the occupation. There is now a
very fair prospect for some real benefit to ac-
crue from the manufacture of silk in Utah, al-
though there must necessarily be time to bring
about any great purpose.
	ZINA D. YOUNG,
		President.


"]

Name: 27_conference_church_john_cannon
Representative_Docs:
['[WomansExponent | Vol5 | 1876 | April | Issue: 22 | Title: GENERAL CONFERENCE AT ST. | Author: Unknown]

	GENERAL CONFERENCE AT ST.
				GEORGE
	This year a new epoch seems to have
opened in the history of the church of Jesus
Christ of Latter-day Saints, and they have
held their General Conference in a Temple
built and consecrated to God and the adminis-
trations of the holy priesthood upon the earth.
The two stands opposite each other, were oc-
cupied by those bearing the priesthood of the
order of Melchizedec and Aaron. In the first
stand sat President Brigham Young, his coun-
selors and the brethren of the Twelve and
some others. In the opposite one Bishop Ed-
ward Hunter and some other Bishops. Presi-
dent B; Young opened the conference by giv-
ing out the hymn. President D. H. Wells of-
fered the dedicatory prayer, the choir then
sang, "High on the mountain top a banner is
unfurled." The Conference was then address-
ed by Elder George Q. Cannon and President
Brigham Young. The combined choir sang
"And it shall come to pass in the last days."
	In the afternoon of the first day Elder Jos-
eph F. Smith, and President John W. Young
addressed the congregation, after which Pres-
ident Brigham Young said he desired to preach
in terms not long but loud. In all our deal we
should do unto others as we would have oth-
ers do unto us. The Choir sang "Oh praise the
Lord all ye nations."
	The second day of Conference in the morn-
ing Elder C. C. Rich and Elder Wilford Wood-
ruff addressed the people and Elder George Q.
Cannon read a long list of names of mission-
aries appointed to different localities. Some to
Europe, some to the United States and some to
Arizona, after which he made a few remarks
and President Brigham Young said to the
Conference, that the purer the Latter-day
Saints were, the greater would be the rage of
the wicked but the less would be their power
to persecute. The choir sang, "As the hart
panteth after the water brooks."
	In the afternoon of the second day of Con-
ference, after the usual regular exercises, Eld-
er George Q. Cannon read another list of
names of missionaries who were called to pro-
claim the Gospel in different parts of the world
and fourteen Elders whose mission it was to
study the Spanish language.
	It was also moved, seconded and unanimous-
ly carried that the Relief Societies throughout
the Church take a mission to raise silk and do
all in their power to clothe themselves and
their families.

[Column 2]
	Elder Cannon also presented the authorities
of the Church who were unanimously sus-
tained by vote of the Conference. Elder Frank-
lin D. Richards, and Elder Erastus Snow oc-
cupied the afternoon, at the close the choir
sang "Behold O God, our shield and look upon
the face of thine anointed."
	Sunday morning third day of Conference,
Elder Brigham Young Jr., and Elder John
Taylor addressed the congregation. President
Brigham Young spoke to the Conference on
the subject of union. He announced that the
seats of the congregation would be reve[-]sed
and Bishop Edward Hunter would administer
the sacrament. Elder George Q. Cannon pre-
sented the names of some more missionaries.
Sunday afternoon Bishop Edward Hunter first
addressed the Conference, he was followed by
Bishop Edwin D. Wooley; then President
Brigham Young said "we will adjourn this
Conference from this house to the New Taber-
nacle in Salt Lake City, commencing at 10
o'clock in the morning of October sixth next."
	The choir sung "How beautiful upon the
mountains," the congregation then arose and
sung, "The spirit of God like a fire is burning."
Benediction pronounced by President Brigham
Young asking the blessing of God upon all
there assembled, and the good and honest in
heart all over the earth; that the spirit of God
might rest down upon the Temple, that an-
gels might visit it, and all who ministered
there for the living and the dead might feel
the holy influence of the power of God.


', '[WomansExponent | Vol18 | 1889 | October | Issue: 10 | Title: THE OCTOBER CONFERENCE. | Author: Unknown]

THE OCTOBER CONFERENCE.
	The General Conference held during this month
commencing on Friday the 4th, inst. was one of
the most interesting and certainly one of the
largest that has ever convened. Thousands of
people from the several Stakes of Zion, came long
distances, hundreds of miles to attend, and Salt
Lake City and County also made a good represen-
tation in numbers. The people flocked daily
morning and evening in immense crowds. Look-
ing upon the assembled multitude one would not
be likely to conclude that “Mormonism” was
dying out as has been predicted by the outside
world.
	There has never been greater interest mani-
fested by the Latter-day Saints in the teaching
and instruction given, than at the late Conference.
Indeed the Saints seemed so desirous of spiritual
instruction that it was apparent upon their coun-
tenances, as they sat and listened to the words of
life and salvation poured out by the speakers who
addressed them from time to time. It was impos-
sible for all who wished to gain admittance, and
on Sunday afternoon an overflow meeting was
held in the Assembly Hall to accommodate some
of those,who were anxious to attend and also that
more of the Saints might have the privilege of
partaking the Sacrament.
	There were in attendance at this Conference,
Pres. Wilford Woodruff and Pres. George Q.
Cannon, of the First Presidency, and of the
Apostles Pres. Lorenzo Snow, Moses Thatcher,
F. M. Lyman, John Henry Smith, H. J. Grant, and
John W. Taylor, also Daniel H. Wells and John
W. Young. Apostle Franklin D. Richards who
has been for some time suffering from rheuma-
tism was only able to attend and fill his place
among the general authorities on Sunday and
not strong enough to address the congregation.
The morning session on Sunday was one of
special interest to the congregation of the Latter-
day Saints. After the opening exercises usual
in the regular services of the Church,
	PRESIDENT WOODRUFF
said: I will say to this assembly that we purpose
to attend to our businesss matters this morning.
There are quite a number of stakes from which
we have not yet received reports; hence we shall
not present a statistical statement to the Confer-
ence. We purpose this morning to present the
authorities of the Church. President Cannon will
submit them for your action.
	President George Q. Cannon then presented
the authorities to be voted upon by the general
assembly, as follows:
	Willford Woodruff, as Prophet, Seer and Reve-
lator and President of the Church of Jesus Christ
of Latter-day Saints in all the world.
	George Q. Cannon, as First Counselor in the
First Presidency.

[Column 2]
	Joseph F. Smith, as Second Counselor in the
First Presidency.
	Lorenzo Snow, as President of the Twelve
Apostles.
	As members of the Council of Twelve Apostles
—Lorenzo Snow, Franklin D. Richards, Brigham
Young, Moses Thatcher, Francis M. Lyman, John
H. Smith, George Teasdale, Heber J. Grant and
John W. Taylor.
At this point

', '[WomansExponent | Vol7 | 1878 | Unknown | Issue: 10 | Title: SEMI ANNUAL CONFERENCE. | Author: Unknown]

SEMI ANNUAL CONFERENCE.

	The Forty-Eighth Semi-Annual Conference
of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day
Saints, convened in the large Tabernacle in
this city, Sunday, October 6th, at 10 a.m.  The
gathering was the largest which has ever as-
sembled in the building.  On the stand were
President John Taylor, Elders Orson Pratt,
Orson Hyde, Lorenzo Snow, George Q. Cannon,
F. D. Richards, C. C. Rich, Joseph F. Smith,
Brigham Young, Albert Carrington, D. H.
Wells, and other officers of the High Priest-
hood, among whom were many of the Presi-
dents of Stakes.  There was a good attendance
of the Tabernacle Choir, conducted by Prof.
Careless, and Prof. J. Daynes, at the organ.
President Taylor called the attention of the
Conference, and it was opened by the choir
singing "I saw a mighty angel fly." Opening
prayer by Elder George Q. Cannon.  Choir
sang, "Author of faith." Elders Orson Hyde
and C. C. Rich addressed the morning meeting,
Elder George Q. Cannon occupied the after-
noon.
	Monday, Oct. 7, second day of Conference,
Elder Orson Pratt, Elder Joseph F. Smith
and President John Taylor preached in the
morning. In the afternoon Elder George Q.
Cannon read the report of President John Tay-
lor, Trustee-in-ltrust, which report was endorsed
by his assistants, the Twelve Apostles, their
Counselors, the Presiding Bishop and the Au-
diting Committee.  Elder Lorenzo Snow then
spoke briefly, and the names of the mission-
aries who had been selected for various coun-
tries, were presented to the Conference to be
sustained by vote.  Elder Albert Carrington
made some closing remarks.
	Tuesday, October 8, Third day. - Elder Geo.
Q. Cannon presented the Authorities of the
Church in their proper order, all of whom were
unanimously sustained by the Conference.
Then followed a report from Morris & Evans,
in relation to funds collected by them for the
benefit of the Welsh Saints; also a report in
relation to the Manti Temple.  These reports
were accepted by the vote of the Conference;
after which short speeches were made by El-
ders Brigham Young, Franklin D. Richards,
Wilford Woodruff and Joseph Young.
	In the afternoon Elder George Q. Cannon
read the report of President John Taylor, re
lating to the condition of the various Stakes,
Missions, Temples,Sunday and District Schools,
Brigham Young Academy and Deseret Univer-
sity, Relief Societies, Young Men's and Young
Women's Mutual Improvement Associations,
and referred to Zion's Co-operative Mercantile
Institution as being a stepping stone to the Un-
ited Order.  This excellent document was fol-
lowed by the reading of the reports of sub-com-
mittees in the various departments of mercan-
tile and manufacturing interests.
	President John Taylor then reviewed the vari-
ous subjects which had been brought up by the
different speakers, giving much instruction on
revelation, as well as explaining the duties of
the Presidency and the Bishopric, and
stated that all the various quorums and au-
thorities of the Church were under the direc-
tion and dictation of the Presidency and
Twelve Apostles.  He then made some state-
ments in relation to the future intentions of Z.
C. M. I., and the necessity of establishing a
board of trade, that all the interests of the en-
tire community might be fairly and honorably
represented.  At the close of his remarks he
blessed the entire congregation.  Elder George
Q. Cannon then presented the names of other
missionaries to be voted for by the Conference,
and the Conference was then adjourned until
the 6th of April, 1879, at 10 o'clock a.m. The
choir sang "Te Deum."  Benediction by Elder
D. H. Wells.
	This Conference has been one of unusual in-
terest, and was most numerously attended, the
Saints having come from all parts of the Terri-
tory.  The weather has been exceedingly fine
and everything indicated the presence of pros-
perity, peace and harmony.


']

Name: 28_money_women_wife_woman
Representative_Docs:
['[WomansExponent | Vol6 | 1877 | May | Issue: 24 | Title: WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		189 | Author: Unknown]

WOMAN'S EXPONENT.		189


[Column 1]
					For the Exponent.
PECUNIARY DEPENDENCE OF WO-
				MEN.

	It is much against Madame Grundy's rule
for a woman to earn money. If a married wo-
man manages in any direct way to earn her
own pocket money, society immediately
frowns her down—what right has a married
woman to earn money? Why don't her hus-
band support her? These and numberless oth-
er questions are asked if any woman dare be
brave enough, or has sufficient independence
to go out of the common groove. Men usual-
ly are very much ashamed of a wife who in-
sists on earning money herself, and it irritates
them beyond expression to feel that a woman
should want any pocket money she will not
ask them for, and give them an account of.
Don't they provide everything that's neces-
sary, and do they ever refuse to hand over to
the wife for extras? But supposing they want
to spend a little means aside from family ex-
penditures, do they ever feel called upon to ac-
count for it? No, why should they, don't they
earn all the money, every cent. I once heard
a mau make the remark when I was a wee bit
of a girl, "my wife never earned a cent in her
life, I've kept my family in style by my own
hard earnings and business tact, ever since we
were married, and we've had ten children to
educate." This was said as a sort of boast that
he was a good husband and father, but, al-
though I was a very little girl then I knew wo-
men had rights as well as men, though that
woman didn't seem to realize it, and I began
from that time to soliloquize on this subject.
I wonder who endured all the sorrows, trials,
aches and pains incident to bearing those ten
children, and in nursing and rearing them;
who but the mother knows the toil, the pa-
tience, the forbearance it requires, leaving out
the hard work, the making and mending and 
cooking. No doubt the very woman to whom
reference had been made, had done all her
household work, save when help was actually
a necessity. When will men feel that their
wives are equal partners in pecuniary matters?
Many women could manage household expen-
ses much more economically were they al-
lowed to make their own purchases; their na-
tures are adapted to these matters and they
know what is most suitable and really make
better bargains than men, besides taking more
pains to get the right thing, instead of just
anything.
	Women have been led into resorting to all
sorts of snbterfuges, and "telling lots of little

[Column 2]
fi[-]s" to get what they want, or money to make
small purchases; to be sure they should not be
tempted to wrong doing even through unjust
or unkind treatment, but such is the bent of 
human nature. Wives feel they have a right
to a certain portion of the means which the
husband earns, if not by right of wifehood, for
cooking his meals, washing his clothes and be-
ing a domestic servant generally, or "maid of
all work," and "by hook or crook" there are
some who will have it however niggardly the
husband may be with them, or however tight
a string he may draw on his purse. Women
have different methods of getting what they
want, some by coaxing and wheedling, some
by tears and entreaties, and others afraid of an
open rupture, or of being denied will accom-
plish their purpose in a way which actually
degrades them in their own eyes, because they
feel it to be morally wrong, but excuse them
selves by thinking there was no other alterna-
tive. No one need think that married women
have time at their disposal, or inclination to go
and earn money if there were no need of it,
and therefore Madame Grundy should be si-
lent on this score at least. The best of hus-
bands is apt to "put on airs" when the wife
wants extra money, but would be astounded
were she to question him as to how, or where
he spends money that he gives no account of
whatever. Many a man worth his thousands
unconsciously perhaps (for charity's sake we
will grant it so), makes his wife undergo a hu-
miliation as grinding as that of the old Quak-
er's wife in the anecdote, when she trembling-
ly asked him for a little change and was re-
torted upon with the question "What hast
thee done with that dime I gave thee last
week?" And many a woman who lives in an
elegant house (her husband's) and rides in a 
handsome carriage (her husband's) cannot
command sufficient money to purchase any
little trifle without making an explanation of
all the why's and wherefore's, and running the
risk of being snubbed into the bargain, or per-
haps flatly refused. All these things are very
mortifying to a sensitive nature, and if the evil
could be remedied, it would save much unhap-
piness, as well as deceit between husband and 
wife, where perfect concord should exist if not
so much for their own comfort, for the sake of
the children who comprehend much more
readily than parents are apt to imagine the lit-
tle causes of unpleasantness and annoyances
between father and mother.

	There are very few married women compar-
atively speaking, who have any time to earn
money however willing they may be, and
however great may be their tact or ingenuity.
Household cares require strict supervision and
young women raising families need all their
physical and mental vigor for maternity and
motherhood, and the husband if thoughtful
and considerate will render material help to 
his posterity by careful attentions and timely
consideration for the wife and mother. Every
woman under these peculiar circumstances
feels much more acutely any neglect, or in-
difference, any unkindness or denial, and the
husband cannot be too careful of the mother
of his offspring. Yet such unfortunately is
not the case; women must learn to look to the
Lord, to lean on the Lord, so we teach and so
we believe, but this does not leave the hns-
band without excuse. If men who are not
willing women should enter a profession, or go
into business, would set the example of liber-
ality towards women in sharing the means
they earn it might have a good effect. Wo-
men who cut, make, mend and do all the work
of a household are scarcely entitled in com-

[Column 3]
mon parlance to more than their board and
clothes, and dare not presume to any extra
privileges, and if their husband takes them
now and then to a theatre, or concert makes
them feel they have had a favor conferred up-
on them, for which they should be most grate-
ful. Many women lie awake at night in fear
and trembling when they are compelled to
ask the husband for money, for even ordinary
comforts; Mrs. Sara J. Andrews Spencer said
at the Woman's Congress in Cleveland last
year, that once in Washington she was listen-
ing to a lecture upon this subject and the 
speaker made the daring assertion that of all
the women there, "not one of them had
twenty-five cents to spend as she pleased with-
out asking somebody for it; "Upon this," said
Mrs. S. "all the old maids and widows clapped
their hands upon their pockets, but the mar-
ried women who were "supported," hung
their heads in silence. They knew it was too
true."
							THYRA.
	S. L. City, May 11, 1878.


', '[WomansExponent | Vol8 | 1879 | October | Issue: 9 | Title: DO WOMEN EARN MONEY? | Author: Unknown]

DO WOMEN EARN MONEY?

	From the way women are referred to, gener-
ally speaking, one would think they were not
entitled to anything, except what they ob-
tained by favor.  Recently a gentleman ob-
served, "Oh, yes, I know that women are giv-
ing a great deal of means for this and that in
their organizations, but where does it all come
from?  Out of the husband's pocket."  Just as
much as to say woman, the wife, the mother,
has no right to anything.  It all belongs to
him, her lord, her master.  It is time the ta-
bles were turned and the other side of the pic-
ture shown.  "One story is good until another
is told" is a homely proverb, but nevertheless
it is a true one.  It is time that women them-
selves estimate the value of their own work,
at any rate appreciate the worth of their own
lives.  How many wives toil from early morn
until late at night, and even into the "wee
sma' hours," and it is scarce recognized?  Per-
haps, were the husband interrogated, he might
say, "Oh, she is a prudent, good woman, she
obeys me, she keeps her place," and so on, but
how few comparatively will say, "Yes, she has
worked as faithfully as I have to accumulate
this property, to make the home, to educate
the children.  And yet, in how many instan-
ces this is really the case.  We remember dis-
tinctly when quite a little girl, of hearing a
clergyman say," My wife never earned a cent
in all her life," and we know, too, that this
good, faithful wife was a complete slave; that
she did more actual work than half a dozen
college-bred gentlemen like himself, and had
more brains.  Do any of you readers know
what is expected of the wife of a clergyman in
a country village or small town?  Do you
know what it is to be the wife of a Bishop?
There is a strong similarity.  This excellent
woman did her own household work, kept his
clerical broadcloth in repair, waited upon him
as if she was his servant instead of his wife,
and was complained of when she was not
dressed to receive company, because, forsooth,
he had his sermons to write, and it took him
four days in a week; and when he was in his
study and his wife had her housework, mend-
ing, making, knitting, baby-tending and all to
do he would call to her every few minutes to
hear this, or that he had written, or suggest
ideas, or look up Latin and Greek he had for-
gotten.  But upon no account could he be dis-
turbed when he was in his study, for their
bread and butter depended upon it.  And so
he preached the very sermons her fertile brain
helped him to write, and dressed with scrupu-
lous nicety, and walked in his garden when
his head ached: but she bent over the baby's
crib, or the hot cooking stove, tired, weary,
and often sick at heart, while the Reverend
arried off the honors, and the young ladies
and the old maids admired his genius and won-
dered at his plain, domestic wife, and thought
she did not half appreciate him.
	And so it is--this man whose wife was a treas-
ure  in every way, finely educated, the daugh-
ter of a clergyman herself, was shut up in the
house from year to year, planning, scheming,
toiling, heart and mind in a constant strain,
to know how to economize, and live genteelly,
and feed, clothe and educate the children, on
the very small salary her husband had the
honor of earning; but "she," poor thing,
"never earned a cent." 
	There are many such records as this all
around; there is no need to go back for them.
We do not allude to the class of women who
are a dead weight in the community, who live
only for pleasure, and are always complaining
of "ennui," but to wives and mothers who
toil from morn till eve, rearing families, and
are so much occupied with work that they
have scarcely time to call upon a friend, or
answer a letter.  Do they earn any money?
Has their time and labor any real money value?
We insist that it has, moreover, that the hus-
band who says whatever she uses is his, does
her an injustice, to say the least.  Does he ask
his wife when he wants to make a trade, or a
purchase, or treat a friend, or bestow charity,
or give his note, or put his name to a note for
a friend; and yet the last two concern the wel-
fare of his family, it may be very materially.
No, of course he don't tell his wife; it is not
her business, she wouldn't understand it if he
did, and it would be a source of annoyance.
The money is his, the property he has earned,
so he thinks and sometimes says, and he gives
her what she needs, so far as he can afford.
	Out of these very simple things great evils
have grown, women who had not the moral
courage to ask for what they wanted, and we
must confess it takes a great deal, but who
have sufficient sense to realize they have some
rights, that they cannot and dare not contend
for, have often been led to deceive, to do things
"on the sly," as it is termed, and justify them-
selves in doing so, feeling that they have a
perfect right to do it; but this is not all - what
effect does a course of this kind, a lifetime of
deceit, have upon the children?  We speak
advisedly when we say there have been in-
stances of this sort, and they are not rare.
Every person who knows anything of the laws
of human nature, must be aware that such a
course would be disastrous to the best interests
of a family, and that there could be no real,
true harmony in the domestic circle where
there was deception, fear and want of confi-
dence.  Where does the blame fall?  Probably
in nine cases out of ten it would be attributed
to the wife.  This  is  a subject  that  much
might be said upon.
	The wife who performs her home-duties
faithfully has no time to earn money outside
her home while she is rearing her children,
but she earns money at home.  Certainly wo-
men earn money.  If a person comes into the
house to do the work, that person must be
paid in money, or its equivalent.  Yet men
will say without a blush,  "My wife never
earned a cent"  And if she happens to be-
stow anything for "charity's sweet sake," it is
not hers, it came out of her husband's pocket.
	We are thankful there are noble men, who
do not entertain these narrow views; and as
women become conversant with business mat-
ters, step out of the kitchen now and then, as-
sociate together in a way calculated to elevate
and benefit each other, study the best methods
of life, health and vigor of body and mind, do
their own thinking, seeking to become good
mothers and rear children who will be an
honor to their parents, having sound bodies
and vigorous intellects, these evils which exist
today may in a great measure be eradicated.
Another and better day will dawn upon wo-
man,  and we believe even in the "magical
matter of money."  The position of women
with relation to money is one which might be
discussed by  intelligent  women advanta-
geously.
	One of the lessons taught in an association
of women should be to use money wisely and
make better investments than the extravagant
adornments of their own persons.  There are
women, who, with the resources they have,
could help much in enterprises that are for the
general good, and thus materially benefit so-
ciety; but when it is expended in tawdry, or
expensive jewelry, elaborate costumes, and
luxuries which only undermine the health, it
is a curse instead of a blessing. The econo-
mizing and saving money from useless ex-
penses and superfluous waste, is one of the
relative duties of woman to the household.
The enormous risks and lavish outlays of busi-
ness men leave this care devolving upon wo-
men, if they have any forethought, or desire
to be a true helpmeet.  One thing we always
notice, that those who are most splendidly
dressed, seldom have anything to give; their
pockets are empty and their hearts are desti-
tute of generosity or benevolence.  Women
who dress simplest and work hardest, as a
rule are the ones who are most useful and most
generous in every public need or enterprise.
	It is necessary, in order that woman may
fulfill the sacred mission given her in this
Church, that she should be highly endowed
with opportunities for culture, and that she
should comprehend her own needs, her true
value; that she make no reckless expenditure
of time, or of bodily or mental power, nerve,
or brain force, in order to ensure an enlightened
motherhood, and that she may walk side by
side with man, her brother, as a co-recipient of
divine blessings and influence, without any
"cruel limit" to her sphere of usefulness, or
education, mental or spiritual, for "the man is
not without the woman nor the woman with-
out the man in the Lord."  But to conclude;
if men will transact the business and fill the
posts of honor and importance, earning the
money, women should be able to judiciously
save, and saving is equivalent to earning.
	How much would a man have to pay out, for
having his socks darned, and his linen kept in
order during his lifetime, leaving out all other
items?					E. B. Wells

ARE WIVES SUPPORTED?

	Among the amusing paragraphs lately cir-
culated in the variety column of newspapers is
one which contains a deeper lesson than they
can ordinarily boast of. "A husband adver-
tised in the Sheffield Daily Telegraph, England,
that he, Thomas A—, would no longer be
answerable for the debts incurred by his wife.
The wife retorted: 'This is to certify that I,
Elizabeth A—, am able to pay my own
debts, now that I have got shut of Tommy.'"
	This retort is a very pointed comment upon
the theory that has prevailed up to the present
day, namely, that the husband was the bread-
winner and stay of the household, and that the
subordinate members, including the wife and
children were supported by him; unprofitable
members of the community, consumers in-
stead of producers. To what degree is this
true, and to how great an extent are the wives
of all the great working classes, who form the
bone and sinew of English life, supported by
their husbands?
	The passing of the Married Women's Pro-
perty Act, which has so gloriously disting-
uished the Parliamentary session of this year,
has been the means of securing property to
the amount of many millions into the hands of
women, hitherto unjustly deprived of its con-
trol. The previous Act of 1870, now included
in the larger Act, had proclaimed the right of
a married woman to the wages which she had
earned by her own labor. No one doubts, the
justice of these two measures, or if a few speci-
mens of that nearly extinct species, the believ-
ers in the divine right of husbands, still exist,
they are dying away, and the next generation
will know them no more. But between the
women who possess inherited property, and the
women who, from the improvidence or incapa-
city of their husbands, are obliged to leave
their homes to earn wages for the support of
the family, there is the still larger class who
find occupation for their fullest energies in the
home itself, in cooking, scouring, making and
mending their husbands' and children's clothes,
and bearing, nursing, and sometimes teaching
the children. The question arises: Are these
women supported? Are they only consumers,
or a most important section of the producers
of the country's wealth? Do they do anything
to earn their own living, and, if so, how can it
be secured to them?
	A young man and woman agree to marry,
and they bring into the joint partnership, not
real or personal property, inherited or acquired,
but their heads and hands only. The man's
head and hands are occupied out of doors; he
is the visible wage-earner; he works ten or
twelve hours and brings home enough, if he
does not spend it at the public-house, to buy
necessaries for the family. Perhaps by extra
industry on his part, or extra good manage-
ment on hers, he saves and puts by money.
She, on her part, has kept a decent and com-

[Column 2]

fortable home for him, has laid out his earn-
ings for their mutual benefit, and has borne
and brought up their family of children, and,
as any one who has been in like circumstances
will acknowledge, has worked not only through
the day of twelve hours, but half the night
also. She has labored in her own vocation as
hard as he has; she has economised, contrived,
schemed, saved, giving the whole of her time
and brains to the common stock, and he has
done no more. And yet the ordinary theory is
that this woman is supported all her life, and
the law, even this law, which is incomparably
juster and more generous than any that has
hitherto been known in England, and which
we have no thought of undervaluing, gives her
no joint right to the income of the family; she
has only the right, which her husband recipro-
cally enjoys, of receiving a pauper's mainten-
ance from him.
	In many cases the unpaid partner of the do-
mestic firm does more than the household
work, though this seems a fair day's measure
for any individual's brain or hands. If they
keep a little shop, she looks after it; if a farm,
she makes the butter, fattens the fowls, or feeds
the calves. To quote again from the wisdom
of the newspaper corners: "A Western man
having lost his wife, a sympathizing friend re-
marked upon his woe-begone appearance.
'Well, I guess you would look thin, too," was
the melancholy rejoinder, 'if you had to get up
before daylight, make the fires, draw water,
split wood, and feed the cattle before breakfast.
I tell you what it is, if I don't get sombody
to fill poor, dear, sainted Maria's place, I
shall be resting by her side before many
weeks.'"
	It is notorious that in the agricultural
States of the Union, where labor is dear, the
farmers' wives take an extensive share in the 
farming operations, besides the house work.
We heard a few weeks ago of one of these
"supported" wives in Kentucky, who, after get-
ting breakfast for her husband and four chil-
dren, picked 135 pounds of cotton before
noon, came home and prepared the family din-
ner, returned to the field and picked 115
more pounds of cotton, and reached the house
again in time to cook supper. This instance
might be repeated a thousand times in Indi-
ana, Iowa, Kansas, etc. Hard as the farmer's
life is, that of the farmer's wife is confessedly
much harder. Besides her own work, she often
takes part in every agricultural operation car-
ried on at the farm. She is frequently killed
by the drudgery, and the statistics of the in-
sane asylums show that a majority of the fe-
male patients come from this overworked class,
and yet these women are "supported" and can-
not claim one shilling as their own out of the
family earnings.
	This seems to us incompatible with common
sense and common justice. During recent
years a good deal of attention has been directed
towards the injurious practice of women con-
tinuing their work in factories or workshops
after they are married. It is contended, and
with much truth, that the husband's wages are
calculated on a scale sufficient to support the
family, and that the physical and moral inter-
ests of the household will suffer if she gives her
time and strength to work which takes her
from home. There is much to be said from
this view, but there is this great difference to
the woman. She goes not only from one kind
of work to another, but from paid work to un-
paid work; from being a self-supporting laborer
she becomes "supported."
	If we could suppose, for the sake of argu-
ment, that all the married women in England
neglected or refused to fulfil their home du-
ties, as wives, mothers and mistresses of the
household, so that hired help must be engaged,
the wealthy man would be forced to supply the
place of his wife by a housekeeper and a gov-

[Column 3]

erness, the poor man to engage the insufficient
help of a charwoman to be cook, nurse, scrub-
ber, mender of clothes, etc., all in one. In
each case, wages must be paid, and the aggre-
gate sum of these wages would probably ex-
ceed not only all the earnings now gained by
married women away from their homes, but
even the earnings of the unmarried. The la-
bor of the household can only be performed
free of cost when it is performed by the wife.
Every one remembers Wilkie Collins' resume
of the question in the "Moonstone." The stew-
ard, whose cottage has been kept clean by a
working woman, marries her, and says:
	"I had another reason likewise of my own
discovering. Selina, being a single woman,
made me pay so much a week for her board
and services. Selina, being my wife, couldn't
charge for her board, and was bound to give
me her services for nothing. That was the
point of view I looked at it from. Economy—
with a dash of love. I put it to my mistress as
in duty bound, just as I had put it to myself.
'I have been turning Selina Goby over in my
mind!' I said, 'and I think, my lady, it will be
cheaper to marry her than to keep her!'"
	No one will deny that while Selina was
earning wages as housekeeper, she was a pro-
ductive member of the community; it is only
when she assumes the additional duties and re-
sponsibilities of a wife, that she becomes sup-
ported, and ceases to have a claim to remuner-
ation. Is there no method by which this in-
justice may be removed? Selina's case is of
frequent occurrence. The lady who has been
governess or school teacher, or post office clerk,
the working woman who has been a shop-hand
or domestic servant, the numerous class of
mill girls and female operatives, give up, when
they marry, a certain means of livelihood,
which, even if it has done no more than pro-
vide them with bare necessaries, was lawfully
their own, and they should have a legal claim
to some equivalent.
	As we have endeavored to point out, the
value of the work they do would be recognized
at once had it to be paid for. Some well-in-
tentioned but over-zealous reformers have sug-
gested that wives and mothers should receive
regular wages from the husbands for the work
they do. This, to our thinking, is manifestly
absurd and impossible. The work of caring
for her family, whether recognized as work or
not, is the duty which she has undertaken to
do when she married. She is morally bound
to perform it, as much as the husband is mor-
ally bound to provide her with the means of
doing it. The sacred duties of wife and mother
are such as can be measured by no debtor or
creditor account. She and her husband have
entered into a partnership, in which each con-
tributes his or her best for the good of the
firm; there can be no question of hire.
	We think, however, the law might recog-
nize the equality of the partnership more than
it does. The right of a widow to inherit after
her husband ought to be more carefully guard-
ed. If he die intestate, and there are uo chil-
dren, she should have the same right to be his
sole heir as he has to be hers. Her claim to
remain as a widow in her married home ought
to be established by law, not resting as it now
does upon family agreements, or the forbear-
ance of the eldest son. It should be impossi-
ble for a man to will the whole of his property
away from his wife. In some cases, as in the
transfer of real property, her signature should
be requisite to the document as well as his, as
is already the law in some countries; and
among the large wage-earning class, the em-
ployer should be bound to pay over to the
woman a proportion of the husband's earnings,
if it can be proved that he does not bring
home sufficient for the family support.
	Above all, the false theory that a married
woman is a hanger on, a supported member of


the commonwealth, as little children are, would
be abandoned, and the money she spends on
herself would be considered as lawfully
"earned" by herself as if it had been paid in-
to her hands by an employer who was not of
her own household.
	A few words from a thoughtful article on
"Treasurers," by Colonel Higginson, in the
Womans Journal are to the point here:
	"We are constantly told that the life-work
of a wife is as arduous and absorbing as that
of her husband. We are told that if she does
her whole duty to her family, she can have no
time to study metaphysics, or to put a slip of
paper into a ballot-box. I think it was the
conservative, Dr. Edward H. Clark, who de-
clared that the duties of the mother of a fam-
ily required as much toil of brain and body
as those of the captain of a ship. Grant it
all; grant that she work as hard as her hus-
band does. If so, the inference is irresistible
that she earns her share of the family income.
The fact that he receives the money and pays
the bills makes him the treasurer of the fami-
ly, that is all, aud he has no more right than
any other treasurer to take airs upon himself
and talk nonsense. When he pays ont money
to her, it is not as a gift, but as earnings.	*
*	*	'This was a present to me from my
wife,' said a rich man, showing an ornament.
'Bought with your money?' said a friend jo-
cosely. 'No,' he said, 'out of her own hard
earnings. She keeps house for this man of
your acquaintance.'"—Englishwoman's Review.


WOMAN’S	EXPONENT.
